_note: Original translation and commentary for the Vakyakanda, written from the Sanskrit of the mula karikas. Not derived from any published translation. 'contested' flags verses where the Sanskrit is genuinely ambiguous or our reading is uncertain.

Verse 2.1-2.section: What is a Sentence? The Eight Views
Verse 2.1-2.translation: (1) The verb-word; a collection; the universal residing in the collection; the single partless word; sequence; the gathering-together in cognition; (2) the first word; each word severally, as requiring the others — thus is opinion about the sentence divided manifold among the proponents of theory.
Verse 2.1-2.commentary: The kanda opens not with a thesis but with a catalogue: eight answers to 'what is a vakya?', compressed into two verses that the whole book will spend itself unpacking. In order: (i) akhyata-sabdah — the sentence is the verb, everything else being its satellites (a Mimamsa-adjacent view: the action-word is where sentencehood lives); (ii) sanghatah — the sheer collection of words; (iii) jatih sanghata-vartini — the universal residing in the collection: sentencehood as a type instantiated by word-strings; (iv) eko 'navayavah sabdah — the one partless word: the sentence-sphota, the view this kanda will defend; (v) kramah — the sequence itself, the ordering as the sentence; (vi) buddhy-anusamhrtih — the gathering-together in cognition: the sentence as a mental act of synthesis; (vii) padam adyam — the first word, which sets the frame the rest merely fills; (viii) prthak sarvam padam sakanksam — every word severally, each 'requiring' the others: the sentence as mutual expectancy distributed over its words. The list is engineered as a spectrum: it runs from views that locate the sentence in a privileged part (i, vii), through views that locate it in the parts collectively (ii, v, viii), through type-theoretic ascent (iii), to views that locate it beyond the parts altogether (iv, vi). Positions (iv) and (vi) are the partless-whole family, divided over whether the unity is in the word or in the mind — and their eventual convergence (the sentence-sphota grasped in pratibha) is where the kanda is headed. The doxographic opening is itself doctrine: that eight serious answers exist is the first datum about how hard the question is.
Verse 2.1-2.contested: the enumeration and even the count of views varies among readers — some collapse (v) and (vi), some split (viii) in two; the karika gives no numerals, and the traditional count of eight follows the commentators

Verse 2.3.translation: What is technically defined in the science for the sake of settling accent-suppression and the like takes 'having parts that require one another' — hence not everything bears the same definition.
Verse 2.3.commentary: A methodological caution before the battle: grammar's own working definition of 'sentence' settles nothing metaphysical. Panini's system needs a sentence-unit to state rules — nighata, the suppression of accent (a finite verb loses its accent when preceded in the same sentence, per Astadhyayi 8.1.28), is the standard case — and for such purposes the sastra defines (paribhasitam) the vakya as sakanksa-avayavam, a thing whose parts mutually require. That is view (viii) of the catalogue, adopted *for bookkeeping*. Bhartrhari's point: a definition framed vyavastha-artham, for regulating specific operations, is fitted to those operations — na sarvam tulya-laksanam, not everything is captured by one mark. The rule-writer's sentence and the philosopher's sentence need not coincide, and citing Panini's practice to settle the ontology would confuse an instrument with a discovery. The verse generalizes 1.28's move (deflating a metaphysical dispute by distinguishing the purposes of definitions) and licenses what follows: the kanda can canvass sentencehood freely because the grammar's operational definition holds only its own ground.

Verse 2.4.translation: The sentence is held to be: having mutually-requiring parts, while — under division — its words have no requirement toward what lies outside; principally about the action, with subordinate elements; single in meaning.
Verse 2.4.commentary: The working consensus-definition, assembled from the tradition's marks. Four conditions: (i) sakanksa-avayavam — internally, the parts require one another (the expectancy of view viii); (ii) para-anakanksa-sabdakam bhede — externally, when the sentence is marked off, its words reach for nothing beyond it: the sentence is where expectancy *closes*, a complete circuit of requirement (this pair of marks — inner akanksa, outer nirakanksata — is the enduring definition Indian theory bequeathed: a sentence is a self-satisfying unit of expectancy); (iii) karma-pradhanam gunavat — action-principal, with subordinates: the verb names the artha around which the rest organizes as gunas (view i, demoted from definition to structure); (iv) ekartham — *single in meaning*. The last mark is the loaded one: it can be read innocently (one topic, one purpose) or with full metaphysical weight — the sentence has *one* meaning, an undivided artha of which the word-meanings are analytical shadows. The kanda will argue for the weighted reading (the vakyartha as pratibha, indivisible). The definition is thus a Trojan structure: three marks any theorist signs, and a fourth that, pressed, yields the sentence-holism the partitive theorists never meant to concede.
Verse 2.4.contested: ekartham is the crux by design — 'having a single purpose', 'about a single thing', and 'possessing one (undivided) meaning' are all available, and which one takes determines whether 2.4 is a neutral consensus or already the siddhanta in disguise

Verse 2.5.translation: And a word of address is a qualifier of the action: 'Devadatta! go' — the accent-suppression here is thus in order.
Verse 2.5.commentary: The definition immediately earning its keep on a scholastic test case. The vocative — sambodhana-padam, 'Devadatta!' — looks grammatically detached: an address is not an argument of the verb, so is it *in* the sentence? The answer: it is kriyaya visesakam, a qualifier of the action — the address fixes *whose* going is enjoined, orienting the injunction as surely as any karaka. Consequence: in vrajani devadatta ('let me go, Devadatta' / 'Devadatta, go'), the verb's nighata, its accent-suppression, is tatha sati in order — the vocative counts as sentence-mate (the rule of 8.1 applies within one vakya), because qualification of the action is exactly the sentence-membership the definition requires. The verse is deliberately minor: watch the definition of 2.4 adjudicate a real Paninian detail, and note the direction of reasoning — semantic integration (does it qualify the kriya?) decides formal treatment (does nighata apply?), not the reverse. Meaning-structure is already sovereign over form, in miniature, before the great argument for it begins.
Verse 2.5.contested: vrajani is the first-person imperative ('let me go'), which sits oddly with a second-person address; the example may be quoted from a ritual context (announcing one's going to Devadatta), and readers divide over whether the oddity is the point (address qualifies even a first-person act) or an artifact of the traditional example

Verse 2.6.translation: Just as even several gerund-clauses are qualifiers of the finite verb, so, they say, a finite verb may there be the qualifier of another finite verb.
Verse 2.6.commentary: Stretching sentence-unity across clause-boundaries. That ktvanta clauses — 'having bathed, having eaten, he goes' — qualify the finite verb, anekam api, however many they are, is uncontroversial: one sentence, one principal action, subordinate members in train (2.4's third mark). The verse then reports the bolder extension (ahuh, 'they say'): a tinanta may qualify a tinanta — one *finite* verb subordinated to another, as in correlative constructions ('he cooks, he eats' as 'when he cooks, he eats'). This dissolves the surface criterion for counting sentences: two finite verbs need not mean two vakyas, if one action stands as visesaka to the other; principality, not finiteness, individuates. The consequence runs deep — sentence-boundaries follow the architecture of *meaning-dependence*, not verb-morphology, so the vakya is a semantic unit through and through, its edges drawn where expectancy closes (2.4) rather than where inflection changes. The partitive theorist who wanted the sentence assembled from formally-marked pieces has already, by 2.6, lost the ability to say where one assembly ends.

Verse 2.7.translation: Just as a single cognition comprehending all its objects is divided up in imitation of the differences of the seen, so is the construing of sentence-meaning.
Verse 2.7.commentary: The kanda's positive doctrine sounded for the first time, on the analogy of the visual field. Take one perceptual act — a single glance at a room. The cognition is *one*: eka eva sarvartha-pratyayah, one awareness comprehending all its objects together. Analysis then divides it — 'I saw a jar, a mat, a lamp' — but the division is drsya-bheda-anukarena, in *imitation of* the differences among the things seen: the plurality belongs to the objects and is retrojected into the act, which was itself undivided. Tatha vakyartha-anugamah: so with the construing of sentence-meaning. Understanding a sentence is one cognitive seizure (the anusamhrti of 2.1's sixth view, the pratibha to come); the 'word-meanings' we report are divisions painted onto that unity in imitation of the divisions of its topic. The analogy is chosen with precision — no one thinks a glance is assembled out of jar-perception plus mat-perception; the unity is primitive, the itemization retrospective. Bhartrhari's claim is that sentence-understanding has exactly this structure, and 1.90's phenomenology (part-seizures as scaffolding toward one grasp) already supplied the temporal side of the picture.

Verse 2.8-9.translation: (8) Just as a picture, though single in its form, is described by exhibitions of its differences — 'blue' and the rest, each with a distinct mark — (9) just so, of a single sentence free of requirement in every direction, description is carried out by other words, which do require.
Verse 2.8-9.commentary: The picture-analogy, working the same holism from the object side. A citra, a painting, is ekasvarupa, one in form — the composition is a single visible whole. Yet we describe it bheda-nidarsanaih, by exhibitions of difference: 'blue here, a figure there', each descriptor bhinna-laksana, marking off its own feature. Nobody concludes the painting *is* an aggregate of blue-patch plus figure-patch; the descriptors are our instruments, their plurality an artifact of description. So (9) the sentence: in itself nirakanksa sarvatah — requirement-free in every direction, expectancy closed (2.4's outer mark, now revealing its metaphysical face: the *complete* thing needs nothing) — while its samakhyana, its description-and-analysis, is conducted sabdantaraih sakanksaih, by *other* words which do require one another: the grammarian's paraphrases, the padas of parsing. The inversion is the argument: mutual requirement, the mark by which view (viii) defined the sentence, turns out to characterize not the sentence but *our talk about it* — the analysans, not the analysandum. Words are needy; the sentence is not; and needy instruments cannot be the substance of the self-sufficient thing they describe.
Verse 2.8-9.contested: samakhyanam we render 'description'; it can carry the technical sense 'naming/designation', making the verses about how the sentence is *referred to* rather than analyzed — the holist point survives either way, but the picture-analogy fits description better

Verse 2.10.translation: As stem, suffix and the rest are divided out within the word, so is the abstraction of words within the sentence set forth.
Verse 2.10.commentary: The apoddhara doctrine — 1.24's buried premise, now stated as the kanda's methodological center. Inside a word, grammar divides prakrti from pratyaya, stem from suffix: pac-ati, root and ending. Every grammarian knows the division is *procedural* — speakers do not utter roots and then endings; the divisions are found by the method of comparing forms, and the 'pieces' exist in the analysis, not in the utterance (the Mahabhasya's own tradition concedes as much about roots and suffixes). Bhartrhari's move: tatha vakye padanam apoddharah — the extraction of *words* from the *sentence* has exactly the same standing. The pada is to the vakya what the suffix is to the pada: an analytical precipitate, upavarnyate, 'set forth in description', real in the grammar and projected onto the speech. The argument's force is ad hominem in the honorable sense — the opponent who accepts stem/suffix abstraction (and every Paniniya must) has already accepted the machinery that dissolves his word-realism one level up. What he treats as building blocks are the *products of carving*, and carving presupposes the uncarved.
Verse 2.10.contested: none of substance; the verse's whole weight lies in taking apoddhara seriously, and the tradition itself (Vrtti and later Paniniyas) reads it as here

Verse 2.11.translation: Within the parts of phonemes are seen shapes resembling other phonemes; and parts of words stand there resembling other words.
Verse 2.11.commentary: Empirical sabotage of the 'real parts' picture, at both levels. Take a phoneme and inspect its stretches: varnantara-sarupam, shapes resembling *other* phonemes appear in its parts — the long vowel contains what sounds like the short, transitions mimic neighboring articulations. Take a word: pada-bhagah stand there padantara-sarupah, word-parts resembling other words — 'kambala' contains 'kam' and 'bala', 'rajapurusa' contains sequences that elsewhere are words in their own right. If parthood were a fact about the speech itself, these embedded look-alikes would *be* parts — the theory would have to say 'kambala' really contains the word 'kam', and every utterance would teem with accidental constituents. The absurdity shows that being-a-part is not a phonetic property at all: nothing in the sound marks the 'real' divisions off from the spurious ones. Division is imposed by the analysis that finds it useful (2.10), and where analysis has no use, resemblance to a word does not make a word. The next verse names the classic examples and the criterion that actually governs.
Verse 2.11.contested: what varna-bhagas, 'parts of phonemes', are is delicate — phases of articulation, or sub-moras; the karika needs only that inspection of stretches yields pseudo-units at every grain, which any phonetician grants

Verse 2.12.translation: 'Vrsabha', 'udaka', 'yavaka' are joined with parts that mean nothing; it is agreement and difference that are the ground of the practice.
Verse 2.12.commentary: The examples, and then — in one line — the actual epistemology of grammatical analysis. Vrsabha ('bull') contains 'vrsa' (itself a word elsewhere) plus '-bha' which means nothing here; udaka ('water') offers 'uda-' and a dangling '-ka'; yavaka ('a red dye') looks like yava ('barley') plus '-ka' but the resemblance buys no meaning. So: words visibly *contain* anarthaka bhagas, meaningless parts — parthood without semantic office. What, then, licenses the divisions grammar *does* make? Anvaya-vyatirekau: agreement and difference — the method of concomitant variation. Where a form recurs *with* a recurring meaning (pac- across pacati, pacatah, paktum, always cooking-), analysis credits it; where presence and absence of form track presence and absence of meaning, a unit is posited; where (as in '-bha' of vrsabha) the form recurs with no meaning-constancy, no unit. This is vyavahara-nibandhanam, the ground of the entire practice: segmentation is an *inference* from distributional evidence, not a perception of joints. The verse is one of the great early statements of the distributional method — and its philosophical sting is that a method is exactly what it is: the parts are as good as the inferences that posit them, theoretical entities to the core.
Verse 2.12.contested: yavaka we gloss as the dye (traditional); some read 'gruel'. Trivial — the example needs only a -ka that adds no sense

Verse 2.13.translation: There is no division of the word — whence should there be one of the meaning? By means of divisions the unlearned man comes to grasp the difference of derivational procedure.
Verse 2.13.commentary: The doctrine stated at full strength, with the divisions relocated to their true home: pedagogy. First line, flat denial: sabdasya na vibhago 'sti — the word (the real one, the sphota) has no division; kuto 'rthasya — and if the signifier is undivided, on what would a division of the *meaning* ride? The pada-artha, the word-meaning as separable item, loses its host. Second line, the constructive relocation: vibhagaih — by means of divisions, the grammarian's stem-suffix-word apparatus — the avidvan, the not-yet-knowing student, pratipadyate, comes to grasp prakriya-bhedam, the difference of derivational procedure. Divisions are didactic instruments: ladders by which the unlearned climb toward a competence that itself operates on wholes. The word avidvan carries the theory of instruction of 1.85 and 2.10 — the false-parts scaffolding is *for* someone, namely the learner, and its justification is what it produces (the vidvan's undivided grasp), not what it depicts. Grammar on this view is a fiction that teaches truly: prakriya, the derivation, is the itikartavyata of language-learning, discharged and dropped like the breath in 1.115.
Verse 2.13.contested: prakriyabhedam can be construed 'the difference (constituted) by derivational procedure' (divisions teach the procedure) or 'the divisions of the procedure' — pedagogically equivalent; note also the polemical edge: the *avidvan* grasps by division, implying the vidvan does not need to

Verse 2.14.translation: As there is no meaning 'brahmana' present in 'brahmana-kambala', so 'Devadatta' and the rest in a sentence would likewise be meaningless.
Verse 2.14.commentary: An opponent's reductio, stated for answering (the reply comes across 2.15-16). The analogy: in brahmana-kambala — 'brahmana-blanket', a blanket *for* a brahmin, or a proper name of a blanket-kind — the element 'brahmana' does not deliver its ordinary meaning: no brahmin figures in the blanket. Compounds notoriously swallow their members' senses (the tradition's rudhi-compounds are the extreme: 'krsnasarpa' names a species, not any black snake). Now the thrust: if the holist is right that sentence-meaning is undivided and word-meanings are mere abstractions, then 'Devadatta' in *devadatta gam abhyaja* would contribute nothing of its own — tathaiva syur anarthakah, the words would be meaningless, all of them, just as 'brahmana' is idle in the blanket-name. But that is absurd: exchange 'Devadatta' for 'Yajnadatta' and the sentence commands a different man. Word-choice manifestly *matters*, item by item — how, unless words carry meanings item by item? The objection is the strongest the partitive side owns: compositional relevance of parts. The holist owes an account of why substituting words changes sentence-meaning if word-meanings are fictions — and 2.15-18 begin paying exactly that debt.
Verse 2.14.contested: brahmanakambale is read by some as 'in (the phrase) brahmana's blanket' (genitive construction) rather than a compound/name; the objection is sharpest on the compound reading, which we adopt with the tradition

Verse 2.15.translation: A general meaning, once obscured, does not stand fast in the particular; how could what has been taken up be abandoned — and, withdrawn, where should it abide?
Verse 2.15.commentary: The holist's counter-battery: the partitive account, run honestly, dissolves in its own hands. Suppose words do carry their own meanings, the general senses the lexicon assigns — 'gauh' means cow-in-general. In the sentence ('bring the white cow'), that samanya-artha is tirobhuta, obscured, superseded by the particular this-cow-here. Then the dilemma, put as two unanswerable questions. If the general meaning *stands* in the particular — na visese 'vatisthate, but it does not: the particular cow is not cowhood; the general, as general, is precisely what the sentence-meaning is not about. If instead it is *abandoned* en route: upattasya kutas tyagah — how is what has been *taken up* thrown away? (The word did its work; its meaning entered the hearer's process; semantic contributions are not retractable.) And if withdrawn — nivrttah kva avatisthatam — *where does it go*? The discarded general meaning has no station in the final cognition, yet it cannot have simply evaporated if it was ever a real ingredient. The trilemma's point: treating word-meanings as real deposits that the sentence then processes generates an untrackable ontology of appearing, superseded, homeless senses. The holist's picture — one undivided vakyartha, word-'meanings' as post hoc abstractions (2.7) — has no such debris: nothing general was ever *deposited*, so nothing needs discarding. The objection of 2.14 is thus met by showing the objector's own machinery jams on the ordinary facts of contextual specificity.

Verse 2.16.translation: If the sentence-meaning is word-less, then the word-meaning too would be such; and this being so, the connection of word with meaning is forfeited.
Verse 2.16.commentary: The opponent's rejoinder, driving at the holist's most exposed joint. Grant the holist that the vakyartha is asabdah — 'word-less', not delivered by the words severally, an undivided content that outruns its verbal vehicle (the pratibha-doctrine in prospect). Then, the opponent presses, by parity the padartha too goes word-less: the word-meaning was extracted from the sentence-meaning (2.10, 2.13), so if the source-content floats free of specific words, the extracts do too. Evam ca sati — and with that, sabdasya arthena sambandho hiyate: the *connection of word and meaning is forfeited* — the very nitya sambandha of 1.23, the foundation of the whole first kanda, the eternal pairing on which Vedic authority itself was made to rest. The objection is beautifully aimed: it plays Bhartrhari's two books against each other. Kanda I sanctified the word-meaning bond; Kanda II seems to dissolve meaning's allegiance to words altogether. The holist must show that undivided sentence-meaning *preserves* rather than severs the word-meaning connection — that words are genuine (if non-exclusive) determinants of the vakyartha even though not its parts. Verses 2.17-18 begin the answer: words as manifestors, not containers, of the one meaning — the sphota-logic of Kanda I redeployed to save exactly the bond the objector says is lost.
Verse 2.16.contested: asabdah we render 'word-less' (not constituted by words); it has been read as 'non-verbal (in nature)', making the objection target the pratibha-doctrine's alleged mysticism directly — the dialectic is the same: parity plus forfeiture of sambandha

Verse 2.17.translation: For some theorists, particular-words have the look of generals; they become manifest among comprehenders through connection with other words.
Verse 2.17.commentary: First installment of the holist's answer to 2.14-16, reported from within the school (kesamcit, 'for some'). Reverse the lexicographer's picture. Words in isolation are not generals awaiting particularization; rather visesa-sabdah — words are *particular-words*, each fitted to the determinate role it plays in determinate sentences — which, cited alone, merely *look* general: samanya-pratirupakah, wearing the general's appearance, as a tool out of use looks like a mere shape. Their real, particular signification vyajyate — is *manifested* (the Kanda I verb, deliberately) — pratipattrsu, in comprehenders, sabdantara-abhisambandhat, through connection with the other words. On this picture 2.15's trilemma never starts: no general meaning is deposited and then homeless, because the 'general meaning' was an artifact of citation, the dictionary's freeze-frame of a word that lives only in sentential motion. And 2.16's forfeiture is answered in the same stroke: the word-meaning bond is intact — each word is bonded to what it (with its fellows) manifests — it was only the *isolationist* version of the bond, word-to-general-sense as a private possession, that has been surrendered. Substitution-sensitivity (2.14) follows naturally: change 'Devadatta' to 'Yajnadatta' and a different particular manifestation ensues, because a different manifestor entered the ensemble. Words matter item by item as *co-manifestors* of one meaning, not as depositors of separable ones.
Verse 2.17.contested: pratipattrsu ('in/among comprehenders') locates the manifestation in hearers, which some soften to 'for the comprehenders'; the stronger locative reading — meaning happens *in* minds, not between words — anticipates the pratibha doctrine and we keep it

Verse 2.18.translation: But for them the entire sentence-meaning is brought to completion at each division; the accomplishment of the meaning, in comprehenders, has the manifest as its auxiliary manifestor.
Verse 2.18.commentary: The radical corollary, and the verse where the holist account becomes genuinely strange and genuinely powerful. For these theorists (tesam tu), the krtsnah vakyarthah — the *entire* sentence-meaning — samapyate pratibhedam: is brought to completion *at each division*, at every word. Not: each word contributes a share until the sum is reached at the end. Rather: the whole meaning is present-in-completion again and again, word by word — each pada an occasion on which the one undivided vakyartha stands accomplished in the comprehender, more and more determinately manifest (vyakta-upavyanjana: the already-manifest serving as auxiliary manifestor of what comes clearer). The picture is the seed-and-ripening of 1.84 turned inside out: there, sounds deposited traces until the last one triggered the whole; here, the whole is *at* every stage, each word re-manifesting it at higher resolution — as each glance at the painting (2.8) sees the *painting*, not a painting-fraction, though later glances see it better. This dissolves the residue of 2.14 completely: 'Devadatta' does not add a Devadatta-brick to a growing wall; it re-precipitates the entire command, now Devadatta-determinate. Siddhir arthasya pratipattrsu: the meaning's accomplishment is in comprehenders — semantics has moved decisively into the mind, where Kanda II will house it (pratibha) from here on. The cost, willingly paid: comprehension is not accumulation but progressive revelation — understanding a sentence is watching one thing come into focus, not watching many things pile up.
Verse 2.18.contested: vyaktopavyanjana is a difficult compound: we resolve it as 'having the (already-)manifest as auxiliary manifestor' — each stage of clarity assists the next; others resolve 'manifestation by clear auxiliary signs'. The verse's doctrine — whole-at-every-word, completion per division — is secure; the mechanism-compound is not

Verse 2.19.translation: Unmanifest and, as it were, possessed of sequence, this word is recited inaudibly; but where the cognition comes to rest, it is sequenceless, as though spread out whole.
Verse 2.19.commentary: Evidence from inner speech. Recite a verse upamsu, under the breath — no sound, no articulation reaching the air — and the recitation still *takes time*, still runs in order: avyaktah kramavan iva, unmanifest yet sequenced-as-it-were. But attend to the other terminus: yatra buddhir avatisthate, where the cognition settles — the verse as *known*, the prayer as *possessed* — and sequence is gone: akramah vitatya iva, sequenceless, spread out as a simultaneous whole, the way a memorized text stands available all-at-once to its knower. One content, two conditions: deployed in inner time, or held in no time. The verse quietly dismantles the assumption that sequence needs *sound*: even soundless speech has krama when deployed — so krama tracks deployment (vrtti), not acoustics, exactly as 1.75-77 assigned it to manifestation-conditions. And the resting cognition shows the same content krama-free — so sequence cannot be constitutive of the word known. The iva twice-over is Bhartrhari's signature scruple: both the inner sequence and the spread-out wholeness are *presentations* of something that is in itself neither.
Verse 2.19.contested: the transcription kramavatr(a)- is faulty; kramavan iva is the evident text and we translate so. Vitatya iva ('as though having spread') is adverbial to the resting cognition — some construe it of the word ('as though extended'), same doctrine

Verse 2.20-21.translation: (20) Just as, though the impulses differ in each case, no difference of act is grasped — in the repetition, the universal is manifested by the acts of whirling and the rest; (21) so, in phonemes, words and sentences, hearing has a like mode of auxiliary manifestation: though its object is absolutely distinct, it is apprehended as if of like form.
Verse 2.20-21.commentary: An analogy from the perception of action, deployed to explain how hearing seems to present parts it does not present. Watch a whirling firebrand or a spinning wheel (bhramana-adi): each revolution rides on its own impulse — aksepa-visesa, distinct casts of force — yet the spectator grasps no karma-bheda, no plurality of acts; what the avrtti, the repetition, manifests is the *universal* of the action, whirling-as-such, one content standing steady over many pushes. (21) applies the model across all three linguistic grains: sruti, hearing, has tulya-upavyanjana, the same auxiliary-manifestation structure (the upavyanjana of 2.18 again) — the many acoustic pulses stand to the heard word/sentence as the many impulses to the one perceived whirling. And the conclusion, stated with both edges: the object manifested is atyanta-bhede, absolutely distinct from the manifesting events — yet sarupa iva pratiyate, it is apprehended *as if* of like form with them: the word seems sound-shaped, seems to share the pulses' seriality, exactly as the whirl seems to inherit the pushes' rhythm. The analogy improves on the mirror (1.49): there, borrowed properties; here, in addition, *repetition manifesting a universal* — many events, one content, apparent form-sharing — which is the precise logical shape the sentence-sphota needs.
Verse 2.20-21.contested: 20a's yatha aksepa-visese 'pi is our construal (impulse-differences not registering as act-differences); the line is compressed and has been read as 'though the projection (of the brand) differs'. The jati manifested by repetition points ahead to the jati-sphota option of 1.93 — whether 2.21 endorses it or merely borrows its structure is open

Verse 2.22.translation: But among eternal things, whence anything prior or posterior in the ultimate sense? It is rather the power of the one that appears thus.
Verse 2.22.commentary: The metaphysical floor put under the whole sequence-discussion. If the linguistic realities — phoneme-, word-, sentence-sphotas, and behind them the sabda-tattva — are nitya, then paramarthatah, in strictness, purva and para have no purchase among them: earlier-and-later is a relation of the produced, and nothing eternal stands in it. Whence, then, the overwhelming appearance of order — 'g' before 'au', subject before predicate? Ekasya eva sa saktih: it is the *power of the one* that so appears. Sequence is not suffered by the eternal items; it is projected by the one word-principle as its mode of self-display (the kala-sakti of 1.3, the vivarta of 1.48, now answering for syntax itself). The verse turns the kanda's local question — is the sentence's krama real? — into the system's global answer: krama is always phenomenal, at every scale, from phoneme-order to world-process; it is how the undivided *presents*, never how it *is*. What follows (2.23-27) descends from this height to argue the case in the hardest empirical arena: the felt difference between long and short vowels, quick and slow speech — precisely where sequence seems most stubbornly real.

Verse 2.23.translation: As in the cognitions 'slow' and 'quick' — though without a difference in their time — things shine forth as if of different time: that is the property of short and long.
Verse 2.23.commentary: The stock objection to timeless words, met on its own ground. 'Slowly' and 'quickly' are how we experience utterances; hrasva and dirgha, short and long vowels, are contrastive *by duration* (1.76's hard datum). Bhartrhari's reply-in-analogy: consider the cognitions themselves. The *thought* 'this was slow' and the *thought* 'this was quick' do not themselves differ in duration — kala-bhedad rte, without any difference of their own time, they present their objects bhinna-kale iva, as if in different time. A cognition of a week is not week-long; awareness of speed is not speedy. Mental acts represent temporal properties without instantiating them. Sa dharmo hrasva-dirghayoh: that is exactly the property-structure of short and long — the word (like the cognition) *presents* duration-difference without *possessing* it; length lives in the presented profile (the prakrta-dhvani, 1.76), not in the presenting sphota. The argument is elegant because its premise is undeniable: everyone grants that cognitions represent times they do not take. Once representing-without-instantiating is admitted anywhere, the word's timeless presentation of temporal contrast is no longer paradoxical — it is one more instance of a structure consciousness exhibits constantly.
Verse 2.23.contested: we take the verse's subject to be cognitions of slow/quick (jnane as locative dual framing them); an alternative construes 'in knowledge, though there is no time-difference, the two appear at different times' more generally — the analogical force is identical

Verse 2.24.translation: Time, being eternal, does not here deserve division by further measures; when the measures that would demarcate are absent, of what sort could sequence be?
Verse 2.24.commentary: The dilemma turned against the realist about temporal sequence. Suppose sequence in speech is real, grounded in time itself. But kala, on the going account (and Bhartrhari's own, 1.3: time as the one sakti on which division is *superimposed*), is nitya and one — na bhedam iharhati, it does not admit of intrinsic division; the 'measures' (matras) that would carve it into befores and afters are not further times (on pain of regress: what orders the measures?). And vyavartinam matranam abhave — in the absence of demarcating measures — kidrsah kramah: what *kind* of thing could sequence even be? The argument has the form of Bhartrhari's best: the opponent needs krama to be an objective order; objective order needs a divided time; divided time needs dividers that are not themselves temporal divisions; nothing fills that office. So sequence cannot be *found* in time — it must be *brought* to it, and the bringer is cognition (2.25 next) operating with the superimposed divisions of 1.3. The verse completes the pincer of 2.22-23: metaphysically, eternals admit no order; psychologically, cognitions represent order without instantiating it; and now temporally, time itself has no native joints for order to consist in. Krama has nowhere left to live but in the presenting mind.
Verse 2.24.contested: the transcription matragnam is corrupt (likely matranam); we translate the evident sense. Para-matrabhih 'by further measures' has also been read 'by supreme/other measures' — the regress-argument stands on any reading

Verse 2.25.translation: The cognition that arises from those is one, though it ranges over the parts; for by its own power it is, as it were, divided — it is the recognizer of sequence.
Verse 2.25.commentary: The positive account: where sequence actually resides. From the many manifesting events ('those' — the sounds, the measures) arises a buddhi that is eka, one act — yet bhaga-vartini, ranging over the parts, touring what it presents as stages. And the crucial attribution: sa hi sva-saktya bhinna iva — by its *own* power it stands as-if-divided; the division is the cognition's self-articulation, not an imprint received from divided objects (2.24 showed there are none to receive from). Its title: krama-pratyavamarsini, the *recognizer of sequence* — deliberately re-sounding 1.124's pratyavamarsini: there, the word-form in awareness was what makes any recognition possible; here, its specific office of constituting order-recognition. Sequence, then, is a categorial contribution of the apprehending mind — brought to the manifold, not extracted from it — and the 'parts' of the sentence are the itinerary of one act's self-divided tour. The verse is the kanda's Copernican moment, and its placement matters: only *after* the object-side (2.22), the representation-side (2.23), and the time-side (2.24) were each shown incapable of housing krama is the mind's own sakti allowed to claim it.
Verse 2.25.contested: tabhyah ('from those', feminine) most naturally picks up the matras of 2.24; some refer it to the srutis. Either way the mothers of the cognition are the manifesting events, and the doctrine is unchanged

Verse 2.26.translation: Through its entanglement with the tracing-out of sequence, a seed is deposited in that cognition; and of that, as between unity and plurality, no settled account stands.
Verse 2.26.commentary: An admission of undecidability at the theory's exact center — rare in any scholastic author and structural here. The one cognition of 2.25, in performing its krama-ullekha, its tracing-out of sequence, is anusanga — entangled, involved — and by that entanglement a bija is deposited in it (the seed-vocabulary of 1.84 and 1.132: consequence lodged for later ripening — here, the residue that makes the *next* apprehension order-wise, the sedimented habit of sequencing). Then the confession: tattva-nanatvayos tasya niruktir na avatisthate — as to whether that (the cognition with its seed; by extension the whole word-mind complex) is *one* or *many*, no nirukti, no settled analysis, stands. Is the sequencing cognition really single (its division mere iva, 2.25) or really plural (its unity the construction)? The question reproduces, inside the mind, the exact antinomy 1.72-74 found in language — and Bhartrhari, consistently, declines it again: both descriptions are available, neither is final, and the undecidability is itself the doctrine (the one appears as many; asking which it 'really' is mistakes appearance-talk for inventory-talk, 1.74). Placing the avowal *here* — where a lesser system would fortify — is the mark of a philosopher tracking his method's limits from inside.
Verse 2.26.contested: the referent of tasya ('of that') is genuinely open: the seed, the cognition, or the word itself; we keep it at the cognition-complex. Some read niruktir navatisthate as 'the etymology/definition does not hold', a weaker point; the parallel with 1.74's darsana-relativity supports the stronger reading

Verse 2.27.translation: But at the moment of realization, this capacity-for-sequence is itself sequenceless — that by which the object, its differentiation withdrawn, is apprehended as differentiated.
Verse 2.27.commentary: The paradox owned, polished, and made the resolution. At bhavana-samaya — the moment of realization, when understanding actually happens (bhavana: the bringing-into-being of the meaning in the mind; the term will do heavy work in the pratibha doctrine) — the krama-samarthyam, the very capacity by which the mind sequences, is akramam, itself sequenceless: the power to run through stages is not itself a staged thing (as the power to move is not itself in motion). And by that sequenceless capacity, the artha — whose own differentiation is vyavrtta, withdrawn, absent in the object as it is (2.22-24) — bhedavan upalabhyate, is apprehended *as* differentiated. Every joint of the completed account is now explicit: undivided object, undivided apprehending power, and between them the *presentation* of division — krama existing nowhere but in the presenting, a pure how mistaken for a what. This is 1.85's productive error given its final form: the mind's sequencing is the upaya, sequenceless in itself, generating the ordered appearance through which alone the unordered can be humanly taken. With sequence thus fully accounted, the kanda can return (2.28ff.) to demolishing the phoneme-and-word ontology that assumed sequence was real all along.

Verse 2.28-29.translation: (28) If the words in the sentence are just those (independent reals), and the phonemes likewise in the word, then within phonemes there would be division of phoneme-parts, as with atoms; (29) and since the parts have no mutual adherence, neither phoneme nor word could come to be — and since those (parts) cannot be designated, let something else be designated!
Verse 2.28-29.commentary: The regress-and-dissolution argument: partitive realism refuted by being taken seriously. Suppose (28) the realist's ladder: sentences really contain words, words really contain phonemes. Consistency forbids stopping: phonemes have stretches (2.11), so varna-bhagah, phoneme-parts, must be real too — paramanuvat, as with atoms, whose divisibility-regress is the tradition's standing scandal (the Vaisesika atom under Buddhist attack: whatever has parts dissolves into them; whatever has none cannot aggregate). Then (29) the two-step execution. First: bhaganam anupaslesat — the parts have no *adherence*, no mutual sticking; sounds are episodic, each gone before the next arrives (1.44's original datum), so nothing binds part to part — na varnau na padam bhavet: by rights neither phoneme nor word could ever come to be; the realist's building-blocks cannot even build his bricks. Second: tesam avyapadesyatvat — the ultimate parts are *undesignatable*, beneath every unit language can mention (what is the name of the first third of 'g'?) — kim anyad apadisyatam: then let something *else* be designated!, a taunt with a doctrine inside it: since analysis bottoms out in the unnameable, the real bearer of names — of designation, of meaning — must be sought elsewhere than at the bottom: in the undivided wholes at the *top*, which is where 2.30 immediately goes. The argument recycles the tradition's finest anti-atomist weaponry against linguistic atomism — likely with full irony intended, since the phoneme-realists included exactly the schools that wielded it.
Verse 2.28-29.contested: 29d's exclamation kim anyad apadisyatam we read as the ironic challenge ('name something else, then!') pivoting to the siddhanta; it can be read as a genuine question ('what else should be designated?') closing the purvapaksa — the pivot to 2.30 favors the ironic reading

Verse 2.30.translation: But that inner word-essence, one, made manifest by the sounds — that is what the others call the word; and in the sentence its oneness is likewise.
Verse 2.30.commentary: The siddhanta re-entering at the point the regress cleared. Where designation bottomed out (2.29), the apare, the other theorists — the sphota-vadins, Bhartrhari's own line — point inward: yad antah sabda-tattvam, the word-essence *within*, ekam, one, nadaih prakasitam, lit up by the sounds (the manifestation doctrine of Kanda I in one clause: prakasita, illumination-vocabulary, 1.94's lamp) — *that* is the word. Not an aggregate surviving the regress, but the undivided thing the regress never touched, since it was never composed. And the extension that makes this Kanda II's verse rather than a repetition: tasya vakye tatha ekata — its oneness in the *sentence* is the same. The sentence is not many sphotas in a row (which would re-open every regress one level up); it is *one* sphota at sentence-grain (2.1's fourth view, now installed), manifested by the whole utterance-event as the word was by its sounds. The antah, 'inner', quietly relocates the discussion for the rest of the kanda: the real word was never in the air — the air carries manifestors; the manifested lives where 2.18 put meaning, in the comprehender. What remains is to say what the *meaning* of this inner sentence-one is — and 2.31 answers with the system's deepest identification.
Verse 2.30.contested: apare, 'the others', is Bhartrhari's usual doxographic distancing even for views he holds; whether 2.30 is reported or owned matters little — by 2.31-33 the voice is unmistakably the author's

Verse 2.31.translation: So too by the meaning-parts is their inner meaning illuminated; word and meaning are the two aspects, standing non-separate, of one and the same self.
Verse 2.31.commentary: The identification toward which both kandas have been converging. First the parallel completed: as sounds manifest the inner word (2.30), so artha-bhagaih — by the meaning-*parts*, the padarthas of analysis, the itemized senses — the antarah arthah, the inner meaning, is illuminated: word-meanings stand to the one vakyartha exactly as sounds to the sphota — manifestors, scaffolding, upaya (2.13, 2.18). Then the thesis that dissolves the remaining dualism: ekasya eva atmanah bhedau sabda-arthau aprthak-sthitau — word and meaning are two aspects, non-separately stationed, of *one self*. Not two relata bonded by a sambandha (the picture 2.16's objector fought to preserve), but one reality self-presented twice: as expressive form and as expressed content — the double luminosity of 1.50 (word shows itself and its meaning) now grounded in identity: it shows both because it *is* both. This is the sabdadvaita cashing its name: at the root, signifier and signified are the sabda-tattva's two faces, and the 'connection of word and meaning' (1.23's nitya sambandha) is eternal precisely because it is not a connection at all — nothing needs tying where nothing was ever apart. The inner sentence-word of 2.30 and its undivided meaning are one thing turned two ways; comprehension (pratibha, soon) is that thing turning.
Verse 2.31.contested: atmanah we render 'self' in the reflexive-metaphysical sense (the one reality; ultimately the vagatman of 1.112); a psychological reading ('of one cognition, word and meaning are the two non-separate aspects') is available and makes the verse epistemology rather than metaphysics — the kanda supports both registers deliberately

Verse 2.32.translation: Illuminator-and-illuminated, and the form of cause-and-effect: these belong, at all times, to that word-essence whose self is the inner measure.
Verse 2.32.commentary: The two great relational idioms of the system, folded back into the one. Throughout both kandas two vocabularies have alternated: prakasaka-prakasya (manifestor/manifested — sounds and sphota, word and meaning, 1.94, 2.30-31) and karya-karana (effect/cause — sounds *produced by* the word, 1.46-47; world proceeding from Brahman, 1.1). Which is the true structure? The verse's answer: both, always, and *internally* — prakasaka-prakasyatvam and karya-karana-rupata are standing features sarvada of the sabda-tattva itself, antarmatra-atmanah, whose self is inner measure (the antah of 2.30 made essential: the word-principle is intrinsically the inner term of every such pair). The point is architectural: illumination and causation are not rival theories of the word-sound or word-meaning link between which the school must choose — they are the two aspect-relations under which the one self-differentiating reality perpetually stands to its own manifestations (2.31's bhedau generalized to relational form). What looks like a relation between two things is the one thing's way of being two. This prepares 2.33's still starker claim: even existence and non-existence are its stationed powers.
Verse 2.32.contested: antarmatratmanah is a hard compound: we resolve 'whose self is the inner measure' (essentially inner); alternatives — 'whose self is mere inwardness', 'of the inner-moment self' — shade the metaphysics without redirecting it

Verse 2.33.translation: In that same one stand established the capacities for existence and for non-existence — sequenceless, appearing as sequence, the ground of all practice.
Verse 2.33.commentary: The high-water mark of the kanda's metaphysics, after which the argument deliberately descends to semantics (2.34ff.). Three predications of the one word-essence, each a resolution of a standing antinomy. Astitva-nastitva-samarthye samavasthite: the capacities for existence *and* non-existence stand established in it — presence and absence alike are its powers (how else could language speak of the non-existent, negate, counterfactualize? — the capacity to present absence is as positive a power as the capacity to present being; the hare's horn lives in speech). Akrame krama-nirbhase: sequenceless, *appearing* as sequence — the whole dossier 2.19-27 sealed into an epithet. Vyavahara-nibandhane: and precisely as such — powers of being and non-being, timeless ground of temporal show — it is the nibandhana of vyavahara, the fastening of all practice: the term that has tracked 'ground' since 1.13 here takes its widest object; everything transacted, said, done, negated, planned, hangs from this. The verse is Kanda II's answer to 1.1: there, Brahman-as-word from whom the world proceeds; here, analyzed to its working parts — the one whose paired capacities and sequence-show *are* the world's practicability. What such a ground cannot be made to do is behave as one item within the practice it grounds — which is exactly the deflation about to be visited on the padartha-realists.
Verse 2.33.contested: the locative absolute construction leaves the verse's syntactic completion open (the sentence continues into the discourse rather than closing); we render it as free-standing predication, as the tradition does

Verse 2.34.translation: Since the means of knowledge is the comprehension itself: in the positing of word-meanings as existent, if a word-meaning is added or subtracted, meaninglessness results.
Verse 2.34.commentary: Return to semantics, with the criterion that governs the remainder of the kanda: samprataya-pramanatvat — the pramana here is the *comprehension itself*; what a sentence means is answerable, finally, to what competent hearers actually understand, not to what a theory computes from parts. Then the test applied against padartha-realism. If word-meanings are existents that compose (padartha-astitva-kalpane), composition should be monotonic: add an item (abhyuccaya), the meaning should grow by it; drop one (tyaga), it should shrink by it. The facts refuse: anarthakyam prasajyate — add or subtract against the sentence's one meaning and you get not more or less meaning but *meaninglessness* (or a different whole altogether): 'bring the cow' plus a stray genitive is not an enriched command but noise; idioms lose everything by one substitution; the same word added here completes, there destroys. Sentence-meaning behaves like a gestalt, not a sum — tolerance zero at its boundary, holistically re-formed within it. The argument is the empirical mirror of 2.15's ontological trilemma, and its premise is the school's deepest methodological commitment: linguistics answers to comprehension (the pratipattr of 2.17-18), the theorist's ledger of parts having no authority against what understanding actually does.
Verse 2.34.contested: we construe abhyuccaye tyagat as the add/subtract test (either operation, against the fixed comprehension, yields anarthakya); the compressed ablatives admit other pairings — e.g. 'because of abandonment (of the general sense) in the aggregation' — which would echo 2.15 instead; the comprehension-criterion in pada a is secure on any parsing

Verse 2.35ab.translation: By the word 'rajan' the king-meaning is understood under a differentiated form —
Verse 2.35ab.commentary: A half-verse (the source transmits it split), opening the compound-analysis: in 'raja-purusa', 'king's man', the element rajan- delivers its sense bhinna-rupena, under a differentiated form — not the free-standing king of 'the king dines' but king-as-qualifier, bent toward the purusa it possesses. The lexical 'same word' contributes differently under construction — the isolationist's fixed deposit (2.15, 2.17) failing again, this time inside a single compound.

Verse 2.35cd.translation: — while in the derivational analysis, a word resembling a finite verb is employed for what stands otherwise.
Verse 2.35cd.commentary: The completion: grammar's vrtti-analysis paraphrases the compound by a clause — 'rajnah purusah', or with a verb-like element supplied ('the man *who belongs to* the king') — employing akhyata-sadrsam padam, a word resembling a finite verb, for what in the compound stands anyatra, otherwise (verblessly, fused). The paraphrase is faithful to the meaning and *false to the form*: the compound contains no verb, yet its analysis requires one. Moral, in the kanda's running key: analysis is translation into the analyst's idiom, not excavation of hidden constituents — the clause 'in' the compound is exactly as real as the phonemes 'in' the sphota (2.10). The pair 35ab/cd thus exhibits, at the smallest scale, both directions of the general doctrine: meaning shifting under construction (ab), and construction-analysis fabricating parts the meaning never had (cd).
Verse 2.35cd.contested: the source splits 2.35 across two pages and the halves' connection is loose; some editions read them as one karika on vrtti (compound-formation) generally — our joint reading follows the evident sense of the received halves

Verse 2.36.translation: As, when 'asvakarna' is said, a certain particular thing is understood without any horse at all — such is the cognition in all cases.
Verse 2.36.commentary: The rudhi-compound as the revealing limit-case, generalized with sudden boldness. Asvakarna, 'horse-ear', names a tree (the sal); when it is said, vinaiva asvena — without any horse whatsoever entering the comprehension — kascid eva visisto 'rthah, a certain quite particular thing (the tree), is understood: the 'members' contribute nothing; the whole word delivers its whole meaning at a stroke. So far, standard: every school parks such compounds in the 'conventional' bin and moves on. Then the last quarter detonates: sarvesu pratyayas tatha — the cognition is like that *in all cases*. What the opaque compound wears openly — meaning attaching to the undivided whole, 'parts' semantically idle — is on this view the true structure of *every* understanding: 'raja-purusa' too is grasped as one word-one meaning, its apparent member-contributions being the analyst's after-the-fact bookkeeping (2.10, 2.13, 2.35cd); the transparent compound differs from the opaque only in *inviting* an analysis, not in being composed. The generalizing move is the kanda's signature (as 1.119 generalized from music): find the case where partition visibly fails, then argue the 'normal' cases differ in salience, not in kind. Comprehension everywhere is asvakarna-comprehension — whole to whole, at once.
Verse 2.36.contested: none textually; doctrinally, whether sarvesu extends to all *compounds* or all *words and sentences whatsoever* divides readers — the kanda's argumentative arc (toward the undivided vakyartha) demands, and we adopt, the wider scope

Verse 2.37.translation: It is for the sake of understanding another meaning in sentences that the resemblance is constructed; for some, the status of conventional word is acknowledged within the science itself.
Verse 2.37.commentary: Two housekeeping clarifications that protect 2.36's generalization. First: when analysis paraphrases — when grammar 'finds' horse and ear in asvakarna, king and man in rajapurusa — the sadrsya, the resemblance between member-look-alikes and free words, is parikalpyate, *constructed*, arthantara-gateh, for the sake of getting at another meaning: paraphrase is a bridge built from known words toward the unfamiliar unit's sense, a heuristic of glossing (the teaching-function of 2.13 again), not a discovery that the unit was secretly a sentence. Second: kesamcit rudhi-sabdatvam sastre eva anugamyate — for some theorists, being-a-conventional-word is acknowledged *within the science itself*: Panini's own system treats certain formations as unitary and underived (avyutpanna), their 'derivations' declined even by the derivers. The point of citing it: the concession the opponent makes at the margin (some words are just wholes) is the thin edge of 2.36's wedge — once *any* word's meaning attaches whole-to-whole with idle parts, partition has lost its claim to be constitutive of meaning as such, and retaining it elsewhere is a choice of bookkeeping, not a finding of fact.
Verse 2.37.contested: arthantara-gateh we take as 'for the sake of reaching the other (=the unit's) meaning' — paraphrase as bridge; it can be read 'because (the compound) conveys a different meaning (than its members)' — the two shade into each other and both serve the verse's deflationary office

Verse 2.38.translation: Even things taken up that are afterwards to be discarded — these they declare to be means; and a fixed rule about means does not necessarily stand.
Verse 2.38.commentary: The pedagogy of fictions, stated as a general maxim and closing the movement. Upadaya api ye heyah — what is *taken up precisely to be let go*: the ladder-entities of the whole discussion (phoneme-divisions, word-meanings, derivational members, the constructed resemblances of 2.37) — tan upayan pracaksate: these, the tradition declares, are *means*. The formulation compresses 1.85 (error as the very means of grasping), 2.13 (divisions for the unlearned), and every iva of both kandas into a rule of method: a scaffold is not refuted by the finished building; it is *discharged*. And the rider does real work: upayanam ca niyamo na avasyam avatisthate — among means there is no necessary fixity: no single ladder is privileged; different learners, different analyses, different fictions may serve (the darsana-tolerance of 1.74 and 2.26 as pedagogy). The maxim shields the school from a tu-quoque it plainly invites — 'your sphota-talk, your levels, your similes: are they not also constructions?' Answer: yes, and offered as upaya, discardable at attainment; the difference between the grammarian and his opponent is not that one uses fictions and the other facts, but that one knows which of his instruments are ladders. The verse is the kanda's methodological self-consciousness at full candor — and the traditional resonance (upaya-heya structures run through Buddhist method too) is surely heard, and meant.
Verse 2.38.contested: none of weight; the verse reads as the maxim it is — its interest is entirely in scope: whether Bhartrhari means it reflexively of his own doctrine (we take it so, with 1.74 and 2.26 as warrant) or only of the opponent's apparatus

Verse 2.39.translation: Somehow or other, some person comes to an understanding of the meaning; the differences — whether fused or divided — are fastenings relative to the sentence.
Verse 2.39.commentary: Deflationary realism about the variety of comprehension, extending 2.38's tolerance from theories to *hearers*. Artham kathamcit purusah kascit sampratipadyate: some person, somehow, arrives at the meaning — the double indefinite (kascit, kathamcit) is the doctrine: routes to comprehension vary irreducibly by hearer, occasion, and preparation; there is no single canonical processing-path any more than a single canonical analysis (2.38). And the differences among construals — samsrsta va vibhakta va, the meaning taken fused (whole-wise) or divided (part-wise) — are vakya-nibandhanah: fastenings *relative to the sentence*, ways of tying the one vakyartha down, not structures in it. The learned hearer may pass through elaborate division, the native speaker through none (2.13's avidvan/vidvan gradient); both arrive, and what they arrive at is identical. The verse quietly performs an important disarmament: the holist does not deny that part-wise comprehension *happens* — people do parse, gloss, construe — he denies that its happening reveals the meaning's composition; parsing is one kathamcit among others. With hearers, theories, and analyses all pluralized around a single invariant comprehension, the ground is fully prepared for naming that invariant — pratibha — and 2.40 begins the approach.
Verse 2.39.contested: vakya-nibandhanah we render 'fastenings relative to the sentence' (bheda-construals anchored to, and answerable to, the one vakya); the compound also admits 'grounded in the sentence' — either way the bhedas are relational, the vakya prior

Verse 2.40.translation: When the connection 'that is this' is set moving by the mind, then sometimes even a single phoneme is the conveyor of the sentence-meaning.
Verse 2.40.commentary: The extreme datum that only the holist can accommodate, offered as the movement's QED. Comprehension consists in an abhisambandha of the form so 'yam — '*that* is *this*': the incoming utterance identified with a content already alive in the hearer (the recognition-structure of 1.124, pratyavamarsa, here at sentence scale). When the mind prakramyate, is set moving toward that identification — context primed, expectation shaped — tada ekah api varnah: then even a *single phoneme* conveys the entire vakyartha, kvacit, on occasion. The phenomenon is common coin: the shared joke's first syllable; the servant who moves at 'ga—'; the pupil for whom the sutra's opening sound unrolls the whole. For compositional theories this is catastrophe — one phoneme has no parts to compose with, no word-meanings to sum; the meaning conveyed (a full sentential content) is unconstructible from the vehicle. For the manifestation theory it is the expected limit-case: if sounds are manifestors of a whole already standing in the comprehender (2.18, 2.30), then *how much* sound is needed is an empirical matter of the hearer's preparedness — normally much, with priming little, in the limit one varna, the fan of 1.116 barely waved. The verse ends the movement by inverting 2.28's regress: the atomist could not build the word from his parts; the holist retrieves the sentence from less than one of them.
Verse 2.40.contested: kvacit guards the claim ('sometimes, somewhere') and we keep its modesty; the traditional illustrations vary, and some readers take the varna here as a pratyahara-style code-sound (technical abbreviation) rather than ordinary speech — the argumentative use is identical

Verse 2.41.translation: Only so much meaning as is expressed by the word in isolation — of just that much meaning, they say, is the word when standing in a sentence the expressor.
Verse 2.41.commentary: The abhihitanvaya position — the Bhatta Mimamsa semantics, the holist's most serious rival — stated with scrupulous fairness at the head of a long examination. The thesis: a word's semantic contribution is *invariant*; kevalena padena, in isolation, 'gauh' expresses cow-in-general — and vakyastham, stationed in a sentence, it expresses tavatah eva, exactly that much, no more or differently. Sentence-meaning then arises downstream: words severally denote their meanings (abhihita), and the denoted meanings connect (anvaya) by expectancy, fitness, proximity. The picture is compositionality at full strength: fixed lexical deposits, syntax as the connective operation on them — the very picture 2.15 and 2.17 attacked, now given its own voice for systematic testing. The verses that follow (2.42-56) will let it and its variants state their best cases, and the objections will not be cheap: Bhartrhari's doxographic conscience — argue the rival to its strongest before dissolving it — is nowhere better displayed than in this stretch.

Verse 2.42.translation: But the surplus that arises when connection has taken place — that, resting on the several words together, they call the sentence-meaning itself.
Verse 2.42.commentary: The rival's account of what the sentence adds. Grant each word its fixed deposit (2.41); when sambandha occurs — the deposits connecting — anyad adhikyam upajayate: a *surplus* arises, something none of the words separately expressed: the relatedness itself, cow-as-being-brought, the state of affairs over and above its ingredient terms. Tam vakyartham eva prahuh: that surplus, they say, *is* the sentence-meaning — aneka-pada-samsraya, resting on the many words jointly, since no one of them delivers it. The account has genuine elegance: it explains compositional productivity (new sentences understood from old words: the deposits are old, only the connection is new) and gives the sentence a meaning of its own without positing a sentence-entity. Its exposed joint, which the sequel probes: the surplus is *generated* — it arises (upajayate) from the connecting of meanings — so its unity is derivative, an assembly-product; and everything Kanda II has argued about assembly (2.15's homeless generals, 2.28's non-adhering parts, 2.34's non-monotonicity) now stands waiting for it.
Verse 2.42.contested: whether 2.42 states the Bhatta abhihitanvaya specifically or a generic connection-semantics is debated; the adhikya ('surplus') formulation matches the Mimamsa doctrine of the sentence-meaning as vakyartha-over-padarthas closely enough that we read it so

Verse 2.43.translation: But that (sentence-meaning), though resting on many words, is brought to completion at each division; by others it is construed as like a universal in the aggregate, or like a number.
Verse 2.43.commentary: Two refinements of the surplus-doctrine, each trying to repair the assembly-problem. First (echoing 2.18 verbatim — pratibhedam samapyate): even granting the vakyartha rests on many words, it completes *at each*: the relational surplus is wholly present wherever it is present at all — a concession the connection-theorist is driven to by the phenomenology (comprehension does not feel like watching a relation assemble) and which quietly surrenders half his picture. Second, the type-theoretic repairs (paraih, by others): the sentence-meaning is jativat samudaye — like a *universal* residing in the aggregate: as cowhood is wholly in each cow yet 'of' the herd only via its members, the vakyartha inheres whole in the word-collection; or samkhyavat — like a *number*: as ten is a property of the group that no member has and no member lacks a share in generating, the sentence-meaning is a collective property, real, unitary, and irreducibly of-the-many. These are the opponent's best structures for a one-over-many meaning — and Bhartrhari has chosen them for their instructive failure: universals and numbers are precisely the entities whose one-in-many character is *unexplained* in the rival ontologies (the Vaisesika's own scandals), so the repair grounds the sentence's unity in structures as problematic as itself. The holist's alternative — the unity is primitive, the plurality constructed (2.30-31) — begins to look like the *cheaper* metaphysics.
Verse 2.43.contested: the referents shift rapidly and paraih is our pivot: we read three positions (per-division completion; jati-model; samkhya-model), the latter two as others' refinements — some read the whole verse as one position with two similes

Verse 2.44-45.translation: (44) Others know the sentence-meaning as a general, conformable to all the particulars; through contact with other meanings it takes on the form of the particular. (45) The relation delimits that hovering condition of it — of the general awaiting its particulars — settling it upon the particular.
Verse 2.44-45.commentary: The anvitabhidhana-adjacent refinement (the direction Prabhakara Mimamsa would systematize): meaning enters *general* and is *specified in construction*. On this view (apare, others), what a word first gives the sentence is a samanya conformable to every particular it might become — 'bring' awaits a bringable, 'cow' awaits a role — sarva-bheda-anugunyam, fitted to all differentiations, committed to none. Arthantara-samsargat, through contact with the other meanings, it bhajate bheda-rupatam, takes on particular form: 'cow' under 'bring' becomes cow-as-patient-of-bringing. (45) adds the dynamics with a remarkable image: before settlement the general exists in pariplavamanata — a *hovering*, a floating unfixity (the word suggests a boat riding loose) — bheda-anakanksatah... which the sambandha avacchinatti, cuts off, delimits, visese nivesayan, installing it in the particular. Comprehension as progressive fixation of hovering indeterminates: each word casts a shifting field of possibility that the others successively pin. Bhartrhari reports this with evident respect — it is the rival picture *closest* to his own (specification-in-context, meanings alive only in construction, 2.17), differing at one joint only: for this theorist the hovering generals are real semantic individuals that get connected; for Bhartrhari the 'generals' and their 'fixation' are both analytical shadows of one undivided vakyartha manifesting (2.18). The next verse probes the connective itself.
Verse 2.44-45.contested: the attribution is anachronistic either way (Prabhakara postdates); we mark the view as the ancestor-type of anvitabhidhana without the school-name. Pariplavamanata is the verse's coinage and our 'hovering' its nearest English

Verse 2.46.translation: The relation is inferable from its effect; a form of it is not seen. Therefore they acknowledge it to be utterly beyond thinghood.
Verse 2.46.commentary: The connective interrogated, and the rival semantics pays its ontological bill. Every connection-theory (2.42-45) trades on sambandha — but what *is* it? Karya-anumeyah: knowable only by inference from its effect (the unified comprehension); rupam tasya na drsyate — no form of it is ever presented; nobody perceives the relating, only the related-having-been. The theorist must therefore concede (pratijanate, they acknowledge) that the relation is atyantam asattva-bhutam — utterly beyond sattva, beyond thinghood: not an entity among entities but at most a structure attributed backward from its supposed work. Bhartrhari lets the concession stand without immediate comment because its dialectical yield is complete: the rival account of sentence-unity now rests on an imperceptible, non-thing connective inferred from the very unity it was invoked to explain — a circle with an invisible center. Against it, the holist's primitive (one self-manifesting vakyartha, 2.31) posits no machinery at all between items, since there are no items to connect. The verse also plants a flag the third kanda will march to: the Sambandha-samuddesa's full theory of relation as paratantrya, essence-less dependence, begins in this asattva-concession.
Verse 2.46.contested: who concedes — the rival driven to it, or Bhartrhari stating his own doctrine of relation through their mouths — is nicely undecidable, and the verse works as both refutation and foreshadowing; we read it as the former with the latter intended

Verse 2.47.translation: The thing-to-be-accomplished is fixed relative to its means; the action has fixed means. That fixity, simply through proximity, shines forth as settled.
Verse 2.47.commentary: Deflation of the connective completed: what work was left for sambandha to do, niyama and sannidhi already do. The semantic roles come *pre-fixed*: sadhye sadhane niyatam — what is to be accomplished is fixed relative to its means (a bringing needs a brought, an agent, an instrument); kriya niyata-sadhana — the action-meaning arrives with its argument-places built in (the karaka-structure that Panini's grammar maps). Given meanings so structured, sannidhana-matrena — through *mere proximity*, the words' co-presence in one utterance — sa niyamah san prakasate: the fixity, already real, simply *shines forth as settled*. Note the verb: prakasate, manifestation-vocabulary — nothing is assembled; an antecedent structure is lit up when its expressors stand together. The connection-theorist's sambandha is thus idle: his 'relating operation' names nothing beyond meanings-with-slots meeting slot-fillers in proximity — and 'meeting in proximity' is an occasion for manifestation, not an act of synthesis. This is the holist absorbing the best insight of 2.44-45 (specification-in-construction) minus its ontology: expectancy (akanksa) is real as the *shape* of the one meaning, not as hooks between many. 2.48 immediately cashes this in grammatical coin.

Verse 2.48.translation: The noun operates there as requiring, in the capacity of subordinate; the occasioning elements, as things-to-be-accomplished, await the action-word.
Verse 2.48.commentary: The karaka-architecture of the sentence, in one couplet — 2.47's abstract niyama shown as the actual grammar of Sanskrit. Nama, the noun, pravartate gunabhavena sakanksam: operates as *subordinate* (guna, the dependent member, 2.4's third mark) and as *requiring* — a noun is intrinsically unfinished, a role-filler seeking its role. The nimittani — the occasioning elements, the nominal contributors read as sadhyatvena, under the aspect of what-is-to-be-accomplished — kriyapadam apeksate: await the action-word. The verb is the sentence's gravitational center: nouns orbit it, their cases the names of their orbits (karakas), their expectancy the pull. This is why the akhyata-view (2.1's first answer) was *almost* right: the verb is indeed where sentence-unity is organized — wrong only in taking the organizer for the unity itself (the verb too is an abstraction from the one vakyartha; it is the *structure's* center, not the meaning's substance). The verse also explains, retroactively, why 2.4 defined the vakya as karma-pradhana: principality of action is the syntactic face of the meaning's one-ness — a single sadhya with its sadhanas is how an undivided content articulates when projected into word-roles (2.7).
Verse 2.48.contested: nimittani ('the occasioning elements') is loose — we take it as the nominal karaka-bearers collectively; some construe it narrowly as the pre-verbal conditions (the karakas qua causal factors of the action); the architecture asserted is the same

Verse 2.49.translation: The particulars that stand settled in the word-meanings, really existent, are followed along in order — the sentence is not an expressor.
Verse 2.49.commentary: The opposite extreme now states its case: the sentence eliminated entirely (the padartha-realist's terminus, counterpart to 2.41's opening). On this view the visesas — the particular determinations that comprehension delivers — santa eva, *really existent*, stand already vyavasthita in the word-meanings themselves; comprehension kramad anugamyante, follows them along in sequence, one deposit after another; and the conclusion is drawn without blinking: na vakyam abhidhayakam — *the sentence expresses nothing*; there is no sentence-level semantics at all, only a parade of word-facts that the hearer strings. This is the mirror-image of the holism it opposes — where Bhartrhari dissolves the words into the sentence, this theorist dissolves the sentence into the words — and Bhartrhari grants it the courtesy of full statement because its existence proves something structurally: the middle positions (2.42-45), trying to have real word-deposits *and* a real sentence-surplus, are unstable, sliding under pressure toward one pole or this one. 2.50-52 will let this eliminativism run to its own dissolution: if only word-order strings the deposits, and order is no word (krama being time's property, not speech's), the view ends with no expressor anywhere — an instructive collapse the holist will collect.
Verse 2.49.contested: santa eva we read with visesah ('really existent particulars', the eliminativist's realism); it can be construed adverbially ('being so, they are followed'); the view's identity — late Mimamsa? a Nyaya strand? — is unrecorded, and the karika keeps it anonymous

Verse 2.50-51.translation: (50) And if (the sentence is) mere order of words, there is no further word to be the expressor; for sequence is a property of time — hence the sentence does not exist. (51) And the possible particulars, unrealized in the word-meanings, are manifested in co-presence; but this order is not in the phonemes.
Verse 2.50-51.commentary: The eliminativism of 2.49 driven to collapse, then the holist's harvest. (50): if what 'strings the deposits' is krama-matra, mere order, note what order is: kramo hi dharmah kalasya — sequence is a property of *time*, not of speech (2.24 established it is not even securely that); it is no sabda at all. So the eliminativist has: word-deposits, plus a non-verbal, non-entity ordering — nanyah sabdo 'sti vacakah, no further word exists to do the expressing of the sentence-content — tena vakyam na vidyate: the sentence, on his own premises, *does not exist*. The reductio is complete and Bhartrhari states it in the opponent's own voice, letting the position pronounce its own emptiness. (51) then gathers what survives: yes — sambhavino bhedah, the possible particulars, avibhavitah in the isolated word-meanings (the hovering generals of 2.44), vyajyante sannidhane, are *manifested in co-presence* (2.47's sannidhi, the verb again vyaj-): co-utterance occasions the determinate whole. But — na tu varnesu ayam kramah — this manifesting order is not *in* the phonemes: the sequence that matters is the mind's traversal (2.25), not an acoustic property; the utterance's serial body is occasion, never bearer. Between the two verses the last rival has dissolved and every salvageable insight has been re-housed in manifestation-theory: deposits→hovering possibles; connection→co-presence; order→cognitive traversal; sentence→the one manifested whole.
Verse 2.50-51.contested: 50's first pada is compressed (sabdanam krama-matre ca) and we supply the conditional; 51's closing na tu varnesv ayam kramah has also been read 'nor does this order (of manifestation) hold among the phonemes' — i.e. manifestation is not phoneme-by-phoneme — which is the same doctrine from the other side

Verse 2.52.translation: The designation 'word', and the term 'sentence', settle upon mere order of phonemes and of words; wordhood is not accepted of those two.
Verse 2.52.commentary: The nominalist coda, reported as the last word of the partitive tradition — its most austere and most self-undermining form. On this view 'pada' and 'vakya' are mere labels: pada-akhya settles on krama-matra of phonemes, vakya-samjna on krama-matra of words — names for stretches of ordering, convenience-terms like 'forest' for trees or 'army' for men, and sabdatvam nesyate tayoh: *wordhood is not granted* to either — word and sentence are not themselves linguistic realities at all, only the phonemes are. The position is the exact linguistic analogue of the Buddhist prajnapti-doctrine (composites as designation-only), and Bhartrhari's economy in refuting it is total: he has already done so. If only phonemes are real and phonemes are meaningless severally (2.12, 2.28-29), and order is no entity (2.50), then meaning has no bearer whatsoever — the view saves ontology by losing language. The deeper irony he leaves implicit: the nominalist's 'mere designation' (akhya, samjna) is itself a *linguistic* act — his theory denies wordhood by means of words, dissolving the very instrument of its own statement. The catalogue of rivals thus ends where 2.28-29 predicted: partition, pursued honestly, terminates in the unnameable — and the way back up is the holist's (2.53-62, where the argument turns constructive).
Verse 2.52.contested: whose nominalism this is remains unrecorded; the prajnapti-parallel is structural, not an attribution.
