Some read the verse not as a rival but as the holist's own concession (word/sentence as designations upon the krama of manifestors) — the sequel's constructive turn tells against that reading

Verse 2.53.translation: But even where wordhood is equal, comprehension is seen to arise from the word; it does not arise phoneme by phoneme — hence they know the meaning to belong to the word.
Verse 2.53.commentary: The constructive turn: from the rubble of the rivals, the minimal argument that meaning attaches to wholes. Concede the nominalist his level playing field — samane 'pi sabdatve, phonemes and words equally 'sound', nothing acoustic privileging either. The *facts of comprehension* (2.34's criterion, the school's constant court of appeal) then discriminate: samprataya drstah padat — understanding is observed to arise from the word; prativarnam tv asau nasti — it does not arise per-phoneme: 'g', 'au', 'h' severally deliver nothing, no fraction of cow arrives with each (1.84 already established the all-at-once arrival with the last sound). Atah padasya artham viduh: therefore the meaning is *the word's* — the semantic unit is fixed not by ontology or acoustics but by where comprehension actually happens. The argument's beauty is its neutrality: it uses no sphota-metaphysics, only the observable non-additivity of understanding — and it sets the pattern the next verses iterate upward: exactly as meaning belongs to the word over the phonemes' heads, it will belong to the sentence over the words' (2.60-61), by the same evidence, at the next grain. The ladder of 2.28 is being climbed in reverse, comprehension lighting each rung.
Verse 2.53.contested: none of weight; the verse is deliberately ecumenical — even a padartha-realist signs it, which is its strategic point

Verse 2.54.translation: As its constituent phonemes, without any expressible content of their own, become meaning-possessed when combined — so also is the sentence accepted.
Verse 2.54.commentary: The parity-ladder, ascending. The word's phonemes are severally anarthaka (2.12, 2.53) — vina vacyena kenacit, with no expressible of their own — yet samuditah, combined, they are arthavantah: the combination is where meaning lives. Vakyam apy evam isyate: accept the same of the sentence — its words, severally, stand to the vakyartha as phonemes to the padartha: contributing no independent deposits, meaning-possessed only as the ensemble. The argument is strictly ad hominem in structure and the more binding for it: every school grants the phoneme-to-word case (no one thinks 'g' means a third of cow); parity of evidence (comprehension arising only at the whole, 2.53) then forces the word-to-sentence case on pain of arbitrariness — what non-question-begging difference marks the levels? The partitive theorist who resists must find in words a semantic self-sufficiency he already denies phonemes, against identical distributional facts (words 'meaning something alone' being citation-artifacts, 2.17). The holism thus propagates up the grains by the opponent's own concessions — the strategy 2.10 inaugurated (apoddhara accepted for stems forces it for words) here reaching the sentence.
Verse 2.54.contested: isyate ('is accepted/desired') marks this as doctrine being *ratified*, not merely reported; some hear in it lingering doxography — but 2.55-56 continue in the same ratifying voice and we read the siddhanta as speaking

Verse 2.55.translation: Meaningless (severally), by way of being means; or meaning-possessed through the word-meaning: uttered in sequence, they declare them to express a sentence-meaning of distinct character.
Verse 2.55.commentary: The two available descriptions of the words, and the invariant they share. Within the ratified holism, what shall we *say* words are? Either anarthakani upayatvat — meaningless severally, their standing that of *means* (the upaya-doctrine of 2.38: taken up to be discharged, semantic scaffolding); or arthavanti padarthena — meaning-possessed, if one insists, but through the word-meaning as analytically assigned (the abstraction of 2.10 credited back to them as a facon de parler). The choice is bookkeeping (1.74, 2.39): on either description, kramena uccaritani — uttered in their sequence — they express a vakyartha *bhinna-laksanam*: of *distinct character*, differently-marked — a meaning whose nature is other than the word-meanings' own, not their sum, aggregate, or relational surplus (contra 2.42) but a content of a different logical kind. The phrase is the movement's yield in two words: sentence-meaning differs from word-meanings not in *size* but in *kind* — which is why no operation on padarthas (adding, connecting, specifying) was ever going to construct it, and why the kanda must next say positively what kind of thing it is. The answer — indivisible, instantaneous, pratibha — is now one movement away.
Verse 2.55.contested: bhinna-laksanam we press as 'of distinct (logical) character'; a flatter reading — 'having a different definition' — is available; the argumentative arc from 2.34 onward (non-constructibility in principle) supports the pressed reading

Verse 2.56.translation: Whether the aggregates are held eternal, or the universal is what is construed, they declare the single sentence's single-meaningedness to be without deviation.
Verse 2.56.commentary: The invariance-result, sealed across the surviving metaphysical options. Two construals of the sentence-unit remain live from the wreckage: nityatve samudayanam — the aggregates themselves eternal (each sentence-whole a nitya unit, the samudaya elevated from heap to real); or jater va parikalpane — the universal-construal (the jati-sphota line of 1.93, 2.43: the sentence as type). Bhartrhari's point: choose either — ekasya ekarthatam ahur vakyasya avyabhicarinim — the *one* sentence's *one*-meaningedness holds without deviation (avyabhicarin, the term for a mark that never strays from what it marks). The eka-artha of 2.4's definition, flagged there as the loaded fourth condition, is here discharged at full metaphysical weight and shown *robust*: unitary meaning is not hostage to the token/type dispute, exactly as Kanda I's manifestation-doctrine was not hostage to the substrate-dispute (1.107) or the nitya/karya dispute (1.28). The method is constant across the work: fight to the death over the structure (undividedness), stay serene over the ontology beneath it. With unity thus secured against all comers, the kanda can afford its most disarming gesture — 2.57-58 next, where the whole war is recast as two legitimate directions of description.
Verse 2.56.contested: none textually; the pairing samudaya-nityatva / jati-parikalpana is our resolution of the compressed alternatives, following the earlier appearances of both options (2.43, 1.93)

Verse 2.57-58.translation: (57) The differences are constructed as posterior to non-difference by the sentence-theorists; the word-seers, however, hold the non-differences to be posterior to difference. (58) And the word-as-base relation is described according to the difference of functioning: either the continuous utterance is the womb of the words, or the continuous utterance rests upon the words.
Verse 2.57-58.commentary: The whole controversy, mapped from above — Bhartrhari's most explicit statement that the war of the kanda is between two *directions of construction*, not two sets of facts. (57): the vakya-vadins (his own side) hold bhedah abheda-purvakah kalpitah — differences are constructed, and construction runs *from* non-difference: the undivided sentence is primitive, words are carved (2.10). The pada-darsins, word-seers, hold abhedan bheda-purvan — non-differences (the sentence-unities) posterior to difference: words primitive, sentences built. Each side's primitives are the other's constructs; the datum — speech understood — is common. (58) then gives the dispute its technical grammatical form, where it had lived all along: samhita, the continuous utterance (the sandhi-fused stream actually spoken), versus the padas (the word-forms isolated by analysis, as in the pada-patha of Vedic recitation against its samhita-patha) — is the stream the *yoni*, the womb, of the words (padanam samhita yonih: analysis extracts them from it), or does the stream *rest on* the words (samhita va padasraya: recitation fuses them into it)? Vrtti-bhedena varnyate: described either way *according to the difference of functioning* — the reciter building samhita from memorized padas, and the analyst deriving padas from heard samhita, are both real procedures, and each grounds one metaphysics. The echo of 1.74 is deliberate and structural (what is primary for some is for others the reverse); but where 1.74 left the antinomy standing, 2.59-62 will now argue that the two directions are *not* symmetric after all — eternality breaks the tie, and it breaks toward the stream.
Verse 2.57-58.contested: 58's alternatives compress the samhita/pada-patha background, which we supply as the tradition does; pada-prakrti-bhava is the technical name of the issue (the Rk-Pratisakhya's own phrase), and whether prakrti there means 'base' or 'source' is itself the ancient form of the same dispute

Verse 2.59.translation: And if the word-recension is something else, an indicator of the continuous text — where eternality holds, how could the word be a product, given that its character is (mere) indication?
Verse 2.59.commentary: The tie of 2.58 broken, on the tradition's own deepest commitment. Consider the pada-amnaya, the word-recension — the pada-patha, that venerable apparatus in which the Veda is transmitted word-by-word, sandhi dissolved. What is its standing? Anyah samhitaya nidarsakah: it is *other* than, and an *indicator* of, the samhita — a pointer toward the continuous text, a laksana (the verse's own word: its character is that of showing). Now the argument: nitye — where eternality holds, and for the Veda it holds by the whole tradition's confession (1.132, 1.144) — katham karyam padam: how could the word be a *product*? If the eternal object is the samhita (and it is the samhita that is *recited*, the pada-patha being a human teaching-instrument attributed to named authors — Sakalya's, the tradition says, and what has an author is karya, 1.132), then the padas are downstream: extracted, indicated, didactic — exactly the apoddhara the holist claimed (2.10). The word-seer's direction of construction would make the eternal rest on the authored; the sentence-seer's makes the authored point at the eternal. Only one direction is consistent with the tradition's own reverence — and it is the holist's. The argument is ad verecundiam only in dress; structurally it is 2.38's upaya-doctrine given a philological body: the pada-patha is the paradigm ladder, and everyone already climbs it as one.
Verse 2.59.contested: the syntax of laksana-darsanat is compressed: we take it as 'given that its (the pada's) character is seen to be indication'; it can be read 'because (only) the laksana (the indicator) is seen (to be karya)' — same force. That the verse presupposes the pada-patha's authored status is the traditional understanding and the argument needs it

Verse 2.60.translation: As the cognition of the word-meaning is not separately felt at each phoneme, so in the words the discerning of the sentence-meaning is not separately felt.
Verse 2.60.commentary: The phenomenological parity-argument, stated in its final polished form — the experiential twin of 2.54's structural ladder. At the word-level: prativarnam asamvedyah padartha-pratyayah — the cognition of the word's meaning is *not felt phoneme by phoneme*; introspect as one may, there is no experience of one-third-of-cow at 'g' (1.83's anupakhyeya cognitions: real perhaps as process, absent as experience). Padesu evam — in the words, likewise: asamvedyam vakyarthasya nirupanam — the discerning of the sentence-meaning is not separately felt word-by-word. This is an empirical claim about comprehension's phenomenology, and it is carefully calibrated: not that nothing happens at each word (2.18 said the whole re-completes there; 1.90 mapped the part-seizures), but that no *separable installment of the sentence-meaning* is experienced as such — what one feels at 'Devadatta' mid-sentence is the still-resolving whole, not a detachable Devadatta-meaning banked and held. The hearer's experience is of one content clarifying, never of contents accumulating — and since comprehension is the pramana here (2.34, 2.53), how it presents *is* the primary evidence of how it is. The rival must now explain away the phenomenology; the holist merely describes it.
Verse 2.60.contested: asamvedya ('not separately felt') is the load-bearing term: the objector may grant unfelt processing while denying that unfelt entails unreal — Bhartrhari's implicit reply is the pramana-point (2.34): what never shows in comprehension cannot be *comprehension's* structure, only a theory's

Verse 2.61.translation: The sentence-meaning enters into the words as they function together; just so does the word-meaning into the phonemes functioning together.
Verse 2.61.commentary: The positive image that replaces deposit-and-assembly, completing 2.60's negative. Vakyarthah samnivisate padesu saha-vrttisu: the sentence-meaning *enters into* — installs itself in, comes to reside in — the words, and the words so entered are saha-vrttisu, *functioning together*: the locative absolute carries the doctrine — only in joint functioning, never severally, are the words the meaning's seat. And downward the same: padarthah into the phonemes saha-vrttisu. The direction of predication is the whole point, and it inverts the rival's at every joint: meaning is not *built up from* the units; it *descends into* them — the whole, primitively real (2.30-31), takes lodging in its manifestors as they operate in concert, the way (the system's oldest images) fire lodges in the churned wood or the reflection in the water. A word 'has meaning', on this picture, in the derivative sense in which a windowpane 'has' the light that fills it while the shutter is open: really present, wholly borrowed, gone when the joint functioning stops (citation, 2.17). Samnivisate also quietly answers where the 'surplus' of 2.42 went: nothing arises over the words; something antecedent *arrives in* them.
Verse 2.61.contested: none of substance; the verse's force is entirely in its verb and its locative — both are as we render them

Verse 2.62.translation: As a subtle graspable, conjoined with another, is grasped along with it — so too a phoneme, bound up with another phoneme, is an expressor.
Verse 2.62.commentary: A concessive coda: even the phoneme recovers a kind of expressorhood — the right kind — under the completed doctrine. The model: a suksma grahya, a subtle perceptible — the tradition's examples are the faint scent, the thin thread, the soft tone — too slight to be grasped alone, is grasped anyena samsrstam saha, conjoined-with-and-alongside another: perception carries the subtle in on the back of the salient. So the varna: samsbaddho 'nyena varnena, bound up with its fellows, vacakas tatha — it *is* an expressor, in that conjoined mode: not the bearer of a meaning-fragment (2.53 stands), but a genuine co-carrier of the whole that only the ensemble delivers. The verse does quiet justice-work at the close of the movement: 2.40 showed one phoneme sufficing (primed manifestation); 2.53 showed no phoneme sufficing (no per-phoneme deposits); the apparent tension resolves in the conjunction-model — expressive power is real at every grain and *inalienably collective*, the phoneme expressing as a finger lifts: only as the hand's. With this, the descent-and-parity movement (2.53-62) closes: meaning belongs to wholes, installs itself downward through joint functioning, and every lesser unit shares expressorhood exactly to the degree it shares the functioning. The kanda now stands ready for its center: what the one meaning is, and how it flashes.
Verse 2.62.contested: the simile's base (what the 'subtle graspable' is) is unstated and the traditional examples vary; nothing turns on the choice — the samsarga-structure (grasped-along-with) is the entire cargo

Verse 2.63.translation: As some meaning is discerned from the uttering of the word, so from the proximity of the phonemes that same meaning is apprehended.
Verse 2.63.commentary: A bridging verse, securing the levels against a last confusion before the great exegetical excursus begins. Meaning is discerned 'from the word's utterance' — and the word's utterance just *is* the phonemes in sannidhya, co-presence (2.47, 2.51). The two descriptions name one event: no stage exists at which phoneme-proximity has done its work and a further word-act follows; apprehension from the word and apprehension from the concerted phonemes are the same apprehension under coarser and finer description. The verse blocks a reifying misstep the ladder-arguments (2.53-62) might invite — as if the word were a second station where meaning is *again* received. There is one reception, describable at any grain; the grains are the analyst's (2.10), the reception is the hearer's. With the levels thus flattened into one manifestation-event, Bhartrhari turns (2.64ff.) to the hardest test his holism faces: the Mimamsa science of ritual exegesis, whose entire apparatus appears to run on exactly the word-level meanings he has dissolved.

Verse 2.64-65.section: The Exegetical Test: Word-Meanings in Ritual Science
Verse 2.64-65.translation: (64) When something is already obtained by capacity, and the explicit text serves (only) restriction — if by that utterly particular (text) the general were annulled, (65) then, when 'let him sacrifice' has obtained substance in general by the mark of capacity, the substance would be excluded by the word 'rice-grains' — and there would then be no substitute.
Verse 2.64-65.commentary: The test-case, drawn from the deep machinery of Mimamsa: the doctrine of pratinidhi, ritual substitution. The injunction vriihibhir yajeta, 'let him sacrifice with rice-grains'. Analysis: yajeta alone, by samarthya, capacity (a sacrifice *needs* some substance), already obtains dravya-in-general; the explicit sruti 'vriihi' then serves niyama, restriction — it selects *which* substance, among the antecedently-possible, shall be used. Now the argument (64): if the particular utterance *annulled* the general (atyantam badhyate) — if 'rice' obliterated the obtained substance-in-general rather than restricting within it — then (65) when rice is unavailable, na syat pratinidhih: no substitute could be licensed. But the ritual science *does* license substitutes (nivara-grains when vriihi fails — the standing doctrine): therefore the general survives inside the particular; 'rice' specifies substance-in-general without destroying it. The exegetical practice thus *presupposes* a layered semantics — general meaning obtained by capacity, particular meaning by explicit text, the second restricting, not replacing, the first. The relevance to the kanda's war is exact and double-edged, and Bhartrhari will work both edges: the layering looks like word-meaning realism (the Mimamsaka's reading), yet the *general's survival under specification* is precisely the holist's hovering-samanya (2.44-45) — and 2.66 begins extracting that second edge.
Verse 2.64-65.contested: the transcription's samarthya-laksanam (65) we read as 'obtained by the mark of capacity'; the argument is the classical pratinidhi-nyaya of the Mimamsa (cf. the sastra's treatment of substitution), which the karikas compress to near-stenography — our expansion follows the traditional case

Verse 2.66.translation: Therefore the word 'vrihi', positing riceness as the surplus, being (itself) for the sake of what is (already) obtained, does not annul substancehood, since there is no conflict.
Verse 2.66.commentary: The resolution of the test-case, and its terms are the holist's. What does 'vrihi' in the injunction *do*? It prakalpayati adhikam vriihitvam — posits riceness as the *addition*, the specific difference — while dravyatvam na badhate: substancehood, already obtained by capacity (2.64), stands unannulled, avirudhatvat, since genus and specification do not conflict: rice *is* a substance; the particular arrives as a determination *of* the general, not a rival to it. The exegetical rule falls out: where the explicit text specifies within the obtained, substitution survives (the general remains to be re-specified when rice fails); where it *conflicts*, annulment (badha) operates. Note what this concedes and what it wins. Conceded: semantic stratification is real — comprehension does distinguish the obtained-general from the text-specific (the Mimamsaka's layering, 2.64). Won: the strata behave exactly as the hovering-account (2.44-45) said — the general persisting *in* its specification, delimited, not discarded (2.15's trilemma dissolved in ritual practice itself: nothing was thrown away; the hovering was pinned). The opponent's own science, run on his own cases, exhibits the holist's semantics. The next verses (2.67-69) generalize the mechanism: specification-without-annulment, and the principled *non-intention* (avivaksa) of what a word could but does not mean here.

Verse 2.67-68.translation: (67) And even when, by that (word), the co-occurrent substancehood is delimited, other particulars are not seen there, because they are not possible; (68) nor are all the particulars indicated by the action-word, as the general is — for a word is not the expressor of everything that exists.
Verse 2.67-68.commentary: Two boundary-theorems for the specification-mechanism of 2.66. First (67): when 'vrihi' delimits its sahacarin, its co-occurrent substancehood, the *other* particulars that substance-in-general might have taken (barley, millet) are simply *not seen* there — asambhavat, because after specification they are no longer possible; they do not linger as suppressed candidates needing annulment. Specification closes possibilities without expending badha on each: the pinned general (2.45) just *is* general-as-riced, the alternatives dead by delimitation, not by execution. Second (68): the converse boundary — the action-word (yajeta), which obtains the general by capacity, does *not* thereby indicate all the particulars under it: na hi sarvesam satam sabdo 'bhidhayakah — *a word is not the expressor of everything that exists* (a maxim with reach far beyond ritual: expression is selective by essence; to mean the genus is not to mean, even faintly, each of its members). Between them the theorems fix the semantics' shape: upward, specification does not drag the whole genus-tree into meaning; downward, the genus-word does not scatter into its instances. Meaning moves at the grain the utterance sets — which is the anti-compositionalist moral in exegetical dress: content is not inherited along ontological lines (genus-member, whole-part) but fixed by the utterance's own act.
Verse 2.67-68.contested: 68's samanyavat we read 'as the general (is indicated)' — the genus is indicated, its members are not; some construe 'like (words for) universals' as a comparison-class. The maxim of 68d is textually secure and is the verses' exportable yield

Verse 2.69.translation: As the qualities, white and the rest, though present, are unintended there — so is there non-intention of the particulars that co-occur with substancehood.
Verse 2.69.commentary: The doctrine of avivaksa — principled non-intention — completing the boundary-theorems with the concept the whole exegetical tradition runs on. The rice-grains actually used are *white* (or red, or old); these gunas are santah, really present — and avivaksitah, *unintended*: the injunction 'with vrihi' says nothing of them; a sacrificer with discolored rice has not deviated. Presence in the object is one thing; presence in the meaning another — vivaksa, speaker's intention (the term that settled 1.155), is what admits features into the said, and its refusals are as constitutive as its grants. So too (the verse generalizes) all the particulars riding with substancehood: co-occurrent, real, unmeant. For the kanda's argument this is the last piece of the exegetical semantics and the most holist of all: what a sentence means is fixed neither by its words' full lexical potential nor by its objects' full property-set, but by the one intentional act the utterance embodies — the vakyartha as a *selection*, whole and indivisible, from which the analyst's 'word-meanings' inherit exactly the determinations the act granted and no others (sato 'vivaksa, 2.136 of Kanda I already named the canon). The Mimamsa exegete, master of avivaksa, has been practicing sentence-holism all along.
Verse 2.69.contested: none of substance; the verse applies 1.136's canon and the tradition's standing doctrine — its work is consolidation

Verse 2.70.translation: Lest there be a substitute in the absence (of what the text names), for the obligatory rite — or for an undertaken optional one — 'omission' is (instead) prescribed.
Verse 2.70.commentary: A precision from the casuistry, marking where substitution's license ends — and thereby what its license was. Some textual elements tolerate no pratinidhi: where what the text names is absent and the element was of the annulling kind (2.66's contrast-class — specification that *conflicts* rather than restricts, or an element textually essential), the science prescribes not substitution but lopa, omission — for the nitya rite (obligatory, which must proceed: it proceeds *without* the element) or the kamya rite already undertaken (optional, but binding once begun). The theoretical cargo: substitution was never a general permission to improvise; it was licensed *exactly by* the survival of the general under specification (2.64-66) — where no general survives (the element being sruti-essential, not capacity-obtained), nothing remains to re-specify, and the only options are omission or abandonment. The casuistry thus confirms the semantics from its negative side: the exegetical operations track, case by case, *which layer of meaning the text's authority entered at* — capacity-obtained layers flex (substitute), text-fixed layers do not (omit). A hermeneutics this fine-grained about layers is the kanda's best witness that comprehension itself is layered manifestation, not flat deposit-summing.
Verse 2.70.contested: the verse's compressed alternatives (ma bhut pratinidhih / lopa upadisyate) we resolve per the standard Mimamsa doctrine of lopa vs. pratinidhi; the transcription's ity(a)padisyate we read as upadisyate

Verse 2.71.translation: For one by whom the sentence-meaning is construed as the qualified action itself — for him, when substance fails and a substitute stands in, that would be a different action.
Verse 2.71.commentary: The exegetical dossier turned, at last, into a direct argument between the kanda's live semantic theories. Consider the theorist for whom the vakyartha is visista eva kriya — the *qualified action as such*: not action-plus-qualifications assembled, but the single qualified whole (rice-sacrificing as one indivisible act-meaning). For him, the verse observes, substitution has a startling consequence: dravyabhave pratinidhau — rice failing, nivara standing in — tat syat kriyantaram: the performed rite is *a different action* (nivara-sacrificing, not rice-sacrificing-with-replacement). The whole being indivisible, vary anything and you have another whole. Yet the ritual science treats the substitute-performance as *the same rite* discharged (the sacrificer has fulfilled vriihibhir yajeta, not violated it) — the practice sides with layering (2.64-66): the rite's identity rides the capacity-obtained stratum (sacrifice-with-substance), which the substitute preserves. The verse is Bhartrhari at his most honest: it is *his own* holism, in its naive form (the undifferentiated qualified-whole), that the practice embarrasses — and the embarrassment is instructive, forcing the refinement the kanda has been building anyway: the one vakyartha is not a *blank* unity but a unity with internal svabhava-structure (obtained strata, specifications, avivaksa: 2.64-70) — undivided, yet not undifferentiated; one act of meaning whose articulation is real *as* articulation-of-one. Holism that cannot say 'same rite, different rice' is too crude; the layered manifestation-holism now on the table can.
Verse 2.71.contested: whether 2.71 is an objection Bhartrhari answers (as we read: it refines his holism) or a position he endorses against the substitution-practice (making lopa the 'correct' outcome) is a genuine crux; the dossier's drift (2.64-70 validating the practice) supports the refining reading

Verse 2.72.translation: And when a word whose meaning is known has had its object conveyed, one like 'pika', not known — 'what is that?' — is inquired after.
Verse 2.72.commentary: A small phenomenological datum with a long reach: the experience of the unknown word. In a sentence otherwise understood — vijnata-artham padam, the known words having conveyed their object — an unknown item ('pika', the tradition's stock example: a Vedic bird-name opaque to later speakers) arrests the hearer: kim iti anuyujyate, 'what is that?' is asked. The datum cuts several ways and Bhartrhari wants them all. Against flat holism: the inquiry is *word-shaped* — the hearer localizes his ignorance to 'pika', which he could not do if comprehension had no word-articulated structure (2.71's lesson again: the one meaning has real joints). Against deposit-atomism: the rest of the sentence *was* understood — comprehension did not wait on the missing deposit but completed around the gap, holding a determinate *place* open (a something-the-sentence-predicates, awaiting species): exactly the hovering-general structure (2.44), here generated by the sentence for a word, inverting the usual direction. The question 'what is pika?' is thus itself evidence of the layered vakyartha: the sentence has already given 'pika' its role-meaning (some bird-agent, say); what is sought is only the residue. Word-meaning as *role-first, filler-later* — the sentence assigning, the lexicon merely filling — is the datum's moral, and it is the holist's.
Verse 2.72.contested: none textually; the pika-example's traditional setting (nirukta discussions of unknown Vedic words) is assumed rather than stated by the karika

Verse 2.73-74.translation: (73) And that which, obtained by capacity, is attached for the sake of the individual — in such attachment it is the explicit text that annuls both indicatory mark and syntactic connection; (74) but a 'white' and the like, not (antecedently) obtained, understood through proximity — that, brought in by effort into the sentence, has a character quite distinct from the explicit text.
Verse 2.73-74.commentary: The hierarchy of evidences — the Mimamsa's celebrated ranking of sruti, linga, vakya (explicit text, indicatory mark, syntactic connection) — cited at its decisive joint and read semantically. (73): where meanings obtained by capacity are being attached to their individuals (anusanga, carrying-over of elements across clauses), conflicts are settled by rank: srutir eva badhika lingavakyayoh — the explicit text annuls mark and connection; what is *said* outranks what is *indicated* outranks what is merely *construed together*. (74) then marks the other grade: a qualifier like 'white', aprapta, not obtained by any capacity, gathered sannidhanena, by mere proximity — yatna-prapita, brought in by effort — is sruti-dharma-vilaksana, of a character quite distinct from explicit-text status: present in the comprehension, lowest in authority, first to yield. For the kanda, the ranking is one more stratigraphy of the single vakyartha (2.71's refinement compounding): comprehension is one, but its contents carry *grades of textual warrant* — said, marked, construed, proximate — and the exegete's conflict-rules are a map of those grades. A semantics of flat deposits has no room for warrant-grades (every word a deposit, all deposits equal); the manifestation-holism has exactly room — the one meaning arrives with its determinations differentially anchored, and badha among evidences is the grammar of that anchoring. The opponent's crown jewels keep testifying for the defense.
Verse 2.73-74.contested: 73 compresses the sruti-linga-vakya doctrine (the classical exposition is the Mimamsa's krama-sutra context) and our reading supplies that frame; 74's yatna-prapita ('brought in by effort') is read by some as pejorative ('dragged in'), by others as neutral ('secured by interpretive labor') — the grade-distinction is the point either way

Verse 2.75.translation: But if the sentence is accepted as undivided with undivided meaning, then, since all of that has the standing of explicit text, nothing will be in conflict with the text itself.
Verse 2.75.commentary: The dossier's first summit: the holist collects the entire hierarchy at a stroke. If — as the kanda has argued — the vakya is abhinnam eva, undivided, its artha abhinna, one, then tat sarvam sruti-bhutam: *everything* in the sentence-meaning has the standing of sruti, of the explicitly said — na srutya eva virotsyate, nothing in it can conflict with the text, being text through and through. The ranking of 2.73-74 (said > marked > construed > proximate) is thereby revealed as an artifact of *analysis*: grades of warrant attach to the analyst's extracted items (his lingas, his construals, his carried-over qualifiers) — to the padartha-layer, the scaffolding (2.10, 2.38); the meaning itself, taken whole as uttered, is all equally *said*. The exegete's conflicts are conflicts among his abstractions; the utterance never disagreed with itself. This is simultaneously a compliment and a demotion of the Mimamsa science: its rules are the correct *statics of the scaffold* — indispensable for the avidvan-analyst working part-wise (2.13) — and idle at the level of the undivided meaning, where there are no ranked parts to collide. The verse thus closes the test the excursus opened (can holism survive exegetical practice?) with the strongest possible verdict: the practice is holism's *own* casuistry, conducted one level down.
Verse 2.75.contested: none of weight; the verse's sweep — sarvam sruti-bhutam — is as bold as we render it, and the tradition reads it so

Verse 2.76.translation: And where there is an aggregate of sentences with mutually-requiring parts, for the establishing of a single meaning — there a (separate) sentence-meaning does not exist either.
Verse 2.76.commentary: The holism propagated one level *further up*, with perfect consistency and startling consequence. Sentences themselves aggregate: the mantra-sequence, the extended injunction, the narrative — vakyanam samudayah — whose members are sakanksa-avayava, mutually requiring (the kanda's own mark of unity, 2.4), jointly establishing eka-artha-prasiddhaye, one meaning. There, the verse rules, vakyarthah api na vidyate: the *individual* sentence-meaning does not exist — it dissolves into the discourse-meaning exactly as padarthas dissolved into it (2.13). The sentence, so long defended as the real unit against the word, was never an absolute: it is the unit *where expectancy closes* (2.4) — and where expectancy spans sentences, the unit is the span. The 'sentence' of the kanda's title was always a variable: the maha-vakya, the great sentence, is whatever whole comprehension actually takes undivided — a verse, a chapter, in the limit (the tradition would say, and Kanda I's 1.1 implies) the one Veda, the one Word. The verse guards the system from making a new atom of the vakya — the same relativism of grain (1.88, 2.10) that dissolved the parts dissolves any absolute whole short of the whole itself. Analysis is scaffolding at *every* level; only the sabda-tattva is no one's abstraction.
Verse 2.76.contested: api ('either/also') carries the argument — as word-meanings do not exist (as independent reals), so sentence-meanings likewise, within the larger unity; some soften to 'the sentence-meaning is not (complete) there' — the radical reading is the consistent one and we keep it

Verse 2.77.translation: 'This operation is incidental'; 'this is obtained by single performance'; 'this, by repetition and differentiation'; 'here, annulment and combination' —
Verse 2.77.commentary: The opening of a remarkable passage (2.77-86): ten verses of pure quotation-catalogue, the working idiom of the ritual exegete recited at speed — each phrase a term of art from the Mimamsa toolbox, strung in oratio recta as if overheard at the sacrificial session. Here: prasangika, the incidental operation (discharged en passant by a rite performed for another purpose); tantrena labhyate, obtained by tantra, single-performance-serving-many (one threshing serves all the grains); avrtti and bheda, repetition and differentiation (when must an element be re-performed for each?); badha and samuccaya, annulment and combination (when do provisions exclude, when cumulate?). The rhetorical strategy unfolds across the catalogue and is stated at 2.87: every one of these operations is *word-meaning-dependent* — each presupposes that 'this element', 'this provision', can be isolated, weighed, transferred; the exegete's entire practice runs on padartha-realism. Bhartrhari recites the catalogue *fairly and at length* precisely because the concession is real and the resolution (2.87-88) must carry its full weight.

Verse 2.78.translation: 'Modification is proper in this domain'; 'the connection of this is not annulled'; 'this is extension of the general'; 'here the particular is extended' —
Verse 2.78.commentary: The catalogue continues with the transfer-operations. Uha, modification — the adaptation of mantras when a rite is transposed (new deity, new substance: the formula's 'rice' re-worded to 'nivara'); the exegete judges where uha is nyayya, proper. Atidesa, extension — the carrying of provisions from the archetype-rite (prakrti) to its derivatives (vikrti): sometimes samanyasya atidesa, the general rule extended; sometimes visesa atidisyate, a particular detail carried across. Each operation is a controlled *edit of meaning under identity of frame* — the rite remains 'the same' while its determinations are re-filled — and thus each presupposes the layered semantics of 2.64-71: only where a general survives its specifications can specification be re-done (uha), only where structure detaches from filling can structure travel (atidesa). The exegete manipulating transfer is manipulating exactly the strata the kanda's refined holism recognizes — a point banked silently for 2.88.

Verse 2.79.translation: 'Here, being-in-need is the qualification'; 'in this, the meaning is not divided'; 'this one's competence is obtained from the teaching'; 'his exclusion is in the other rite' —
Verse 2.79.commentary: The catalogue's third stretch: the doctrine of adhikara, competence — who may perform, who is addressed. Arthitva, being-in-need (desire for the fruit) as the qualifying condition; the scope-rulings ('the meaning is not divided' — one provision, not distributed); competence sastrat prapta, conferred by the teaching itself; and vyudasa, exclusion — this performer barred from that other rite. The exegete here is doing *semantics of address*: determining, from the injunction's words, the class of its addressees — reading subject-position out of sentence-meaning. That address is part of meaning (an injunction *to someone*) while no word in the sentence names the addressee is itself quiet evidence for meaning outrunning deposits — the vakyartha including, as structure, what no padartha supplies.

Verse 2.80.translation: 'This order is obtained from the text'; 'this, from the order of utterance and the rest'; 'this order here has force'; 'in this one, order is not intended' —
Verse 2.80.commentary: The krama-doctrine: in what sequence must the rite's elements proceed? The sources are ranked (another hierarchy, sibling to 2.73's): order from explicit sruti; order from utterance-sequence (the mantras' own order projected onto acts) and the other lesser sources (performance-order of the archetype, and so on); orders that are balavan, forceful, binding; and orders avivaksita, not intended at all — mere textual accident, no rule (1.136's canon and 2.69's avivaksa, applied to sequence itself). For the kanda this last is the resonant entry: even the exegete, sequence's greatest taskmaster, must distinguish *meant* order from *merely present* order — conceding that krama as such carries no authority until intention ratifies it. Sequence unintended is noise even in scripture: the ritual science's own avivaksita-krama is 2.24-27's doctrine (order as contributed, not found) speaking in the opponent's dialect.

Verse 2.81.translation: 'This is connected with the limbs of another'; 'this does not occasion the subsidiaries'; 'this occasions them'; 'this here is inseparable' —
Verse 2.81.commentary: The catalogue's structural stretch: anga-relations, the rite's part-whole engineering. Elements connected para-angaih, with another's limbs (serving a different principal than proximity suggests); elements aprayojaka, not occasioning the subsidiaries (present without commanding a retinue); their opposites, prayojaka; and the nantariyaka, the inseparable — what cannot be omitted without the whole failing. The exegete is here mapping *dependence-structure*: which parts serve which wholes, what can detach, what cannot. It is the sentence's own architecture (2.48's karaka-orbits, 2.4's guna-pradhana) rediscovered in the rite — unsurprisingly, since the rite is *known only through its injunctions*: ritual structure is sentence-structure enacted, and the exegete's anga-map is a syntax written in offerings.

Verse 2.82.translation: 'This is the principal, this the remainder'; 'this, moreover, is the order of application'; 'this assists it directly'; 'this qualifies it from afar' —
Verse 2.82.commentary: Continuation of the dependence-map, now graded by intimacy. Pradhana and sesa, principal and remainder (the Mimamsa's master-pair: everything in the rite is either end or auxiliary-to-end); viniyoga-krama, the order of application (which auxiliary is deployed to which principal, and in what precedence); and the two grades of service — saksat upakari, the direct assistant, touching the principal's own accomplishment, versus arad visesaka, the qualifier *from afar*, contributing at a distance through intermediaries. The two grades are the exegete's version of the semantic distinction the kanda drew at 2.73-74 (warranted nearly versus remotely): contribution is not flat; it has metric structure, near and far, and the science measures it. A theory of meaning as flat deposit-summing has no natural home for 'assistance from afar'; a theory of one meaning differentially articulated (2.71, 2.75) does.

Verse 2.83.translation: 'In this, the difference lies in the operation of the capacity, while here the fruit is what differs'; 'this difference is born of the connection'; 'there, the difference is not intended' —
Verse 2.83.commentary: The individuation-stretch: when is there *one* element, when two? The exegete's tests: sometimes sakti-vyapara-bheda, difference in the capacity's operation (one item, two workings); sometimes phala-bheda, difference of fruit (two results, hence two rites); sometimes bheda sambandhat jata, difference generated merely by the connection (relational, not intrinsic — the item differs only qua related); and sometimes, again, aviviaksita — present difference, unintended, hence null for the science. This is a working ontology of *aspects*: the exegete individuates by operation, by outcome, by relation, and by intention — never by bare form. Individuation answering to function and intention rather than to phonetic or physical identity is precisely how the kanda individuated its own objects (the word one under many utterances, 1.71; the sentence one under many construals, 2.39) — the catalogue keeps testifying that expert practice everywhere runs on the semantics the theory articulates.

Verse 2.84.translation: 'This is negation of the predicated, while this here is exclusionary negation'; 'this is secondary, this primary'; 'this is pervasive'; 'this weighty, this light' —
Verse 2.84.commentary: The logical stretch, headed by the tradition's most consequential distinction: prasajya-pratisedha versus paryudasa — negation that *denies a predication* ('he does not eat' — the act negated as such) versus negation that *excludes toward an affirmation* ('he eats not-this' — a positive selection wearing negative dress). The pair, born in ritual exegesis (does 'na' prohibit the act or redirect it?), became common property of every Indian logical school; its presence here marks the catalogue's reach into semantics proper. With it: gauna and mukhya, secondary and primary senses (metaphor's grammar, to which the kanda returns at length later); vyapin, the pervasive provision (holding across the whole domain); guru and laghu, weighty and light (the maxim-calculus of interpretive economy: prefer the lighter assumption). Negation's two kinds are the entry to watch: they are *indistinguishable in wording* — 'na' plus verb — and distinguished wholly by the one intended sentence-meaning (prohibition versus redirected injunction). No deposit-semantics reads them apart; comprehension of the undivided vakyartha does, every time an exegete rules.

Verse 2.85.translation: 'The limb-and-limbed relation of this is by differentiation'; 'this is optioned manifoldly'; 'this is restricted'; 'here fitness arises for it' —
Verse 2.85.commentary: The modal stretch: how provisions *bind*. Anga-angi-bhava bhedena, the part-whole relation obtaining under differentiation (the same element limb to one principal, not another); vikalpa, option — where texts permit alternatives, bahudha, manifoldly (and the science's deep discomfort with vikalpa, its rule that option is a last resort, is itself a preference for determinate meaning); niyama, restriction (the alternative closed); yogyatva, fitness arising — the element becoming eligible in context. Option, restriction, fitness: the deontic modalities of the rite, and, transposed, of the sentence (what may, must, can fill this role) — the karaka-fitness (yogyata) that sentence-comprehension weighs (2.44-47) here governing offerings. The catalogue is nearly complete; its cumulative effect — the sheer *density* of part-wise operations in expert practice — is about to be conceded in full (2.87) and absorbed (2.88).

Verse 2.86.translation: 'Of this, the difference is inferred from a mark seen in another sentence'; 'this word-meaning is divided out, having been abstracted from the word' —
Verse 2.86.commentary: The catalogue's last and most self-referential entries. First: difference established vakyantare drstat lingat — from a mark seen *in another sentence*: the exegete reads across texts, one passage's indication individuating another's provision (intertextual inference, the linga of 2.73 working at distance). Second — and with this the catalogue closes on the kanda's own key-term: ayam sabdad apoddhrtya padarthah pravibhajyate — 'this word-meaning is divided out, having been *abstracted* from the word'. The exegete himself says apoddhrtya: his practice's own idiom confesses that padarthas are extracts (2.10's doctrine in the practitioner's mouth). The catalogue thus ends by quoting the opponent performing the holist's analysis: ten verses of part-wise operations, and the last operation named is the abstraction that generates the parts. Nothing remains but to state the moral both ways — the objection (2.87: all this collapses if words express nothing) and the resolution (2.88: all this stands, as the statics of abstraction, within the undivided meaning).

Verse 2.87-88.translation: (87) Thus, the properties in sentences that are fastened to word-meanings — all of them would fail to be construed, if the word were non-expressive; (88) but when, though the sentence-meaning is undivided, abstraction is made according to the difference of capacities, what was stated is not contradicted — by way of the division into other (subordinate) sentences.
Verse 2.87-88.commentary: The excursus resolved, both edges honored. (87) states the objection at the full weight the ten-verse catalogue earned it: the dharmas operative in sentences — prasanga and tantra, uha and atidesa, adhikara, krama, anga-structure, the negations, the modalities: everything 2.77-86 recited — are padartha-upanibandhanah, *fastened to word-meanings*; sarve te na prakalperan — all of them would fail, the entire science with them, padam cet syad avacakam, if the word expressed nothing. The concession is total: no exegesis without word-meanings. (88) then resolves without retreat: avibhakte 'pi vakyarthe — the sentence-meaning remaining undivided — apoddhrte sakti-bhedat — abstraction being performed *according to the difference of capacities* (the saktis: the real articulations of the one meaning, 2.71's structure, 2.83's operations) — yatha-uktam na virudhyate: nothing stated is contradicted, the whole practice standing, vakyantara-vibhagena, by way of division into subordinate sentences — the analyst's re-casting of the one utterance as many part-statements (2.35cd's paraphrase-clauses, the exegete's provision-sentences), each a legitimate *station of the scaffold*. The settlement, in one sentence: word-meanings are real as capacities of the undivided meaning and fictional as its components — and the exegetical science is the complete, correct engineering manual of the scaffold, true of the vakyartha *as analyzed*, silent on it as it is. Both kandas' method is hereby vindicated at once: analysis everywhere (or the sciences die, 87), constitution nowhere (or comprehension lies, 88). The excursus that began as holism's hardest test ends as its most detailed confirmation.
Verse 2.87-88.contested: 88's vakyantara-vibhagena admits two construals — 'by division into other sentences' (the analyst's part-statements, our reading) or 'by the divisions belonging to other sentences' (transferring articulations across contexts); the settlement's substance — undivided meaning, capacity-wise abstraction, practice preserved — is identical on either

Verse 2.89.translation: Just as, of a single fragrance, a construction of difference is made across flowers and the rest — so too in the sentence a difference of meaning is prescribed.
Verse 2.89.commentary: A new movement opens with a delicate analogy about projected difference. One gandha — fragrance as such, or a single perfume — is met in flower, in oil, in unguent; and the mind parikalpana, constructs difference: jasmine-scent, oil-scent, as if the fragrance itself were many. The differences belong to the substrates and the occasions; the projected plurality is a construction upon one thing variously housed. Tatha vakye 'pi: so in the sentence — artha-bheda vidhiyate, difference of meaning is *prescribed*, laid on: the padarthas, the itemized senses, are the jasmine-scent and oil-scent of one undivided vakyartha, differentiated by its housings (the words) rather than in itself. Vidhiyate is drily chosen: prescription is the analyst's act (2.10), not the meaning's structure. The analogy also opens the movement's real theme — the psychology of similarity and misconstrual (2.90-94): how one thing partially apprehended generates confident pluralities, which is both how analysis works and how it errs.

Verse 2.90.translation: As in the gavaya, or in the man-lion, enveloped in a single cognition, one apprehends a part as simply similar to another kind —
Verse 2.90.commentary: The similarity-psychology, opened with the tradition's two best specimens. The gavaya, the wild ox, is the upamana-example of every epistemology primer: the townsman told 'a gavaya is like your cow' meets one and *recognizes via similarity*. The narasimha, the man-lion — Visnu's terrible avatara — is the fused kind: one being, leonine above, human below. In both cases the object arrives eka-jnana-avrta, wrapped in a *single* cognition — one seeing of one thing — and within that single seeing a *part* presents as sadrsam jatyantarasya, similar to another kind altogether: the gavaya's dewlap recalls the cow, the man-lion's mane the lion. The datum Bhartrhari wants: similarity-to-another is apprehended *inside an undivided grasp of one* — the comparison does not dismember the object into a cow-part plus a remainder; the man-lion is not half a man. Partial resemblance, wholly registered, within unbroken unity: precisely the structure the sentence needs (2.93-94 will apply it), where 'shared words' between two sentences are dewlaps — real resemblances of undivided wholes, not shared components.
Verse 2.90.contested: the verse runs syntactically into 2.91 (yatha ... 2.92 tatha); we mark the suspension with a dash. Narasimhe is read by some as 'in (the word) narasimha' (the compound as example) — the perceptual reading fits eka-jnana-avrte better

Verse 2.91.translation: — but the part that is unfamiliar, unseen before, he beholds; and to that extent the bewildered man everywhere arrives at non-comprehension.
Verse 2.91.commentary: The error-half of the psychology. The provincial meeting the man-lion knows lion-manes; the human torso in that setting is aprasiddham, adrstam — unfamiliar, never seen *so* — and before it he is mudhah, bewildered: tavati asamvidam pratipadyate, to just that extent he arrives at non-comprehension, and (sarvatra) the bewilderment spreads over the whole: not knowing the part, he cannot close his grasp of the thing. The observation is exact: comprehension of wholes tolerates familiar-part-resemblance smoothly (2.90) and is *globally* disrupted by one unfamiliar region — the unknown does not politely subtract itself; it unsettles the entire object (as the pika-word did the sentence, 2.72, the parallel Bhartrhari is building toward). Confidence in wholes, fragility at unfamiliar parts: both facts will transfer to sentences in 2.92-94 — where they explain, respectively, why analysis works on familiar material and why 'similarity of parts' between sentences can seduce the analyst into inventing shared components (the fallacy 2.92 names).
Verse 2.91.contested: asamvidam ('non-comprehension') is our reading of the transcription; the line has been construed 'he attributes (to it) whatever he has not comprehended' — the psychology (local unfamiliarity, global unsettling) is common to both

Verse 2.92.translation: So, through the presence of a 'pika' and the like, in a sentence utterly distinct, one fancies — as of something similar — the cognition of a meaning that is not there.
Verse 2.92.commentary: The fallacy diagnosed, closing the triptych. A sentence containing the opaque 'pika' (2.72) is atyanta-vilaksana, utterly distinct — *its* meaning is its own, undivided, partly unmanifest to this hearer. But the hearer, like 2.90's provincial, finds familiar dewlaps: the *other* words, met before in other sentences. And by that yoga, that co-presence, he manyate — fancies — sadrsasya iva samjnanam asato 'rthasya: a cognition, as it were of the similar, of a meaning *that is not there* — he projects into this sentence the meanings those look-alike words 'had' elsewhere, assembling a pseudo-comprehension out of remembered fragments. The diagnosis cuts deep against deposit-semantics: what the atomist calls 'the word's standing meaning, carried into each sentence' is here anatomized as the *hearer's transfer by resemblance* — real psychology, illegitimate semantics; the analogical shadow of other wholes mistaken for a component of this one. Word-meanings, once more (2.17), are citation-artifacts — but now with the error-theory supplied: they are *memories of other sentences*, reified. What guards against the fallacy is what guarded the provincial: completing the acquaintance — hearing the whole as the whole it is.
Verse 2.92.contested: asato 'rthasya ('of a meaning that is not there') is pointed: not-present-in-this-sentence, rather than non-existent absolutely; some press the stronger reading (fancied meanings are unreal as such) — the diagnosis stands on the weaker

Verse 2.93-94.translation: (93) Of one thing, similarity is marked in one part and difference in another — of a partless illumination, by a mind itself partless; (94) just so is similarity settled in one part and difference in another even of sentences whose properties are utterly distinct — though parts there are none.
Verse 2.93-94.commentary: The movement's resolution: partial similarity *without parts* — the apparent contradiction dissolved by the psychology just built. (93) states the general form with maximal paradox: one nirbhaga prakasa, one partless illumination (a color-expanse, a face, any unitary presentation), is marked similar-*here*, different-*there* — by a cetas, a mind, itself nirbhaga, partless (2.25-27: the sequenceless capacity). Neither term of the comparison has parts; yet 'part-wise' comparison happens — because *aspect-discrimination is the mind's act upon wholes*, not a reading-off of components: the partless cognition self-articulates (sva-saktya, 2.25) in registering respects of likeness. Two faces are alike 'about the eyes' though neither face is an assembly. (94) then delivers the application the movement was built for: so with sentences — atyanta-bhinna-dharmanam, of utterly distinct properties (each vakyartha its own undivided whole, 2.92), bhaga-abhave 'pi, *though parts there are none*: similarity settles 'in one part' (the shared-seeming words), difference 'in another'. Inter-sentential resemblance — the deposit-theorist's master-datum: 'the same word recurs with the same meaning across sentences' (1.71) — is thus accounted whole-to-whole: sentences resemble *in respects*, as faces do, and the 'recurrent word-meaning' is a respect of resemblance among undivided meanings, not a shared brick. The atomist's strongest evidence is hereby not denied but re-parented: recurrence is real, and it is a similarity-fact, not a composition-fact.
Verse 2.93-94.contested: 93's simile-base (the partless prakasa) is unspecified — light, cognition, or the sphota itself; all three work and the tradition wavers. The doctrine of 94 — respect-wise resemblance without composition — is the movement's secured yield on any choice

Verse 2.95.translation: If the form of the words is destroyed, how could there be the construction of a boundary? And when no boundary is grasped in the word, how is the meaning intended to be discerned?
Verse 2.95.commentary: An objector's double question, sharp enough that Bhartrhari devotes the next movement (2.96-111) to the machinery it demands. If — as the holism now says — the words' own forms are dissolved into the sentence (rupa-nasa: no independent word-reals), then: katham avadhi-kalpana — how is a *boundary* constructed at all? Where does one sentence (or word, in analysis) end and the next begin? And agrhita-avadhau sabde — the word/utterance having no grasped boundary — katham artho vivacyate: how is the meaning to be discerned/intended? Comprehension surely requires *delimitation*: the hearer must take *this much* as the unit to be understood (2.4's expectancy-closure presupposed a where-it-closes). The objection is the practical shadow of 2.76: if units dissolve upward and downward, the theory owes an account of segmentation — how speech is *portioned* by hearers and analysts if the portions are not objectively there. Bhartrhari's answer, unfolding across 2.96-111, will be drawn from grammar's own devices: tantra (single-utterance-serving-many), ekasesa (one-remainder), the sandhi-doctrine — cases where the science itself manufactures and dissolves boundaries at need, showing delimitation to be operational, not ontological.
Verse 2.95.contested: vivacyate we read with the transcription as from vi-vac (be discerned/expressed); a variant tradition reads vivaksyate ('is intended') — the objection is the same: no boundary, no determinate unit of meaning

Verse 2.96.translation: Like a commingling of forms, it stands settled in the word elsewhere; that form, among the various forms, is not measured out by single-performance.
Verse 2.96.commentary: The answer begins, with grammar's strangest boundary-phenomena as evidence. Forms samsarga iva, as-if-commingled: in the continuous utterance (samhita), word-shapes interpenetrate — sandhi fuses edges, one sound serving two words at once (the pararupa of 2.101 ahead) — the 'boundary' being genuinely *shared territory*. And such a form, standing among many forms, na tantrena parimisyate — is not measured out by tantra, single-performance: one cannot always utter a shared form once and have it count for each sharer; the commingled is not the common. The verse's work is to put on the table cases where wordhood's edges are *objectively indeterminate* in the utterance — fusion, sharing, overlap — so that the objector of 2.95 cannot pretend boundaries are simply given in speech and merely reported by grammar. They are not given; they are *managed* — by devices (tantra here glimpsed, ekasesa and the rest to come) whose very existence shows segmentation to be the grammar's engineering upon a continuum, exactly as the holist said (2.58: samhita as womb).
Verse 2.96.contested: the verse is among the kanda's most compressed and our construal — commingled forms resisting tantra-measurement — follows the movement's arc into 2.97-101; alternative readings take tantrena with what follows ('by the single utterance, that form is not exhausted among its various (occurrences)'), same evidentiary point

Verse 2.97.translation: In that non-difference, there operates as it were a commingling of differences; therefore the form, not being other than the other form, is (nonetheless) divided out.
Verse 2.97.commentary: The metaphysical gloss on 2.96's phenomena, stated as the movement's theorem. Tasmin abhede — in that non-difference, the fused continuum of actual utterance — bhedanam samsarga iva vartate: a commingling *of differences* operates, as-it-were: the analyst's word-forms are 'in' the stream the way currents are in a river — distinguishable flows of what is one water. Therefore: rupam rupantarat ananyat — the form is *not other* than the other form (at the fusion, the 'end' of one word and the 'start' of the next are numerically one stretch of sound) — and yet pravibhajyate, it *is divided out*: analysis assigns the shared stretch twice, to each word, without scruple and without falsity. Division-of-the-undifferent: grammar's daily sandhi-practice performs, at the phonetic joint, exactly the operation the holist claims for all analysis (2.10, 2.89) — real distinction drawn where no real otherness stands, valid as procedure, silent as ontology. The objector's boundary (2.95) is thus answered in kind: boundaries are *drawn*, expertly and reproducibly, upon the boundariless — and their reproducibility (the science's rules) is what their objectivity consists in.
Verse 2.97.contested: ananyat ('not other') we take at the fusion-point specifically; read globally ('the form is nothing other than the other form') the verse asserts full identity of all word-forms in the one utterance — the stronger claim is congenial to the kanda but the sandhi-context suggests the local one

Verse 2.98-99.translation: (98) In the science, sometimes oneness is relied upon even for the conveyor, while sometimes its difference from the conveyed is established — of grasper and grasped. (99) In 'u' oneness is relied upon and the respective-correspondence constructed; in the mention of lrn and lrt, difference is constructed together with the two graspables.
Verse 2.98-99.commentary: The evidence made technical: Panini's own metalanguage manipulates the identity and difference of *its own signs* at convenience. (98) states the practice: the sastra sometimes relies on the *oneness* of a pratyayaka, a conveying sign (one mention serving many conveyed items), sometimes on its *difference* from and among the conveyed — the grahana-grahya relation (mention and mentioned) engineered per rule. (99) gives the worked examples from the grammar's depths: the single technical 'u' relied on as one and distributed yatha-samkhyam, by respective correspondence (one sign, many assignments in order); the mention of lrn and lrt (the conditional and second-future affix-names) constructed as *different*, each with its own graspable. The argument: if word-forms were self-delimiting reals, the science could not treat one sign as one here and as many there, could not fuse and split mention at need — but it does, ubiquitously and without paradox, because signs *have* the individuation their use assigns (2.83's aspect-individuation, now in the metalanguage). Grammar's meta-practice is the finest-grained confirmation available: even where language talks about language, boundaries and identities are operational postings, not discoveries — and what holds of the mention holds of the mentioned (1.65's regress collapsed into evidence).
Verse 2.98-99.contested: the examples are stenographic and our unpacking (u-pratyahara distributed; lrn/lrt differentiated) follows the grammatical tradition's cases; specialists locate the precise sutras variously — the meta-linguistic moral is unaffected

Verse 2.100.translation: The form 'yasya' (in the rule) is the an-form expressing its name-bearers; for there is no possibility of mention by way of what is (merely) being understood.
Verse 2.100.commentary: A precision on how mention works, guarding the meta-practice of 2.98-99 from a misreading. In a rule like Panini's (the tradition points to 6.4.148, yasya iti ca), the cited form functions as the *an-form* — the technically-shaped sign-body — abhidhayaka of its samjnins, expressing the items it names. The principle in the second line: na hi pratiyamanena grahanasya asti sambhavah — mention cannot proceed by way of the merely-understood: what a rule *grasps* (grahana) must be a *form given in the rule*, not a content the reader would understand anyway; the metalanguage operates on exhibited shapes, not on inferred senses. This looks anti-holist — mention riding forms! — and is in fact the division of labor the settlement (2.88) drew: within the scaffold, forms are the currency (the science individuates by shape, cites by shape, 2.98-99 engineers shape-identity); the scaffold's forms are themselves postings within the undivided (2.97). Rule-craft is form-craft; comprehension is whole-craft; the kanda's care is to keep each sovereign in its own house.
Verse 2.100.contested: the sutra-context of yasya/an is disputed among the grammarians themselves and our identification follows the common gloss; the verse's principle — grahana requires exhibited form — is clear regardless of the rule intended

Verse 2.101.translation: And this 'u', undivided, is the fastening of different sentences; and its mention is made under difference — like the single subsequent-form belonging to both.
Verse 2.101.commentary: The boundary-sharing phenomenon named at last in its classical form. The undivided 'u' (the technical sign of 2.99, or the sandhi-vowel) serves as bhinna-vakya-nibandhanam — the *fastening of different sentences/rules*: one form, anchoring plural contexts, mentioned bhedena, under difference, as many as its employments. The simile seals it: pararupam iva dvayoh — like the pararupa, the 'subsequent form' of sandhi doctrine, where two adjacent vowels resolve into *one* sound that grammatically *belongs to both* words (the single 'e' of a fusion being the end of one word and the beginning of the next, by rule). One phonetic body, two grammatical citizenships: the limit-case of everything the movement has argued. Boundaries not only can be drawn on the undifferent (2.97) — the science *assigns the same stretch to two units at once* when the economy of description demands. After pararupa, the objector's 'where does the word end?' (2.95) has its answer: where the grammar, for its purposes, posts the end — including, when useful, in two places at once.
Verse 2.101.contested: whether the 'u' here is the pratyahara-sign continued from 2.99 or the sandhi-vowel is undecidable and immaterial — the pararupa-simile carries the doctrine either way

Verse 2.102.translation: And by the one who sets aside the lengthening of the base before pluta, and likewise the coalescence of the two vowels, difference among words is relied upon to the utmost.
Verse 2.102.commentary: The counter-practice acknowledged: grammar also *maximizes* boundaries when its purposes so run. Certain operations are vyudasyat, set aside — blocked: the pluta (protracted) vowel does not trigger the base-lengthening (anga-vivrddhi) that its shape might invite; vowel-coalescence (samahara acoh) is barred at certain junctures — and such blockings are effected precisely by *relying on difference to the utmost* (atyantam asritah bhedah): treating the words as utterly separate, their edges rigid, so that cross-boundary operations cannot see across. Read with 2.101, the pair completes the demonstration: the same science that fuses boundaries (pararupa: one form, two words) also *hardens* them (blocking: two words, no interaction) — per rule, per need. Boundary-strength is a *parameter of description*, tuned case by case — which is unintelligible if boundaries are facts of the speech, and exactly intelligible if they are postings of the analysis (2.97). The objector's segmentation (2.95) has now been shown, from the science's own casebook, to be manufactured in both directions: dissolved where description profits, absolutized where it must.
Verse 2.102.contested: the specific blockings compressed here are variously identified (the pluta-contexts of Panini 6.1, the samahara-exceptions); our rendering keeps the operations generic — the tuning-of-boundaries moral is the verse's evident office

Verse 2.103.translation: As, in words like ardharca, difference of form (obtains) according to sequence — so, by single-performance, when there is one-wordhood, the hearing of the different is otherwise.
Verse 2.103.commentary: A final pair of boundary-cases, from the lexicon's own oddities. Ardharca, 'half-verse' — the traditional head of a gana (the ardharcadi class) of words wavering between genders and, emblematically here, between *analyses*: read krama-wise, as sequence (ardha + rca), its form divides one way; read tantra-wise, as one word uttered once, the sruti — the hearing — of the 'different' components is anyatha, otherwise: the parts are not heard *as* parts at all. One phonetic body; two hearings; the difference made not by the sound but by the descriptive frame (sequence-analysis versus unit-citation) under which it is taken. The compound-word — language's own boundary-dissolving device, where yesterday's phrase is today's word — is the natural last witness: lexicalization is boundary-*history*, edges fading as analysis-frames shift, and no acoustic fact marks the transition. Segmentation, once more, follows the taking, not the sound (2.58's vrtti-bhedena, now at the lexical scale).
Verse 2.103.contested: the verse is elliptical to the edge of riddle; our construal — ardharca-class words as frame-dependent in their part-hearing — follows the gana-tradition and the movement's argument; readers who take ardharcadi merely as 'words with variable gender' get a thinner but compatible point (form-difference without meaning-difference, frame-assigned)

Verse 2.104.translation: In the domain of the continuous utterance, the phonemes, unchanging in their own form, go as it were to the condition of other words, through the assumption of another capacity.
Verse 2.104.commentary: The movement's doctrine crystallized in one verse — the samhita as the place where identity and difference trade places. In samhita-visaya, the continuous utterance, the varnas are svarupena avikarinah: unchanged in their own form — the sounds are what they are, the stream is one. And yet sabdantaratvam yanti iva: they *go, as it were, to being other words* — the same stretches, under sandhi and re-segmentation, belong now to this word, now to that (2.101's double citizenship) — sakty-antara-parigrahat, through the assumption of *another capacity*. Nothing in the sound changes; its office changes. Sakti, the kanda's deepest word (2.88: abstraction follows capacity-difference), here does its finest work: wordhood is a capacity *worn* by stretches of the invariant stream, and re-segmentation is capacity-exchange, not sound-change. The iva, as ever, holds the ontology honest: they do not *become* other words (there are no word-things to become); they present under other offices. The objector of 2.95 receives here the theory his question deserved: boundaries are the seams of capacities, and capacities are assigned by the whole (the one vakya, the one samhita) to its own presented articulations.
Verse 2.104.contested: none of weight; the verse is the movement's clearest and the tradition treats it as summary

Verse 2.105.translation: It is seen, among graspable things, that through modification of the sense-faculty and the rest, the grasping differs without the thing abandoning its own self; that is the way with the heard.
Verse 2.105.commentary: The epistemological warrant for 2.104, drawn from perception at large. Among grahya vastuni, graspable things generally, grasping varies indriya-adi-vikarena — with modifications of the faculty and its conditions: the same white shell grasped yellow by the jaundiced eye, the same taste sweet or flat by the fevered tongue — bhinnam grahanam atma-tyagad rte: the grasping differs *without the thing's abandoning its self*. Perceptual variation is routinely receiver-side; no one infers a new shell from a new seeming. Sa kramah srutau: that is the pattern with the heard — the one utterance-stream grasped now as these words, now as those (2.103-104), under different analytic 'faculties' (the frames, the trainings, the purposes of hearers and analysts), remains self-same throughout; the variation of segmentation is variation of grasping. The verse generalizes the kanda's constant move (1.75-77: delivery-properties are grasping-conditions; 2.25: sequence is the cognition's act) into a maxim: *difference of apprehension never by itself certifies difference in the apprehended* — the principle on which every reduction of parts to postings has quietly run, here stated as common epistemology.
Verse 2.105.contested: srutau we take as 'with respect to the heard (utterance)'; some take it 'in the Veda' (sruti), making the verse about scriptural construal — the maxim covers both and the movement favors the acoustic reading

Verse 2.106.translation: Through connection with the act of denoting, even among words unmodified, the form — one and the same — shines forth with utter difference.
Verse 2.106.commentary: The converse of 2.104-105, completing the chiasmus: there, invariant sound wearing different word-capacities; here, invariant word-form shining as utterly different *through its denoting-employments*. Abhidhana-kriya-yoga — connection with the act of denoting: the same form 'gauh', unmodified (avikrtesu sabdesu), deployed of the beast, of the constellation, in citation, in metaphor — tad eva ekam rupam atyanta-bhedena prakasate: the *very same one* form radiates with utter difference. Neither sound nor lexical shape changes; the act it serves in changes, and with it everything comprehension receives. Prakasate keeps the register honest — manifestation, not transformation: the differences are modes of the one form's shining, occasioned by employment (the vyapara-individuation of 2.83). Between 2.104 and 2.106 the full doctrine of linguistic identity stands: sameness of sound underdetermines wordhood (capacity decides); sameness of word-form underdetermines meaning (employment decides) — identity at every level is office-identity, and office is conferred by the undivided act of speaking. The Vedic recitation-sciences supply the next verse's confirmation.
Verse 2.106.contested: none of substance; the verse's placement (before the saman-example) marks it as the principle 2.107 instantiates

Verse 2.107.translation: The saman is (nothing but) the rks, or mere melody — not, however, a further substance; by difference of melody, those very rks, modified, are severally grasped.
Verse 2.107.commentary: The confirmation from sacred musicology — the tradition's own limit-case of one-form-many-shinings. What *is* a saman, a chant of the Samaveda? The doctrine the verse ratifies: the samans are the rks — the Rgvedic verses themselves — sung; or say, mere giti, melody, laid upon them; na tu dravyantaram: *not a further substance*, no third thing over verse and tune. Giti-bhedat, by difference of melody, ta eva rcah, those very verses, vikrtah, modified (stretched, repeated, ornamented by the chant's syllable-insertions), vigrhyante — are severally grasped, counted as distinct samans, catalogued, named, transmitted as separate sacred items. An entire Veda — its canon, its schools, its ritual employments — individuated by *mode of rendition* upon unchanged texts: office-identity (2.106) practiced at scripture's own scale, with the tradition's full authority behind the na dravyantaram. If the samans can be many while being the rks, the analyst's words can be many while being the one utterance (2.104): the sacred case licenses the semantic one.
Verse 2.107.contested: rco va gitimatre va compresses a debate the Mimamsa also conducts (is the saman the verse, the melody, or the composite?); the karika's own verdict — no further dravya — is what the argument needs, and both remaining options serve it

Verse 2.108.translation: When, by a device, the utterances of different words having a single remainder are gathered — in uttering them by single-performance, correctness in the science is accepted.
Verse 2.108.commentary: The ekasesa doctrine arrives — grammar's most audacious boundary-device, and the movement's crowning exhibit. Panini's rule (1.2.64ff.): when several identical forms would occur together ('ramas ca ramas ca'), *one remains* — ekasesa, single-remainder — 'ramau', 'ramah': one uttered form standing for many words. The verse: such gatherings, upayat, by device — sruti-samhare, in the gathering-up of the (plural) utterances — uttered tantrena, by single-performance (the ritual term of 2.77 now naturalized in grammar): sadhutvam isyate, correctness is accepted in the science. Weigh what the science thereby countenances: *several words* — really several, by its own count: each with its case, its reference, its role — *whose utterance is one form uttered once*. The deposit-theorist's minimal assumption — one word-token, one meaning-contribution — is breached by rule: here are meanings without severally-uttered vehicles, vehicles bearing plural citizenship not at their edges (2.101) but in toto. Only office-identity absorbs this smoothly: the one form serves many word-offices, the offices conferred by the construction's needs — the device is 'correct' because correctness was always adjudicated at the whole (2.109 next states exactly that condition).
Verse 2.108.contested: the transcription wavers (samhare/samharo); the ekasesa-identification is the tradition's and certain. Upayat ('by device') quietly re-invokes 2.38: ekasesa as upaya, the scaffold's own scaffolding
