Verse 2.109.translation: And having taken up a single utterance even for those possessing difference of form, the uttering must be done by single-performance; otherwise they are not correct.
Verse 2.109.commentary: The ekasesa-doctrine extended and its condition stated with legislative force. Extended: even rupa-bhedavatam api — even where the gathered words *differ in form* (the dvandva-adjacent cases the grammarians debate: 'pita-mata' gathered as 'pitarau', 'parents', the mother uttered nowhere yet meant) — a single sruti is parigrhya, taken up, and the uttering *must* be tantrena, by single-performance. Then the condition, unblinking: anyatha te na sadhavah — otherwise (uttered severally where the device is prescribed) they are *not correct*. Mark the inversion this completes: correctness — sadhutva, Kanda I's dharma-bearing property (1.27, 1.141) — here *requires* the non-utterance of words that are nonetheless fully meant and grammatically counted. The severally-spelled-out 'correct' forms would be solecism; the fused device is the norm. Against any semantics that pins meaning to uttered vehicles, the science's own norm of correctness legislates meaning-without-vehicle wherever ekasesa runs. The scaffold does not merely tolerate the holist's ontology; at these joints it *mandates* it.
Verse 2.109.contested: which form-different gatherings fall under the rule is the grammarians' own long dispute (the ekasesa of 'pitarau'-type is contested in the tradition); the karika legislates the principle and leaves the casebook to the sastra

Verse 2.110.translation: And of sentences of like form, it is taught by the science: from utterance by single-performance, a single correct form is obtained.
Verse 2.110.commentary: The device propagated to the sentence-level — completing the movement at the grain where the kanda lives. Sarupanam vakyanam: sentences of like form — the shared refrain, the repeated mantra serving multiple ritual stations, the common formula meant severally in several connections. The science teaches (sastrena pratipaditam): uttered tantrena, once-for-many, ekam rupam sadhu upalabhyate — *one* correct form is obtained, and it discharges them all. Whole sentences, then — each with its own vakyartha, its own station, its own fruit — borne by one uttered body: the ekasesa-logic (2.108-109) at full scale. The theoretical harvest is the movement's QED: if one utterance can *be* many sentences (as office), then sentencehood was never in the utterance — it was always the office conferred by the employment-whole (the rite, the discourse, 2.76's larger unity) upon the uttered form. The boundary-question of 2.95 dissolves in its final form: not only are edges posted, whole *units* are posted — one body, many sentences; many bodies (the substitute-cases), one rite; the mapping engineered by the science at every grain, answerable only to the undivided purposes it serves.
Verse 2.110.contested: none of weight; the sentence-level tantra is the ritual-exegetical practice (one recitation serving many applications) and the verse cites it as settled

Verse 2.111.translation: As one thing comes to have many forms through the taking-up of the reed and the like — just so, through single-performance, many may come to have a single form.
Verse 2.111.commentary: The movement's closing simile, chosen for its perfect symmetry. One breath, taken up through nalika-adi — the reed-pipe and its kin (the flute's bores, the instruments' channels) — becomes many-formed: one wind, many notes, the plurality conferred by the apertures it is passed through. Yatha — and tatha, inversely: through tantra, single-performance, bahunam eka-rupata — the many (words, sentences, rites) come to *one* form: plurality gathered into a single uttered body, the unity conferred by the device it is passed through. One-to-many by instrumental taking-up; many-to-one by performative gathering: the two operations the whole movement has documented (2.96-110), here shown to be the same operation run in opposite directions — form and office trading multiplicities under devices. Beneath both, unmoved, the invariant: the breath; the utterance-stream; the undivided meaning. The simile quietly re-sounds 1.116's fan and the vamsa-flute of the old cosmologies: manifestation as one wind through many stops — the kanda's technical grammar of boundaries ending on the system's oldest music.
Verse 2.111.contested: nalika-adi we take as the wind-instrument's reed/pipe (the traditional gloss); some read 'the (moulding-)tube' of metallurgy — one metal, many castings; either instrument serves the one-through-channels figure

Verse 2.112.translation: As there is separate occurrence of sentences whose words are of like form, so, in the absence of another sentence, these would have separate meaningfulness.
Verse 2.112.commentary: A hinge-verse, turning the movement's results toward the kanda's next great topic (the abhidheya/prayojana distinction of 2.113). Sentences pada-sarupanam — of like word-forms — occur prthak, separately: the same wording, uttered in two settings, is *two sentences* (office-individuation, 2.110 inverted: one form, many units — here each unit its own occurrence). And vakyantara-abhave — where no other sentence stands (no larger discourse absorbing them, 2.76) — syad esam prthag-arthata: they *would have* separate meaningfulness, each its own vakyartha, notwithstanding identity of wording. Same words, different sentences, different meanings: the last nail in form-semantics, driven with the movement's accumulated hammer — if meaning rode wording, like wording would compel like meaning; it does not (the repeated formula means per station, 2.110; the quoted sentence means otherwise than its original; the rehearsed line otherwise than the avowal). What fixes each occurrence's meaning is its *employment-whole* — and the anatomy of that fixing, the difference between what a sentence's words denote and what the sentence is *for*, is precisely where the text now turns.
Verse 2.112.contested: the double conditional (occurrence separate; meaning separate *in the absence of a larger sentence*) we read as stated; some construe vakyantara-abhave as 'since there is no (real) other sentence' — a denial that re-wordings are ever distinct — against the movement's drift, and we decline it

Verse 2.113.translation: The expressible is the word's meaning; the purpose is the sentence's meaning — whoever (holds otherwise), for him no connection among sentences is intelligible.
Verse 2.113.commentary: The distinction on which the kanda's remaining semantics is built, legislated in a half-line each. Abhidheyah padasya arthah: the *expressible* — the denotable, the lexical content — is the word's meaning: what analysis assigns the extracted units (2.10), the scaffold's currency (2.88). Vakyasya arthah prayojanam: the sentence's meaning is *purpose* — prayojana: what the utterance is deployed to accomplish, its point, its fruit-in-view. The category-difference is the doctrine: word-meanings are contents; sentence-meaning is *telos* — not a bigger content assembled from small ones but a different kind of thing altogether (2.55's bhinna-laksana, now named): the one act-of-meaning whose service the contents are abstracted as serving. And the argument appended: whoever holds otherwise — meaning as content all the way up — tasya na sambandho vakyanam upapadyate: for him *connection among sentences* is unintelligible. Discourse-coherence (the samudaya of 2.76, the paragraph, the rite's liturgy) is teleological through and through: sentences connect by subserving ends within larger ends (2.82's viniyoga at discourse-scale); mere contents, stacked, connect not at all — no proposition reaches for its successor; purposes do. The kanda's two questions — what unifies the sentence? what unifies sentences? — receive here one answer: prayojana, the undivided point, at every scale the principle of unity that content-semantics forever lacks.
Verse 2.113.contested: yasya tasya we render 'whoever (holds otherwise), for him' with the standard supplement; the ellipse can be filled differently ('of whatever [word], connection... is not intelligible [without purpose]') — the abhidheya/prayojana legislation, the verse's substance, is textually secure

Verse 2.114.translation: There, it is the action-words themselves that mutually depend; and the connection, attached to the action-word, is thereby understood.
Verse 2.114.commentary: The teleology of 2.113 given its grammatical mechanics — how purpose-connection actually shows in the texture of discourse. Tatra — there, among connected sentences — kriya-padani eva vyapeksante parasparam: it is the *action-words* that mutually depend: verb reaches for verb across sentence-edges ('having-bathed... he-offers... he-recites...' — the rite's liturgy; 'she left... he followed' — the narrative's spine); the inter-sentential expectancy that 2.113 said content cannot ground runs concretely along the verbs. And the sambandha, kriya-pada-anusakta, attached to the action-word, atah pratiyate — is understood therefrom: discourse-connection is *read off* verbal dependence. The mechanics ratify the metaphysics: verbs are grammar's purpose-bearers — each names a sadhya, a to-be-accomplished (2.48) — so purposes-within-purposes (2.113's coherence) surface as verb-upon-verb dependence; the karaka-architecture that organized the single sentence (nouns orbiting the verb) iterates upward as sentences orbiting principal actions (2.6's tinanta qualifying tinanta, now at discourse-scale, its promise redeemed). One structure, three scales — noun to verb, clause to clause, sentence to sentence — and at every scale the same principle: unity flows from the end in view, and the end wears the verb.
Verse 2.114.contested: none of substance; the verse applies 2.6 and 2.48 at discourse-scale as we render, and the tradition reads it so

Verse 2.115.translation: On the construction of word-meaning as the individual: either repetition, or restatement; but the meaning, complete in each, operates in them as co-present.
Verse 2.115.commentary: A dilemma for individual-denotation, resolved by completion-per-each. If words denote vyaktis, individuals, then in 'gauh gauh' (or in a plural: 'the cows') the second occurrence is either avrtti, repetition — the same individual denoted twice, redundantly — or anuvada, restatement — reference back, adding nothing. Either way the second token is semantically idle, which affronts the economy every school professes. The holist's escape re-uses 2.18: pratyekam tu samapto 'rthah — the meaning is *complete in each* occurrence — and sahabhutesu vartate, operates in them as co-present: neither token is a fraction or an echo; each is a station of the whole meaning, their plurality the utterance's mode of presenting one content under co-presence (as each glance sees the whole painting, 2.8). The dilemma dissolves because it presupposed deposit-arithmetic — count the tokens, count the contributions — and the whole-at-each principle refuses the count.

Verse 2.116.translation: Though the sentence-meaning is free of construction, the constructions rest upon the bringing-into-being; on this topic the doctrines of the ancients have gone many ways.
Verse 2.116.commentary: The pivot into the kanda's center. The vakyartha itself is avikalpa — free of conceptual construction: one, undivided, given whole (everything since 2.7 has argued it). The vikalpas — the constructions, the itemized word-meanings and relations analysis reports — bhavana-asrayah: rest upon *bhavana*, the bringing-into-being — the cognitive realization-process in the hearer (2.27's bhavana-samaya, where the sequenceless capacity works). Constructions belong to the coming-to-understand, not to the understood: process-artifacts, real as process, projected as content. And the honest doxographic register resumes: atra adhikarane, on this very topic — the relation of constructed articulation to unconstructed meaning — the purvesam vadah, the ancients' doctrines, bahudha gatah, have gone many ways. The next verses (2.117-135) canvass them: pratibha by practice, convention, the denotation-catalogue — the tradition's whole semantics laid out before Bhartrhari's own resolution (2.143ff.). The avikalpa/vikalpa pair also silently aligns the kanda with the perception-debate (nirvikalpaka/savikalpaka): sentence-understanding as the grammarian's pratyaksa, given whole before construction — against the Buddhist, for whom construction *is* the verbal; the battle of 1.123 rejoined on new ground.
Verse 2.116.contested: bhavana we render 'bringing-into-being' (the realization-process); the term also means 'meditative cultivation' and 'the efficacy-toward-being' of Mimamsa verb-theory — Bhartrhari plausibly hears all three; the Mimamsa resonance matters at 2.124-125

Verse 2.117.translation: By others, every word is held to be a cause of intuition through practice — as in the conveying of things as they are to children and to animals.
Verse 2.117.commentary: Pratibha enters — through a reported view, as the kanda's great terms always do. Aparaih smrtam: others hold that sarvo 'pi sabdah, every word, is pratibha-hetuh abhyasat — a cause of *pratibha*, intuitive flash, through *abhyasa*, practice, repeated exposure (the term from 1.35: the jeweller's untransmittable expertise). Their evidence: balanam ca tirascam ca — children and *animals*: the calf finds the udder at a call, the dog answers its name, the infant obeys before it can parse — yathartha-pratipadane, in being brought to things as they are. Comprehension without grammar, lexicon, or inference: word acting on prepared life directly, waking a knowing that was never taught propositionally. The view's radicalism: it makes *practice-formed intuition* the entire mechanism of understanding — no deposits, no assembly, just trained flash — dissolving semantics into a natural history of responsiveness. Bhartrhari will not leave it here (2.118-119 immediately press its edges), but he has chosen his reporters carefully: the animal-and-infant datum is *unanswerable* by deposit-semantics (what lexicon does the calf consult?), and pratibha, entering as another school's word, will by 2.143-152 have become his own doctrine's name — abhyasa-formed intuition generalized into the very form of sentence-understanding, and (at 2.146-152) of skill and instinct across the living world.
Verse 2.117.contested: whose view 2.117 reports is unrecorded; the abhyasa-mechanism suggests naturalist strands the tradition preserves namelessly. That Bhartrhari ultimately *adopts* pratibha (2.143) while here attributing it is his standard doxographic choreography (cf. 2.30), not evidence against adoption

Verse 2.118.translation: And that practice, by some, is held to be convention, without received tradition — the showing that 'immediately after this, this is to be done'.
Verse 2.118.commentary: The practice-doctrine given its conventionalist reading, and quietly its most subversive one. Kaiscit: some construe the abhyasa of 2.117 as samaya — *convention*, agreement — and anagama, *without received tradition*: not the beginningless transmission Kanda I sanctified (1.27-31, 1.141) but mere settled usage, upadarsanam, an ostensive showing: 'anantaram idam karyam asmat' — after this (word), this is to be done. Language-learning as drill: word paired with expected act, the pairing exhausted by regularity — no eternal sambandha (1.23), no sista-lineage, just coordination grown stable. The view is the ancient ancestor of every use-theory of meaning: to understand a word is to know what to do next. Bhartrhari reports it without immediate rebuttal because its half-truth is one he needs (comprehension *is* trained responsiveness; the calf of 2.117 proves it) while its frame — convention *instead of* tradition — collides with his deepest commitments; 2.119 lets the collision surface on the conventionalist's own terrain: the words whose 'expected act' no drill could ever exhibit.
Verse 2.118.contested: anagamah we read as qualifying the abhyasa ('a practice without agama') — the conventionalist's denial of tradition; it has been read as a separate charge ('and that practice is un-traditional') in Bhartrhari's own voice, making the verse rebuttal rather than report — the sequel favors report

Verse 2.119.translation: 'All words have a meaning' — such is the mark of the conveyable; for 'apurva', 'deity' and 'heaven', they say, it is the same as for 'cow' and the rest.
Verse 2.119.commentary: The conventionalist's ostension-model tested against the words that break it — and the tradition's own semantics stated in the testing. The maxim: asti arthah sarva-sabdanam — all words have meaning; meaningfulness is the pratyayya-laksana, the defining mark of the conveyable (a 'word' that conveyed nothing would not be a word, 2.53). Now the test-set: apurva — the unseen ritual potency, the efficacy that bridges rite and remote fruit (Mimamsa's central posit); devata — the deity as injunction addresses it; svarga — heaven, the promised fruit no living speaker has seen. For these, they say, the semantics is samam gavadisu — *the same as for 'cow'*: meaning attaches in the ordinary way. But no upadarsana (2.118) ever paired 'svarga' with an exhibited heaven; no drill, no ostension, no expected-next-act exhausts these meanings. If they mean as 'cow' means — and the whole exegetical science (2.64-88) requires it — then meaning-as-such cannot be constituted by exhibition-drill: the conventionalist's mechanism fails precisely where scripture's core vocabulary lives. The verse thus turns the maxim of universal meaningfulness into an argument: semantics must be of a kind that covers the unexhibitable — which trained-flash pratibha (waked by words, not shown by objects) and eternal-sambandha tradition can claim, and samaya-drill cannot.
Verse 2.119.contested: whether 2.119 is the conventionalist's own boast (our reading: 'the same as for cow', which 2.120 then dismantles) or already the rebuttal is delicately poised; either way the apurva-set is the fulcrum, and the tradition reads the verse as the test it plainly is

Verse 2.120.translation: But the grasping of the form through practice in observing usage — that is not the word's object; for that rests upon a different effort.
Verse 2.120.commentary: The discrimination that saves pratibha from its conventionalist reading — task 2.118 set, here discharged with one clean cut. Two things practice yields, and they must not be confused. Prayoga-darsana-abhyasat, from practice in observing usage, comes akara-avagraha — the grasping of the *form*: the learner's growing command of shapes, collocations, the sound-habits of the community (2.118's drill, real and necessary). But: na sa sabdasya visayah — that is not the *word's object*, not meaning; sa hi yatna-antara-asrayah, it rests on a *different effort* — the effort of form-acquisition, distinct in kind from the semantic waking the word performs (2.117). Learning the language and understanding the utterance are two achievements: drill builds the instrument; meaning happens when the built instrument is played (the fan of 1.116 must exist — and its existing is not the breeze). The conventionalist's error is a level-confusion: he watched the form-drill and called it semantics. With the cut made, both truths stand in order: abhyasa conditions comprehension (against intellectualism), meaning outruns every drill (against conventionalism, 2.119's test-words now accounted: their *forms* are drilled like any; their meanings wake as all meanings do — by pratibha, whose theory now has the field). The catalogue of denotation-doctrines (2.121ff.) follows as the last survey before that theory is named in full.
Verse 2.120.contested: yatna-antara ('a different effort') we take as form-learning versus meaning-grasp; some construe 'dependent on another's effort' (the teacher's) — the level-cut survives either construal

Verse 2.121.translation: Some differences are illuminated by words as their expressors; certain others, arising-in-consequence, are (also) thought to be word-meaning.
Verse 2.121.commentary: The denotation-catalogue opens with its governing distinction: the *expressed* versus the *entailed*. Kecid bhedah — some determinations — prakasyante sabdaih tad-abhidhayibhih: are illuminated by words as their proper expressors — what the word is *for*, its abhidheya (2.113). Kamscit — others — anunispadinah: *arising-in-consequence*, following along — determinations that come to cognition with the expressed without being expressed: say 'cow' and the hearer has also, willy-nilly, singular number, presence, generic shape. Are these consequences *meaning*? Manvate — some think so, stretching sabdartha to cover the whole cognitive wake; others (the verses following) draw the line tight. The distinction is the catalogue's instrument: each doctrine surveyed (2.122-135) is a different placement of the expressed/entailed boundary — jati-theorists parking individuals as anunispadin (2.122), form-theorists parking shapes (2.123), action-theorists parking usage (2.124) — and Bhartrhari's eventual verdict (2.134-135: comprehension varies per hearer; the boundary is drawn by vivaksa and construal, not found) is already latent in the fact that the boundary *can* be disputed at all.
Verse 2.121.contested: anunispadin ('arising-in-consequence') is the catalogue's coinage; 'concomitant', 'supervenient', 'resultant' all serve — the term marks the entailed-but-unexpressed, and our rendering keeps its processual flavor

Verse 2.122.translation: When the word conveys the universal, the individual comes along as an adjunct; the universal-word does not lean upon the differences residing in the individual.
Verse 2.122.commentary: The jati-doctrine, catalogued first as the grammarians' default (1.93's kin, Vajapyayana's line in the tradition's memory). The word expresses the universal: 'gauh' means cowhood. The vyakti, the individual — since cowhood stands nowhere but in cows — anusangini, comes along as adjunct: entailed, not expressed (2.121's boundary drawn at the genus). Consequence, stated as the doctrine's signature: na tad-vyakti-gatan bhedan jati-sabdo 'valambate — the universal-word does not *lean on* the differences residing in the individual: its semantic weight never rests on this cow's color, size, or station; those enter comprehension (if at all) as the adjunct's baggage, available to context, invisible to the word. The doctrine's strength is 2.69's avivaksa made systematic (unmeant particulars, principled); its strain shows where language *does* reach individuals ('bring *this* cow') — deixis, names, the whole apparatus the vyakti-theorists (Vyadi's line) built their rival case on. The catalogue holds both in suspension; the kanda's own resolution (individuation by employment, 2.104-106) has already dissolved the question's presupposition — that the word, rather than the utterance-act, is where denotation is fixed.

Verse 2.123.translation: Nor does the expressor convey the shapes of pots and the like; because it is installed upon the bare thing, the cognition of those is inseparably-consequent.
Verse 2.123.commentary: The boundary-instrument applied to a subtler case: shape. Say 'ghata', pot. The hearer's cognition arrives *shaped* — a bulging-bellied, narrow-necked something stands in it. Did the word convey the akara? The doctrine here catalogued answers no: na akaran pratyayati vacakah — the expressor does not convey the shapes; vastu-matra-nivesitvat, it is installed upon the *bare thing* — the pot as such, unconfigured — and tad-gatih nantariyaki: the shape-cognition is *inseparably-consequent* (nantariyaka, 2.81's term for the rite's inseparable elements): you cannot cognize pot bare, so shape rides in of necessity — entailed by the cognitive act's own conditions, expressed by nothing. The analysis is fine: it distinguishes what the word semantically carries from what cognition must supply to host the carriage — the imagery, the default configuration, the mind's obligatory furnishing. A hearer's rich pot-image is thus no evidence of a rich pot-meaning: semantics thin, hosting thick. The verse arms the catalogue's eventual moral (2.134-135): since hosting varies per mind while the word stands one, the seeming variety of 'what the word brings' is the variety of its receptions.
Verse 2.123.contested: vastu-matra ('the bare thing') is the doctrine's load-bearing posit — thing-sans-configuration — and its coherence is precisely what rival schools (Buddhist apoha-theorists above all) deny; the karika catalogues without defending it

Verse 2.124.translation: An action enjoined by word is never seen apart from employment; the employment, however, arising-in-consequence, is understood as the word's meaning.
Verse 2.124.commentary: The boundary-instrument at the verb — where Mimamsa lives. Kriya sabda-codita, action word-enjoined ('let him sacrifice'), is never seen vina prayogena, apart from *employment*: an enjoined action exists only as deployed — by an agent, with means, in performance; the bare act-type never walks alone. So what does the verb mean? The doctrine catalogued: the prayoga, the employment — arising anunispadi, in-consequence (the act-type cannot be hosted without it, 2.123's structure at the verb) — *is understood as* the word's meaning: comprehension takes the verb to mean the deployed act, agent-and-means included, because that is the only form in which action is cognizable. The placement is the inverse of 2.122-123: there the entailed was excluded from meaning (individual, shape); here the entailed is *absorbed into* meaning (employment) — the catalogue exhibiting, by design, that the expressed/entailed boundary migrates doctrine by doctrine. The Mimamsa resonance is loud: bhavana, the verb's efficacy-toward-being, with its trio of what-whereby-how, is exactly an employment-semantics of the verb — and 2.125 next catalogues its restriction.
Verse 2.124.contested: we read prayoga as 'employment/deployment' (the act as performed); the word also means 'usage' (the linguistic employment), yielding 'usage is understood as meaning' — a use-theory entry in the catalogue; the kriya-context favors the performative sense

Verse 2.125.translation: But the employments that are fixed, and the means that is fixed — of these, expressibility by the word is followed by others.
Verse 2.125.commentary: The employment-semantics restricted to its lawful domain. Not every deployment enters the verb's meaning — only niyatah prayogah, the *fixed* employments, and the niyatam sadhanam, the *fixed* means: what the injunction's own economy determines (the rite's constitutive performances, its textually-settled instruments — 2.47's niyama, 2.64-70's obtained-and-specified strata). Of *these*, aparaih anugamyate, others hold, sabda-abhidheyatvam — expressibility by the word: the verb means its act *cum* its fixed circumstantials; the variable ones (this priest, that morning) stay outside as mere hosting (2.123). The restriction rescues 2.124 from absurdity (else 'sacrifice' would mean every accident of every performance) and completes a small system: meaning = act-type + textually-fixed frame; entailment = whatever performance adds. It is the exegete's sruti/linga stratigraphy (2.73-74) reappearing as verb-semantics — and its lesson for the catalogue is the constant one: every workable denotation-doctrine ends by indexing meaning to *fixity*, to what the utterance-economy settles — fixity being precisely what the undivided vakyartha distributes (2.88), not what word-atoms possess.

Verse 2.128.translation: When, through the connection 'that is this', the word's form is made one with the meaning — that word they declare to be abhijalpa, identifying-speech.
Verse 2.128.commentary: The promised definition, and one of the kanda's psychological gems. Take the recognition-structure so 'yam iti abhisambandhat — 'that is this' (2.40's own formula: the incoming identified with the possessed): when by it the sabdasya rupam, the word's form, ekikrtam arthena, is *made one with* the meaning — when 'cow' and cow have fused in the speaker's cognition so thoroughly that the word is transparently its thing, thing wordlike, word thinglike — tam abhijalpam pracaksate: that fused condition of the word is abhijalpa. The definition names ordinary semantic consciousness: the mature speaker does not experience word and meaning as paired items (only the analyst, and the learner mid-drill, do); he experiences *one* — the meant-word, the worded-thing (1.50's double shining, lived as fusion; the identification samjna/consciousness pun of 1.126 enacted). The abhijalpa-doctrine of 2.127 then reads: what a word expresses is itself-in-fusion — meaning as the word's achieved transparency. And the fusion explains the catalogue's whole disarray (2.129-130 draw exactly this): because word and meaning present as one, every school's boundary-dispute (which side owns what) is a quarrel over joints that lived speech does not exhibit — the joints appear only under vivaksa-driven analysis, which is where the next verse takes the ledger.
Verse 2.128.contested: none of substance in the definition; its reach — whether abhijalpa names a special condition (citation-like) or ordinary comprehension as such — divides readers, and we take the ordinary-condition reading with the movement's drift

Verse 2.129-130.translation: (129) Since their non-separate meaningfulness is unswerving by established currency, some one form, somewhere, stands as principal; (130) in the world, the word operates having assumed the form of the meaning; in the science, however, the two-formedness is divided out according to intention.
Verse 2.129-130.commentary: The fusion-doctrine made stratigraphic, and the catalogue's quarrels dissolved in two strokes. (129): tayoh aprthag-arthatve — word and meaning being non-separately meaningful (the abhijalpa-fusion), and that non-separation avyabhicarin, unswerving, secured by rudhi, established currency (2.128's 'that is this' hardened by usage, 1.128) — still, kimcid eva kvacid rupam pradhanyena avatisthate: some one form, in some context, stands as *principal* — now the thing-face (ordinary talk), now the word-face (quotation, grammar): salience rotates within the fusion without breaking it. (130) then draws the two-register conclusion: loke — in the world — the word operates artha-rupatam pratipannah, *having assumed the meaning's form*: transparent, self-effacing (1.54's unnoticed instrument; the world sees through words to things). Sastre tu — in the science — ubhaya-rupatvam pravibhaktam vivaksaya: the *two*-formedness is divided out, by intention: the analyst's vivaksa splits the fusion, holds word-face and thing-face apart, and works the seam (svam rupam, 1.59-68; the grahana-craft of 2.98-100). Between loka and sastra the whole catalogue is adjudicated: every doctrine surveyed was a sastra-posture — a way of splitting for a purpose — while lived meaning is the unsplit fusion; the boundary of expressed and entailed (2.121) is drawn by vivaksa, wherever the analysis needs it, and *that is all the drawing there is*.
Verse 2.129-130.contested: 129's syntax is dense (aprthag-arthatve rudher avyabhicarini as locative frame) and our construal follows the movement; some take rudhi as subject ('currency being unswerving in their non-separation'). The loka/sastra division of 130 is textually pristine and is the passage's yield

Verse 2.131.translation: Whether from incapacity or from omnipotence, the fixed construction of action and the rest, for one single meaning, is constructed by words alone.
Verse 2.131.commentary: The ledger's radical closing entry before the perception-turn: even the categories are wordwrought. The parikalpana — the construction of kriya-adi, action-agent-instrument-and-the-rest (the karakas, the very architecture of 2.48): for ekasya arthasya, one single meaning (the undivided vakyartha — one event, say), a *fixed* construal into doer-doing-done is laid down — sabdaih eva prakalpita, constructed *by words alone*. The event itself is one; that it comes carved as agent-acting-on-patient is the contribution of the sentence-form that presents it (active or passive, this the karta, that the karman — carvings the same event tolerates interchangeably). And the alternative etiologies, offered with a shrug: asakteh — from *incapacity*: language cannot present the undivided except through role-fictions (the mind's part-scaffolding, 1.85, 2.13); sarva-sakteh va — or from *omnipotence*: the word-principle (whose sakti binds the universe, 1.118) freely articulates the one as many. Weakness or sovereignty — the two readings of vivarta itself (1.1's contested edge) — and Bhartrhari declines to choose here as there. Either way the theorem stands: the categorial grid — action, agent, instrument, the furniture of every sastra including his own — is of language, not read off the world; Kanda III's samuddesas (padartha by padartha) will spend six hundred verses honoring exactly this theorem: each category examined *as what words posit*.
Verse 2.131.contested: asakteh/sarva-sakteh we read as genuine alternatives (incapacity of speakers/language, or omnipotence of the word-power); some assign the first to language and the second to the meaning — the theorem (categories as word-constructions) is unaffected

Verse 2.132.translation: Or: the meaning that is the object of cognition, fastened to the external thing — that, known as the external thing, is by some held to be the word's meaning.
Verse 2.132.commentary: The catalogue's epistemological entry — the doctrine that meanings are *mental contents mistaken for things*. The artha that words convey is buddhi-visaya, an object *of cognition* — a content in the mind — bahya-vastu-nibandhana, fastened to (occasioned by, answerable to) the external thing; and it is jnatah bahya-vastu iti — *known as* the external thing: comprehension systematically takes the inner content for the outer object. What 'cow' gives you is a cognition-shape, taken as the beast. The doctrine (kaiscid isyate — the Buddhist resonance is unmistakable: meaning as buddhi-pratibhasa, the apoha-tradition's ancestor) is catalogued without polemic because Bhartrhari needs its insight while rejecting its frame: that comprehension traffics in cognition-side contents is his own teaching (2.18's siddhi in comprehenders; 2.123's hosting; pratibha ahead) — that this convicts meaning of *error* (mistaking inner for outer) presupposes the very inner/outer ledger his word-monism dissolves (2.31: word and meaning two faces of one; 1.129: the 'external' circle itself word-built). The next verse presses the entry to its idealist limit — and there the kanda's own metaphysics looks back at the objector.
Verse 2.132.contested: the doctrine's attribution (Buddhist vijnana-leaning strands, or a grammarian's own buddhyartha-view) is the classical question; the karika's kaiscit preserves the ambiguity and we do too

Verse 2.133.translation: Those that appear in cognition, possessed of form, knowable, fastened to distinct memory — (so far as they so appear); but beyond that, it is mere awareness, otherwise.
Verse 2.133.commentary: The idealist limit, stated with a precision that leaves the catalogue behind. Whatever presents — the akaravantah, the formed ones: samvedyah, knowable contents, vyakta-smrti-nibandhanah, fastened to distinct memory (recognizable, re-identifiable — the whole apparatus of stable objects) — ye pratyavabhasante: they *appear*, and their being formed-knowable-memorable is exactly their manner of appearing. Atah anyatha: beyond that — behind, beneath the appearing — samvin-matram: *mere awareness*, awareness-only, unformed, unfastened. The verse's economy is severe: objecthood analyzed without remainder into appearance-structure; what is not appearance-structure is bare samvit. Whether this is the catalogued opponent's terminus (vijnana-vada naming itself) or Bhartrhari's own floor showing through (the samvit here and the sabda-tattva of 1.1 differing, on his account, in name only — awareness whose essence is word, 1.126) is genuinely undecidable and probably meant to be: at the limit, the grammarian's word-monism and the idealist's awareness-monism face each other as mirror-doctrines, quarreling over whether the ultimate light is verbal — the one dispute (1.123-124 already staked it) neither can cede. The catalogue ends here, on its deepest entry; 2.134 steps back into daylight with the observation that dissolves catalogues as such.
Verse 2.133.contested: the verse's voice — opponent's conclusion or author's floor — is the crux stated above; the transcription's pratyavabhasanta we read as present middle. Samvin-matram is the only occurrence of the term in the kanda and its weight is accordingly disputed

Verse 2.134.translation: As the sense-faculty, coming into contact, is a displayer according to its variety, just so from the word the comprehension of the meaning is manifold.
Verse 2.134.commentary: The daylight-observation that adjudicates the whole catalogue: *display varies with the displayer*. The indriya, meeting its object, upadarsakam vaicitryena — displays according to its own variety: the same lamp shows differently to the keen eye and the dim, the jaundiced and the clear (2.105's maxim, now aimed at semantics); no one concludes the lamp is many. Tathaiva sabdat — just so from the word: pratipattir anekadha, comprehension is *manifold* — the same utterance waking richer and poorer, thicker and thinner uptakes per hearer (the child, the expert, the exegete, the provincial of 2.91). The application to the catalogue is immediate and lethal: each denotation-doctrine (2.121-133) canonized *one uptake-profile* — the genus-grasper's, the shape-hoster's, the employment-reader's, the idealist's — and legislated it as 'the word's meaning'. But uptake-profiles are receiver-facts (2.123's hosting, 2.135 next: division per own-cognition); the word, like the lamp, is one, and the doctrines were a physiology of hearers mistaken for a semantics of words. What remains constant under all the variety — the one thing every uptake is an uptake *of* — is the undivided vakyartha; and the flash in which any hearer, at his own grain, seizes it whole now finally receives its name (2.143: pratibha).

Verse 2.135.translation: Set going by the speaker in one way entirely, among differing comprehenders the word's meaning is divided out, each according to his own cognition.
Verse 2.135.commentary: The catalogue's verdict completed on the speaker's side, and semantics resituated once for all between two minds. Vaktra anyathaiva prakrantah: launched by the speaker *in one way entirely* — the utterance leaves as one determinate act, the speaker's vivaksa whole in it (1.155's criterion; 2.130's intention). Bhinnesu pratipattrsu: among differing comprehenders it sva-pratyaya-anusarena pravibhajyate — is divided out, each according to his own cognition: every hearer carves the one launch at his own grain, with his own furnishing (2.123), his own training (2.117), his own purposes — the manifold of 2.134 given its mechanism. Between the one launching and the many carvings, where does 'the meaning' live? The kanda's whole architecture answers: in neither ledger as the doctrines kept it — not in word-atoms (dissolved), not in hearer-constructions (variable), but in the undivided act that launching and uptake both answer to: the vakyartha as the invariant of communication, seized whole (never carved the same way twice) by the flash each comprehension is. The two-mind picture also quietly prices error and depth: uptake can fall short of launch (the mudha of 2.91), exceed it (the exegete's construals), or meet it — and meeting it is not matching carvings but sharing the whole: the samepratibha waked. The stage for 2.143 is fully set; 2.136-142 first generalize the receiver-relativity to *seeing itself*, so that when pratibha is named it will be named for perception and speech alike.
Verse 2.135.contested: anyathaiva ('in one way entirely / quite otherwise') is pointed: some read it as 'otherwise (than the hearers take it)' — launch and uptake never coinciding; our reading ('in one determinate way') keeps the invariant the movement needs, and the two shade together in any case

Verse 2.136.translation: Even regarding a single visible object, seeing divides, each separately; and with the passage of time, even the same one sees it otherwise again.
Verse 2.136.commentary: Receiver-relativity generalized to perception — the first of the movement's two supports (2.137 gives the word-side) for reading comprehension-variety as unalarming. Ekasminn api drsye 'rthe: one visible thing, publicly there. Darsanam bhidyate prthak: the seeings divide, observer by observer — the painter, the timber-merchant and the botanist before one tree; the lover and the anatomist before one face. Kalantarena ca eko 'pi: and across time even the *same* seer — tam pasyaty anyatha punah: sees it otherwise again; yesterday's tree is not today's to the same eyes grown older. If seeing itself — the pramana of pramanas — is thus per-seer and per-time without anyone doubting the tree's unity, then comprehension's per-hearer variety (2.134-135) convicts language of nothing: it exhibits the common condition of all uptake of the one by the many. The verse is 2.105's maxim (difference of grasping certifies no difference in the grasped) turned from defense into framework — and it prepares the movement's harder edge (2.138-141): if all seeing is perspectival, the authority of *any* seeing, including perception's, needs auditing — which the seers' case (2.139) and the sky's false floor (2.140) then conduct.

Verse 2.137.translation: And of even a single word, by the fixed occasioning conditions, the meaning is constructed manifoldly — by one (hearer) and by many.
Verse 2.137.commentary: The word-side support, completing the pair. Ekasya api sabdasya: one word, self-same. Nimittaih avasthitaih — by the occasioning conditions, themselves *fixed* (the contexts, constructions, employments: 2.148's nimitta-bheda, the settled apparatus of use) — arthah bahudha parikalpyate: the meaning is constructed manifoldly — ekena bahubhis ca: by one hearer (across occasions, moods, ages: 2.136's diachrony imported) and by many (across the room: 2.135's synchrony). Note the care: the nimittas are avasthita, *fixed* — the variety is not anarchy; construals are occasioned, rule-shadowed, answerable (the same fixities the exegete's science maps, 2.64-88) — and yet within the fixities, parikalpyate: construction, per mind, irreducibly. Law without uniformity: the shape of every practice the kanda has audited (grammar, exegesis, perception) and now of meaning itself. With both supports standing — seeing divides (2.136), construal divides under fixed occasions (2.137) — the movement can draw its severe epistemological conclusions: what *never* divides is not given to ordinary seers and speakers at all (2.138-139), the given teems with false floors (2.140), and the wise must therefore audit even perception by reason (2.141) while leaving the world's working namings undisturbed (2.142). Then, against that chastened background, the one positive absolute the kanda will assert: the flash (2.143).

Verse 2.138-139.translation: (138) Therefore, for those who have not seen reality, seeing and speech alike are at fault, full of traps, forever unsettled; (139) and the seers' vision, which is somewhat settled in reality — by it there is no worldly practice, nor is it fastened to words.
Verse 2.138-139.commentary: The movement's severe theorem: between fallible practice and settled vision there is no overlap. (138): for adrsta-tattvanam, those who have not seen reality — all of us in the ordinary case — both darsana and vacana, seeing and saying, are sa-aparadham, at fault, bahu-cchalam, full of traps (chala: the debater's snare — our very perceptions and phrasings bait us), nityam eva anavasthitam: *forever unsettled* — the per-seer, per-time, per-hearer divisions of 2.136-137 now pronounced as chronic condition, not occasional noise. (139): and the rsis' darsana — which is kimcid avasthitam tattve, somewhat settled in reality (the vision-knowledge of 1.37-38, its authority intact) — na tena vyavaharah asti: by it *no worldly practice runs* — the settled seeing does not transact, buy, teach, or litigate; and na tat sabda-nibandhanam: nor is it fastened to words — what the seer sees whole does not parcel into speech (1.145's dream-mode showing; the param padam beyond the three, 1.142). The theorem cuts both ways with equal edge: practice cannot borrow vision's settledness (the market runs on trapped seeing and unsettled saying — and runs); vision cannot fund practice (settled, it falls silent). Between them stands everything the two kandas have built: language as the unsettled-but-lawful medium (2.137) in which unseeing beings transact — redeemable not by importing the seers' silence but by the discipline of the word itself (1.14, 1.131). 2.140 next gives unsettled seeing its emblem; 2.141-142 give the wise their protocol for living in it.
Verse 2.138-139.contested: kimcid ('somewhat') in 139 is a studied qualification — even the rsis' vision is only *somewhat* settled, or settled *in some respect*; readers who take it as 'a certain vision, settled' remove the hedge; we keep it — the theorem is harsher and more Bhartrharian with it

Verse 2.140.translation: The sky is seen as if it had a floor; the firefly, like a fire-carrier; and there is no floor in the sky at all, nor is the firefly fire.
Verse 2.140.commentary: The emblem: perception's confident deliverances of what is not there. Talavat drsyate vyoma — the sky *looks floored*: the vault, the dome, the blue ceiling every eye reports and no sky possesses; khadyotah havyavad iva — the firefly like a fire-bearer: a spark, to all seeing, and no fire at all. Na eva ca asti talam vyomni, na khadyotah hutasanah: flatly — no floor, no fire. The examples are chosen as *universal and incorrigible* illusions: not the sick eye's jaundice (2.105) or the fool's fancy (2.91) but percepts every healthy observer shares and no correction dispels — one still *sees* the dome after learning there is none. Perception at its best, unanimous and stable, can thus be false to fact: unanimity is no truth-mark, stability no floor (the pun is the verse's own). This is 1.129's alata-cakra radicalized — there, construction where nothing was; here, construction *canonized in every normal seeing* — and it discharges the movement's last debt: if even agreed perception needs auditing, the variety of comprehension (2.134-137) was never the scandal; the scandal would be trusting any uptake, however shared, without the yukti that 2.141 now prescribes.
Verse 2.140.contested: none; the verse is limpid and the tradition quotes it widely — its work is emblematic, and the floor/settledness pun (tala/avasthita) may be heard or not without loss

Verse 2.141.translation: Therefore the wise man should examine even a perceptible object by reasoning; he should not posit the object seen merely on the authority of seeing.
Verse 2.141.commentary: The protocol, drawn with tasmat-force from the emblem. Pratyaksam api artham — even the *perceptible*, even what stands before the eyes — vidvan ikseta yuktitah: the wise man should examine by reasoning; na darsanasya pramanyat drsyam artham prakalpayet: he should not posit the seen object on seeing's authority alone — the dome and the firefly's fire being exactly what such positing yields. Mark the placement of this rationalism: Kanda I demoted tarka against agama (1.30-42: reasoning cannot found dharma); here tarka is *promoted* against unaudited perception — and the two rulings are one epistemology, not a reversal: reasoning is corrective, never foundational (1.135-137: the eye for the sightless, sovereign nowhere, employed everywhere) — it audits the given (perceptual or textual) without replacing it. The verse's quiet radicalism is its scope: pratyaksam api — the pramana every school treats as archetype is made a defendant; where 2.140 showed *that* seeing errs incorrigibly, 2.141 draws the duty: conviction is earned by cross-examination, not conferred by vividness. For the kanda's own subject the transfer is immediate: comprehension (speech's 'perception') likewise binds only as audited — the exegete's science (2.64-88) being exactly that audit, institutionalized.
Verse 2.141.contested: none of substance; the verse is the tradition's standing citation for grammarian rationalism, and its harmony with 1.30-42 (corrective vs. foundational tarka) is as we state

Verse 2.142.translation: The naming that worldly speakers, in practice, give to things whose reality cannot be told — the truly wise man should not construct alternatives against it.
Verse 2.142.commentary: The protocol's companion, guarding it from misuse — audit the given, but do not harass the working namings. Things are, in their tattva, asamakhyeya — *untellable*: reality outruns designation (the seers' settled vision itself sabda-free, 2.139; the untellable expertise of 1.35). Yet laukikaih, worldly speakers, vyavahare, in practice, samakhyanam — name them, and the namings *work*: the market's 'cow', the household's 'fire', the whole serviceable taxonomy of the unseeing. Tat prajnah na vikalpayet: against *that*, the truly wise man constructs no alternatives — he does not correct the world's speech toward metaphysical exactitude, does not replace 'the sky's dome' with disquisitions at the well. The pairing with 2.141 is exact and deliberate: examine even the perceived (rigor inward, where conviction is formed); leave the world's namings standing (charity outward, where practice runs) — for the namings never claimed tattva; they claimed serviceability, and by that claim they are true (vyavahara-satya, the working truth the whole system has honored since 1.28's vyavastha). The wise man's silence before working speech is thus not condescension but accuracy about what kind of instrument language is — and it is the exact temper in which Kanda III will examine the categories: each padartha audited as *posited by words for practice*, none arraigned for failing to be the untellable.
Verse 2.142.contested: na vikalpayet we render 'should not construct alternatives against (it)'; the verb also yields 'should not subject it to (skeptical) analysis' — the charity-ruling is the same; note the deliberate echo and inversion of 1.29's nanarthika vyavastha (the sista's codification purposeful) — here the *world's* codification receives the same protection

Verse 2.143.translation: When the meanings have been grasped in their separation, an intuition altogether other is born; that, brought about by the word-meanings, they call the sentence-meaning.
Verse 2.143.commentary: The kanda's summit: the vakyartha named at last, and its name is *pratibha* — the flash. The mechanics first, stated with all the movement's accumulated care: viccheda-grahane arthanam — when the meanings (the padarthas, the scaffold's items) have been grasped *in their separation* (the analyst's and learner's part-wise passage: 1.90's seizures, 2.13's ladder) — pratibha *anyaiva* jayate: an intuition *altogether other* is born — other in kind, not in degree; not the parts' sum, echo, or relational closure but a new indivisible arising (2.55's bhinna-laksana cashed): the understanding itself, the click, the whole dawning at once. Tam vakyartha iti ahuh: *that* they call the sentence-meaning — not a proposition the flash grasps: the flash *is* the meaning, comprehension and content one event (2.31's identity, lived). And the genetic clause, holding both truths without strain: padarthaih upapaditam — *brought about by* the word-meanings: the parts are real causes (upapadita — occasioned, ushered) of what they neither contain nor compose; scaffolding that precipitates the edifice (1.84's ripening; 2.18's per-word re-completion; 2.38's discharged means — every earlier structure converging here). This is the doctrine the opening catalogue promised under 'buddhy-anusamhrti' (2.1) and the whole kanda has been assembling: sentence-meaning as an *event in the comprehender* — indivisible, occasioned, other — the flash in which the trained mind (2.117's abhyasa) meets the launched whole (2.135) and the undivided shows itself through its own dismantled ladder. The verses following will defend its ineffability (it cannot be told to another — only waked), distribute it across the living world (the bird's nest-craft, the beast's know-how: pratibha as the very form of practical intelligence), and ground it finally in the word-essence itself: the flash as the sabda-tattva's self-recognition in a mind — 1.142's sabda-purva yoga arriving at its terminus.
Verse 2.143.contested: anyaiva is the verse's hinge and we press it fully ('altogether other' — the flash as novel indivisible); a softer reading ('quite different [from the padarthas]') is possible but under-reads the kanda's architecture. That pratibha is here *defined* as vakyartha (not merely compared to it) is the tradition's own reading and ours

Verse 2.126ab.translation: Or the aggregate is the expressible — a cumulation free of alternative-construction; or else an unreal commingling is, by some, held to be the word's meaning.
Verse 2.126ab.commentary: The catalogue reaches the composite-doctrines — and, in its second line, crosses a threshold. First: samudayo 'bhidheyah — the *aggregate* is what is expressed: word-meaning as the whole bundle (thing + universal + shape + number...), taken avikalpa-samuccaya, as a cumulation *without* alternative-construction: all-at-once, no either/or among aspects — the maximalist placement of 2.121's boundary (everything the cognition hosts, meaning owns). Then the leap: asatyo va api samsargah — or an *unreal commingling*: the word's meaning is a samsarga, a mixture — of thing and form, referent and concept — that is *asatya*, not real: a constructed composite answering to nothing's actual structure, thrown up by language itself. With that entry the catalogue admits semantic *irrealism*: meanings as useful unrealities — and Bhartrhari's own deep teaching (the vikalpas as bhavana-artifacts, 2.116; the constructed circle of 1.129) stands suddenly among the catalogued, wearing another school's name. The next verse completes the irrealist family and adds its subtlest member.
Verse 2.126ab.contested: the source's numbering staggers here (126ab with 127cd printed together); we follow the evident karika-boundaries. Asatya-samsarga is glossed by some as the Buddhist-leaning entry (meaning as constructed fusion), by others as a grammarian's own limit-doctrine — the catalogue's silence on attribution is likely deliberate NOTE: wisdomlib paginates 2.126-127 as half-verses with a duplicated '2.127cd' label; commentary for each verse is attached to its 'ab' page and covers the full verse.

Verse 2.127ab.translation: Or that real which has the unreal as its adjunct is the word's fastening; or the word itself, having come to the condition of identifying-speech, attains expressibility.
Verse 2.127ab.commentary: The irrealist family completed by its two profoundest members. First: asatya-upadhi yat satyam — the *real with unreal adjunct*: the word fastens (nibandhana) to something genuinely real — but only as dressed in unreal upadhis, the limiting-conditions construction supplies (the satya here inevitably recalling 1.1's Brahman under avidya's upadhis: word-meaning as reality-wearing-constructions, semantics as the microcosm of vivarta). Second, stranger and destined for a future: sabdo va api abhijalpatvam agatah yati vacyatam — or the *word itself*, come to the condition of *abhijalpa*, identifying-speech, attains expressibility: the word means... itself-as-fused-with-its-object; meaning is the word in a special reflexive condition. The term abhijalpa is defined in the very next verse — the catalogue pausing, uniquely, to unpack one of its own entries: a signal of weight. Between them the two doctrines bracket the deepest question the catalogue holds: whether the unreality in meaning belongs to the *object-side* (real thing, constructed dress) or to the *word-side* (the word itself, self-fused, doing duty as its own meaning) — and Bhartrhari's system, which identifies word and meaning at the root (2.31), has stakes in both.
Verse 2.127ab.contested: the source's segmentation of 126-127 is disturbed (halves printed under both numbers); our reconstruction follows the sense-units. Abhijalpa is rare enough that 2.128's definition is the tradition's anchor for it

Verse 2.144.translation: It cannot in any way be told to others as 'this is that'; established by its own operation within each self, it is not describable even by the one who has it.
Verse 2.144.commentary: Pratibha's first mark: ineffability, and of a peculiarly strict kind. It cannot be told to others idam tad iti — under the 'this is that' formula, the very structure of identifying-communication (2.40, 2.128's so 'yam): to convey a content one must pair it with something the hearer already holds, and the flash pairs with nothing — it *is* the pairing-event, not a term in one. Pratyatma-vrtti-siddha: established by its own operation *within each self* — self-certifying, self-locating, irreducibly first-personal. Then the harder clause, which distinguishes this from ordinary privacy: kartrapi na nirupyate — it is not describable *even by its own possessor*. Not merely 'I cannot convey it to you' but 'I cannot state it to myself': the flash is not an inner object one inspects and then reports badly; there is nothing standing at a distance to inspect. This is the exact structure of 1.35's connoisseur — the jeweller who knows and cannot say what he knows, asamakhyeya — now generalized from expertise to *every act of understanding a sentence*. Every comprehension is connoisseurship. Note what this costs and buys: it costs the theory any hope of *displaying* the vakyartha (one can only occasion it in another, by uttering — which is what speech is); it buys the explanation of why every attempt to state a sentence's meaning yields another sentence, needing its own flash, forever.
Verse 2.144.contested: kartra ('by the agent/possessor') we read as the one in whom the pratibha arises; some construe 'even by the speaker' (the utterer cannot describe what he wakes) — both readings make ineffability structural rather than merely practical

Verse 2.145.translation: Unexamined, it effects as it were a close conjoining of the meanings; and, as though all-formed, it operates as their objecthood.
Verse 2.145.commentary: Pratibha's second mark: it does the work that connection-theories invented sambandha to do (2.42-46), and does it without machinery. Upaslesam iva arthanam sa karoti — it effects, as it were, a *close conjoining* of the meanings: in the flash the items cohere — the cow is brought, the agent acts, the parts hold together — precisely the relatedness the abhihitanvaya theorist had to purchase with an imperceptible, non-thing connective (2.46). And avicarita — *unexamined*: the conjoining is not a judgement one makes but a coherence one finds already made; scrutiny arrives afterward, upon a whole already unified. Sarva-rupyam iva — as though *all-formed*, wearing every relevant form at once (the whole complete in each aspect, 2.18) — visayatvena vartate: it operates *as their objecthood* — the flash is not a cognition *of* objects standing apart; it is that in which the meanings have their standing-as-objects at all. Both iva's are exact: the conjoining is real-as-presented, not an assembly performed; the all-formedness is completeness, not a sum of forms. Where the rivals posited relations between semantic atoms, pratibha *is* the relatedness — self-arising, prior to inspection, constituting rather than linking its terms.

Verse 2.146.translation: Whether generated directly by the word, or through the following of realization — in the matter of what-is-to-be-done, no one at all transgresses it.
Verse 2.146.commentary: Pratibha's third mark: it is *practically sovereign*. Two genetic routes are conceded: saksat sabdena janitam — generated directly by the word (the hearer's flash on being spoken to: the kanda's home case); or bhavana-anugamena — through the following of bhavana, realization and cultivation (2.116's process, 2.117's abhyasa). Either origin, one authority: itikartavyatayam — in the domain of what-is-to-be-done (Kanda I's term for practical know-how, 1.121, there declared wholly word-dependent) — na kascid ativartate: *no one transgresses it*. Not 'no one should' — no one *does*: when the flash says what is called for, action follows its dictate; deliberation neither precedes nor overrides it, because deliberation is itself conducted in flashes. This is the doctrine's turn from semantics toward life. Comprehension, know-how and conduct converge on a single phenomenon: the immediate, untellable, self-certifying seizure by which any agent knows what is to be done. The next verses press exactly this convergence outward — first to the whole human world, then, far more radically, to the animal one.

Verse 2.147.translation: The whole world regards it as a means of knowledge; and through it the undertakings even of animals are extended.
Verse 2.147.commentary: The sovereignty of 2.146 given its two-fold evidence. First, epistemic standing: pramanatvena tam lokah sarvah samanupasyati — the *entire* world regards pratibha as a pramana, an authoritative means of knowing: not a private feeling but the thing everyone in fact acts on and treats as knowledge — the schools' formal lists (perception, inference, testimony) notwithstanding, the working epistemology of every agent is the flash. Second, and with a jolt: samarambhah pratayante tirascam api tadvasat — undertakings *even of animals* are extended by its power. Tiryanc — beasts, 'the horizontal ones' — has appeared before (1.126: sentience in all kinds; 2.117: children and animals comprehending), but here it becomes the argument's centre of gravity: if the flash is what animals act by, then pratibha is not an achievement of language-users at all — it is the form of practical intelligence as such, and human sentence-comprehension is one dialect of a capacity running through everything alive. The verses following make good on this with a series of examples remarkable for their observational precision.

Verse 2.148.translation: As, from the ripening of particular substances, powers such as intoxication are seen to arise without effort — so are the intuitions of those who possess them.
Verse 2.148.commentary: The naturalist model for pratibha's arising, chosen with care. Certain substances, on paripaka — ripening, fermentation — develop mada-adi-saktayah, powers such as intoxication: the grape left to itself becomes wine; nobody *makes* the power, and no ingredient contains it. Ayatna-jah: they arise *without effort* — unwilled, unassembled, the outcome of a thing's own maturation under conditions. Pratibhah tadvatam tatha: so with the intuitions of those who have them. Every clause carries doctrine. Ripening: the flash comes of maturation (abhyasa, 2.117; the seed-and-paripaka of 1.84), not of construction. Effortless: it is not produced by the will that awaits it — one cannot decide to understand; understanding dawns (2.145's avicarita). And *emergent*: intoxicating power is not found in the grape's parts, as the vakyartha is not in the padarthas (2.143's anyaiva) — the fermented whole has a capacity its ingredients lack. Bhartrhari here naturalizes the flash without deflating it: pratibha is a real power of a matured being, as wine's potency is of a matured substance — which is precisely why it can belong to birds as well as to brahmins, as the next verses insist.
Verse 2.148.contested: tadvatam ('of those who possess them') is neutral as to the possessors' kind — deliberately, given 2.147's animals and 2.149's cuckoo; some construe 'of those who possess (the requisite ripening)', which tightens the analogy further

Verse 2.149.translation: Who modifies the voice-function of the male cuckoo in spring? By whom are creatures like the spider taught in the making of nests and the like?
Verse 2.149.commentary: Two questions, and their form is the argument. Spring comes; the kokila's song changes — its svara-vrtti, its very voice-function, transformed, on time, without instruction: kah vikurute, *who* does this? The spider and its kin build — kulaya-karane kena siksitah: by *whom* taught? The expected answers are all unavailable. Not imitation (the hatchling spider has watched no one). Not inference (2.34's reasoners, deliberating, could not derive a web). Not agama in the narrow sense (spiders keep no lineage of sistas). Not trial and error (the first web holds). The tradition around these verses reaches for karma and vasana — ripened residues of prior lives — and Bhartrhari's own bhavana-vocabulary (2.146, 2.151) accommodates that. But the verse's philosophical force does not depend on the metaphysics of rebirth: it depends on the *phenomenon*, undeniable and undomesticated by any epistemology of the day — determinate, complex, immediately-known, unlearned competence. Naming it pratibha is less an explanation than a placement: whatever the cuckoo has, the hearer of a sentence has too, and the schools admitting only perception, inference and testimony have no cell for either. A modern reader will recognize the questions as those that eventually produced ethology; Bhartrhari's use of them is to widen the concept of knowing until it is large enough to hold both the web and the word.
Verse 2.149.contested: jantvadayah we take with the tradition as spiders and such web/nest-builders (kulaya covers both); the list is deliberately open — the argument wants the whole class of unlearned crafts

Verse 2.150.translation: In the activities of eating, affection, aversion, flight and the rest — established by kind and by descent — who is the employer, for beasts and birds?
Verse 2.150.commentary: The interrogation widened from craft to the whole repertoire of animal life. Not merely nest-building but the basic modes: ahara, feeding; priti and apadvesa, affection and aversion (the animal's evaluative world: what it goes toward, what it flees); plavana-adi, springing and flight. And the specification that matters: jaty-anvaya-prasiddhasu — *established by kind and by descent*, running true in the lineage, each species with its settled repertoire, transmitted without teaching. Kah prayokta: *who is the employer* — who deploys these activities, sets them going at the right moment toward the right object? The question is put in the grammarian's own idiom: prayoktr is the term for the speaker, the one who employs words (1.130's inner word standing in the prayoktr's self; 1.147's speaker intending gauh). The choice is exact and discloses the design: as the speaker's inner word deploys itself into audible speech, so *something* deploys the beast's inheritance into timely action — and the answer is not a further agent but the pratibha itself, the ripened power (2.148) that is its own employer. Life's know-how and speech's comprehension are one structure at two grains — which is why 2.151 can say, with a straight face, that this too comes from agama.
Verse 2.150.contested: the transcription's apadvesa/plavana we read as aversion and springing-flight; the exact list varies in the manuscripts and nothing turns on its members

Verse 2.151.translation: This arises precisely from tradition, which is followed by realization; but tradition is distinguished according to nearness and remoteness.
Verse 2.151.commentary: The audacious answer to 2.149-150, and the verse where the kanda's naturalism and Kanda I's traditionalism become a single doctrine. Whence the spider's web, the cuckoo's spring song, the beast's fear? Bhavana-anugatad agamad eva jayate: from *agama* — received tradition — followed by bhavana, realization. The claim sounds absurd only if agama is confined to texts. Bhartrhari's move is to widen it: what is handed down and ripened *in a lineage* is agama, whether the vehicle is a recension of the Veda or the inheritance of a species (2.150's jaty-anvaya-prasiddha was already the tell). Then the calibration that makes the widening precise rather than glib: asatti-viprakarsabhyam agamas tu visisyate — tradition is *distinguished by nearness and remoteness*: the brahmin's Veda is proximate agama, recited yesterday, present to consciousness; the spider's is remote — sedimented past all recollection, ripened into the body's own certainty, and for that reason *more* unerring, not less (the web never fails; the recitation may). This is Kanda I's deepest thesis (1.30: even the seers' knowledge is agama-purvaka; nothing is known without inheritance) extended to its limit: *all* competence is inherited and ripened, and the difference between scripture, expertise and instinct is one of distance, not of kind. The six-fold classification follows immediately, and this verse is its ground.
Verse 2.151.contested: reading agama here as 'tradition/inheritance' in the wide sense is required by the context (spiders have no scripture) and is the tradition's own gloss; a narrower reading forces the verse into incoherence, which is itself an argument for the wide one

Verse 2.152.translation: From nature, from conduct, from the practice of yoga, from the unseen, from what is brought about, and from what is conveyed by the distinguished — thus they know intuition to be sixfold.
Verse 2.152.commentary: The classification, and with it the movement's close: pratibha in six kinds, ordered — not accidentally — from the animal to the sage. Svabhava, nature: the cuckoo's song, the spider's web (2.149-150) — flash as the ripened endowment of a kind. Carana, conduct: the flash bred by a way of life, by observance and habit of living. Abhyasa, practice: the jeweller's eye, the grammarian's ear (1.35, 2.117) — expertise. Yoga: the flash of disciplined contemplation, the yogin's seeing (1.37's unclouded mind). Adrsta, the unseen: what karma ripens unaccountably — the sudden knowing no biography explains. And visistopahita, conveyed by the distinguished: the flash *transmitted* by an eminent one, teacher to pupil, seer to hearer — the only member of the six that passes between persons, and it does so by *waking* rather than telling (2.144's ineffability strictly preserved: the master does not describe the flash, he occasions it). Read against 2.151, the six are the spectrum of agama by distance: nature is tradition at its most remote and most certain, transmission from the distinguished its nearest and most fragile, the middle terms marking the intervals. Read against 2.143, the whole is the kanda's answer to its opening question — what is a sentence's meaning? A flash of the sixth kind, waked in a hearer by a speaker; the same flash, at bottom, by which the bird knows its nest. The Vakyakanda's central argument is complete: sentence-meaning is not a thing assembled out of words but an event in a living mind, continuous with all knowing-how, occasioned by speech and ineffable in it.
Verse 2.152.contested: the six members are stable in the tradition though their glosses vary; visistopahita is the contested one — 'conveyed by (contact with) the distinguished' (our reading, making it transmitted pratibha) versus 'endowed by a distinguished cause', which loses the transmission-sense the sequence seems to want

Verse 2.153-154.section: What the Word Does Not Convey
Verse 2.153-154.translation: (153) As the word 'cow' is employed with respect to an object marked out by the substances conjoined with it, and yet is not the illuminator of those particulars — (154) so, though the word operates upon cows and the like which are commingled with shape, colour and limbs, it does not take those in.
Verse 2.153-154.commentary: A new movement opens, and its method is subtractive: to fix what the word conveys by cataloguing what it does not. The object 'cow' is met samyogibhih dravyaih laksite — marked out by the substances conjoined with it: the beast comes with horns, dewlap, tether, field, all present in the encounter, all serviceable for pointing it out. And yet: na tv asau tesam visesanam prakasakah — the word is *not the illuminator of those particulars*. (154) makes the point structural rather than incidental: akara-varna-avayavaih samsrstesu — shape, colour and limbs being *commingled* in every cow one meets (there is no unconfigured cow to encounter) — sabdah pravartamano 'pi na tan angikaroti: the word, though it operates upon such commingled objects, *does not take them in*, does not accept them into what it says. The distinction being drawn is between the object's furniture and the word's cargo: everything the thing has is present to the hearer, and almost none of it is *meant* (2.69's avivaksa, 2.123's inseparable hosting, now made the organizing principle of a whole movement). The verses following (2.155-163) will test the principle at its edges: parts, quantity, measure, the conditions under which usage lapses — mapping precisely how little the word carries and how much the encounter supplies.

Verse 2.155.translation: The word that is employed with respect to a thing distinguished by configuration, colour and the rest — its operation is not found in that thing's limb.
Verse 2.155.commentary: The subtraction's first theorem: word-meaning does not distribute to parts. 'Cow' is employed of a thing that is samsthana-varnadaih visistam — distinguished by configuration and colour: a whole individual, determinate in every respect. Yet na tasya avayave pravrttir upalabhyate: the word's operation is *not found in its limb* — the cow's leg is not a cow, and 'cow' has nothing to say about it. The observation looks trivial and is not: it establishes that denotation is grain-fixed and non-inheriting, the semantic counterpart of 2.68's maxim (a word is not the expressor of everything that exists). If word-meaning flowed to parts, 'cow' would half-mean 'hoof', and every term would leak into its object's mereology. It does not: the word settles at exactly one grain — the grain its employment sets (2.104-106) — and both the whole's furniture (2.153-154) and the parts beneath it lie outside. The theorem also guards the sphota-doctrine's flank: undivided meaning attaching to undivided object, with no partitive echo on either side.

Verse 2.156.translation: Indifferent to number, measure and configuration, the expressor operates — with respect to a drop and to an aggregate alike — in the case of water and the like.
Verse 2.156.commentary: The theorem tested on mass-terms, where it shows most cleanly. 'Salila', water: the word is samkhya-pramana-samsthana-nirapeksah — *indifferent* to number, to measure, to configuration; and it operates equally bindau ca samudaye ca — upon a *drop* and upon an *ocean*. One term, spanning every quantity and shape its stuff can take, with no adjustment and no strain. For the atomist semantics this is awkward (what fixed deposit is common to drop and sea?); for the subtraction-doctrine it is the expected result: quantity, extent, shape are the *encounter's* furniture, never the word's cargo — the drop and the ocean differ in everything except what 'water' says of them. The example also quietly indicts the vyakti-theorist (2.122's rival): if words denoted individuals, 'water' would need an individual, and neither drop nor sea nor the sum of them presents one. What the word settles on is the stuff as such — and 'as such' is precisely the residue left when number, measure and configuration have been subtracted.
Verse 2.156.contested: salilādisu ('water and the like') marks the class of mass-terms without theorizing it; whether Bhartrhari means to distinguish mass from count nouns systematically, or merely to exhibit indifference-to-quantity in a favourable case, is not stated

Verse 2.157.translation: Of that which stands settled in oil and the like, delimited by processing and the rest, one says that a single portion is that very thing — its residing in the parts (is thus admitted).
Verse 2.157.commentary: The counter-case, honestly admitted, and it complicates 2.155's theorem. Take taila, oil, samskara-adi-paricchinne — delimited by processing and such (this pressing, this vessel, this batch: the stuff individuated by its history). Of such a mass one says ekadesam tattvena — a single portion *is that very thing*: any ladleful of the oil is oil, wholly and without remainder — tasya avayava-vartita, its residing in the parts is thereby granted. Mass-terms *do* distribute to their parts, exactly where count-terms (2.155's cow and its leg) do not. Bhartrhari records the asymmetry without smoothing it, and it repays notice: what a word reaches is not fixed by any general rule of mereology but by the *kind of standing* its object has under that employment — divisible stuffs distributing, articulated wholes not. This is the movement's method working honestly: the subtraction (what the word omits) yields different residues for different terms, and the residue is settled by the employment, never by the ontology alone. Later semantics would call this the mass/count distinction and make heavy weather of it; the verse simply lets the two behaviours stand side by side.
Verse 2.157.contested: the syntax is compressed and our construal (oil's portion being oil, hence part-residence granted for such terms) follows the contrast with 2.155-156; some read the verse as reporting an opponent's over-extension of part-residence, to be corrected — the asymmetry it records is real on either reading

Verse 2.158.translation: The naming that is employed as bound up with a particular meaning — on the departure of that meaning, its employment ceases.
Verse 2.158.commentary: The subtraction given its operational test: what a word *really* carries is what its usage cannot survive losing. Yena arthena abhisambaddham abhidhanam prayujyate — the naming is employed as bound to a certain meaning; tad-artha-apagame tasya prayogo vinivartate: that meaning departing, the employment *ceases*. This is the criterion the whole movement needs, and it is a dependency-test of exactly the modern kind: vary the object and watch the word — where usage lapses, meaning was; where usage persists, meaning was not. Lose the cow's colour and 'cow' stands (so colour was never carried, 2.153-154); lose the cowness and 'cow' falls (so cowness was). The test converts the doctrine of avivaksa from a claim about intention into an observable of usage — and 2.159-160 apply it immediately, first to the features that come and go without disturbing the word, then to the one whose loss silences it.

Verse 2.159.translation: But those possible properties which the word does not take within itself when employed — it does not, as a rule, require their presence.
Verse 2.159.commentary: The test's negative half. Sambhavino dharman — the *possible* properties: those a thing of this kind might have, and this one does (the cow's horns, hair, hooves, the colour of the day) — the word, when employed, does *not take them within*: antarniya na prayujyate, they are not carried inside the naming. Hence: tesam sannidhyam niyamena na vyapeksate — the word does not, *as a rule*, require their presence.
