The consequence is exactly the usage-behaviour 2.158 made criterial: a hornless cow is a cow, a shorn one is a cow, the word does not falter — because it never asked. Niyamena, 'as a rule', is precise and not a hedge: these features may be relevant on occasion (a context may make horns matter), but no rule of the word requires them; their bearing is the employment's business, not the term's. What we are watching is the emergence, from usage-observation, of something like the necessary/accidental distinction — arrived at not by metaphysics but by asking which losses stop the mouth.

Verse 2.160.translation: As the word 'cow' is seen (to operate) even where hair, hooves and the like deviate — not so does it operate when the universal is dissociated.
Verse 2.160.commentary: The test's positive half, and the movement's sharpest single stroke. Roma-sapha-adinam vyabhicare 'pi — hair, hooves and the rest *deviating*: absent, atypical, lost — drsyate go-sabdah: 'cow' is still seen in use, unembarrassed. Na tatha jater viprayoge pravartate — *not so* when the jati is dissociated: let cowness go and the word will not follow; no usage survives it. Two features, two fates, one test (2.158): the deviating ones were never carried; the one whose loss silences the word was its whole cargo. This is the jati-doctrine (2.122) not asserted but *derived* — reached by watching where speech breaks — and it is the movement's yield: after everything has been subtracted (the conjoined substances, the shape and colour, the limbs, the number and measure, the possible properties), the residue is the universal, and the residue is the meaning. Vyabhicara, deviation, is the test's technical name and would become the standard tool of Indian logic for isolating the invariable; here it is turned on semantics with complete clarity: meaning is what does not deviate.

Verse 2.161.translation: Difficult it is for anyone in the world to see all the parts; but from certain parts seen, the whole object is inferred.
Verse 2.161.commentary: A pivot from what words carry to how *objects* are given — the movement's second half, on the gap between what is presented and what is taken. Durlabham kasyacil loke sarva-avayava-darsanam: seeing *all* the parts is beyond anyone — no one ever sees the whole cow (the far side, the interior, the moment before and after are never given); perception is always partial. Kaiscit tv avayavair drstaih arthah krtsno 'numiyate: yet from *some* parts seen, the whole object is inferred — and effortlessly, without felt inference, the cow simply *there* in her entirety. The observation is doing two jobs. It secures the earlier subtractions: if the object is never wholly given anyway, the word's indifference to its furniture (2.153-156) is no defect but a fit with the epistemic situation. And it prepares 2.163's principle: comprehension routinely delivers wholes on partial warrant — which is what hearing a word does. The verse belongs with 1.90's part-seizures and 2.90's man-lion: Bhartrhari's persistent interest in how the mind completes what is shown, and how much of the given is in fact supplied.

Verse 2.162.translation: So too, in the case of jasmine, lotus and the like, whose qualities are constant companions of their scent, the determination of the qualities is observed.
Verse 2.162.commentary: The completion-principle exemplified, with the tradition's favourite evidence: scent. The jasmine's fragrance reaches one in the dark; and with it, not fragrance alone but the flower — its whiteness, its form, its whole quality-complex — avadharanam, *determined*, settled, present to the mind. The mechanism: gunanam sahacarinam nitya-sambandhinam — the qualities are constant companions of the scent, invariably linked; so one companion given, the company follows. This is co-occurrence hardened into cognitive delivery: what has never been met apart is never taken apart. The example serves the movement precisely because the delivery is *not* the scent's doing — the scent conveys scent; the flower arrives by the mind's completion along invariable association (2.161's inference-without-inferring, made specific). Transferred to language, as 2.163 immediately transfers it: the word conveys its narrow cargo (the jati, 2.160), and everything else the hearer finds in his comprehension — the shape, the colour, the whole imagined cow — arrives as the scent's company does: by association, not by the word.

Verse 2.163.translation: Therefore, when there is comprehension from the word of the possible meaning, the object whose dissociation has not been seen is understood by way of its being connected.
Verse 2.163.commentary: The movement's conclusion, gathering both halves. Tasmat: from the subtraction (words carry only the non-deviating, 2.158-160) and the completion (minds deliver wholes from parts by invariable association, 2.161-162) it follows: sambhavino arthasya sabdat sampratyaye sati — when the word has conveyed its possible meaning — adrsta-viprayogo 'rthah sambandhitvena gamyate: the object *whose dissociation has never been seen* is understood by way of *being connected*. Two channels, cleanly separated. The word gives what it gives: the invariant (2.160). Everything else in the hearer's comprehension — the cow's shape, her four legs, the world of default furnishing — comes by sambandhitva: it rides in because it has never been met apart, exactly as the flower rides in on its scent. Note the criterion's elegance: adrsta-viprayoga, 'not seen dissociated' — not *impossible* to dissociate, merely never yet met apart; the completion is defeasible, an association-effect, not an entailment (the hornless cow of 2.159 defeats it and the word survives). The movement thus ends with a two-channel semantics that a modern reader can recognize: strict semantic content, thin and invariant; and a generous pragmatic penumbra supplied by the hearer's world-knowledge. The kanda's next topic — number and the case-endings — is the natural test of exactly this boundary.
Verse 2.163.contested: sambhavinah arthasya we take as 'the possible meaning' — that which the word can convey (recalling 2.159's sambhavino dharmah); some construe 'the existent object'. The two-channel result is unaffected

Verse 2.164-165.translation: (164) The case-endings might be either expressive or manifesting of duality and the rest; or the aggregate might be the expressor of a numbered object. (165) Or, without any expression of number, they say that certain words such as 'go' convey, by difference of their own form, objects already accompanied by difference of number.
Verse 2.164-165.commentary: Number — the perfect test of the two-channel doctrine (2.163), since number is precisely a determination that sometimes seems said and sometimes seems merely there. Three accounts are canvassed. (i) The vibhaktis, the case-endings, are vacikah — *expressive* of duality and the rest: 'gavau' says two, the dual ending denoting the number outright. (ii) They are dyotikah — *manifesting*: the ending does not denote number but lights up a number already in the meaning — the illuminator/expressor distinction (Kanda I's whole apparatus) applied to inflection, and destined for a long career in Indian semantics (the dyotaka-doctrine would become the standard account of particles). (iii) The samudaya, the aggregate — stem-plus-ending taken as one unit — is the expressor of the numbered object: no division of labour at all, the whole form denoting the whole content (2.36's asvakarna-principle at the level of inflection). Then (165) the fourth and strangest: without any number-expression whatever, some words (the tradition instances 'go') convey numbered objects sva-rupa-bhedena — by *difference of their own form*: the form-difference simply *is* the number-difference, nothing denoting anything. The four exhaust the logical space, and the exhaustiveness is the point: number's status is genuinely undecidable by inspection, which is what a two-channel semantics predicts for exactly such borderline determinations.
Verse 2.164-165.contested: 165's kamscid ahur gavadayah is grammatically awkward and our construal (certain words convey number by their own form-difference) follows the sense; the verse has also been read as reporting that certain *theorists* say this. Either way the fourth option is a form-difference account without denotation

Verse 2.166.translation: Those words which have eternal connection, whose capacities are known in isolation — their meaning is divided out by agreement and difference.
Verse 2.166.commentary: The method for adjudicating 2.164-165, and it is the method of 2.12: distributional analysis. Where words are nitya-sambandha — eternally connected with their meanings (Kanda I's thesis, 1.23) — and vaiveke jnata-saktayah — their capacities *known in isolation*, established independently — then anvaya-vyatirekabhyam tesam artho vibhajyate: their meaning is divided out by *agreement and difference*: covary the form and watch the meaning; where they track, credit a unit; where they do not, do not. The verse is doing careful methodological work. It states the *conditions* under which the distributional method may be trusted — independently-known capacities, stable connection — and thereby marks that the method is not universally applicable: it is an instrument with a domain, not a metaphysics. This matters immediately, because 2.167 will exhibit exactly the cases where the conditions fail, and the whole point of the pair is that the analyst's chief tool has an edge past which it does not cut.

Verse 2.167.translation: And where the construing of those two can be made without deviation, there is a fixed rule; but there is no such fixed rule in the case of nut, sap and the like.
Verse 2.167.commentary: The method's limit, exhibited in the grammar's own machinery. Where anvaya and vyatireka can be applied avyabhicarena — without deviation, tracking perfectly — niyamah, a fixed rule obtains: the segmentation is secure, the unit real (as 'pac-' is, recurring with cooking wherever it recurs). But: na tv evam niyamo nut-sab-adisu — no such fixity for nut, sap and their kind. These are Panini's augments and technical elements — nut (the n-augment), sap (the class-marker of the first conjugation) — formatives that appear and vanish by rule with *no* constant meaning tracking them: sap marks a conjugation class and means nothing at all. The method finds them by distribution and finds no meaning to credit them with. So the analyst's own inventory contains units that are real *as units* and empty *as meanings* — 2.12's anarthaka parts (the '-bha' of vrsabha) now met not in the lexicon's oddities but at the heart of the derivational system. The moral for 2.164's dispute: one cannot settle whether the dual ending *denotes* two merely by noting that it covaries with twoness — covariance certifies a unit, never a meaning; sap covaries with everything in its class and means nothing. And the moral for the kanda: the tool that generates padarthas cannot, by itself, tell which of them are semantic — which is precisely why the meanings had to be sought at the whole (2.143).
Verse 2.167.contested: the transcription's nut-sab-adisu we read as the grammatical augments/markers nut and sap; some editions read differently, but the class intended — meaningless formatives isolable by distribution — is not in doubt

Verse 2.168.translation: When direct expression is possible, indication is not to be assumed; for the capacities of words, in combination, are fixed relatively.
Verse 2.168.commentary: Two maxims, the second considerably deeper than the first. (i) Sambhave va abhidhanasya laksanam tu na kalpyate: where abhidhana — direct expression — is available, do not posit laksana, indication (secondary or transferred meaning). This is the standard interpretive economy of the tradition (do not reach for metaphor while the literal serves), and it will govern the coming discussion of secondary meaning. (ii) The ground offered for it, and it is the movement's most compressed insight: apeksikyah hi samsarge niyatah sabda-saktayah — the capacities of words, *in combination*, are fixed *relatively*. Sakti — the word's power, the thing every semantics has been trying to locate — is here declared apeksiki: relational, index-bound, determinate only against the combination it stands in. Not: each word has a fixed power and combination merely displays it. Rather: powers are *fixed by* relation — the same word carrying different capacity in different company, and carrying determinate capacity only *because* of company (2.17's particular-words looking general in citation; 2.104's capacity-assumption in the stream). The economy-maxim then follows naturally: if capacity is settled relationally, then in any given combination the capacity simply *is* what the combination fixes — and there is no room to import laksana while the relationally-fixed abhidhana is doing the work.

Verse 2.169.translation: No syntactic connection of meaning is seen in the case of 'kupa', 'supa', 'yupa'; therefore it is understood that the aggregate alone expresses a different meaning.
Verse 2.169.commentary: The relational-capacity doctrine of 2.168 exhibited in a case that has become famous. Kupa (well), supa (broth), yupa (sacrificial post) — three words sharing '-upa', differing in one phoneme, and their meanings share *nothing*: na anvayah arthasya drsyate, no syntactic or semantic connection whatever is seen. The rhyme is total and the sense is null. Hence: sanghatasya eva arthantara-vacitvam gamyate — it is the *aggregate alone*, the whole word, that expresses its wholly different meaning. The example does the work of a hundred arguments. Distributional method (2.166) would find '-upa' as a recurrent segment; the segment means nothing; the three words are not variations on a theme but three unrelated wholes that happen to sound alike. Phonetic overlap is thus proved semantically inert — which is 2.11's point (word-parts resembling other words) driven home with maximum economy, and which vindicates 2.36's generalization (comprehension is whole-to-whole everywhere, the transparent compound differing from the opaque only in inviting analysis). Kupa-supa-yupa is the kanda's emblem of the fact that meaning does not live in the pieces.

Verse 2.170.translation: Explanations differ in the operations of deriving words; among the many possible meanings, some one occasion is accepted.
Verse 2.170.commentary: Etymology entered — and immediately relativized. Anvakhyanani bhidyante sabda-vyutpatti-karmasu: *explanations differ* in the business of deriving words; the etymologist's craft (the nirukta-tradition's whole enterprise) does not converge — the same word receives incompatible derivations from different authorities, each defensible. And the reason: bahunam sambhave arthanam nimittam kimcid isyate — many meanings being *possible*, some one occasion is *accepted*: the derivation does not discover the word's meaning; it selects one nimitta, one occasioning ground, from among several the form permits. Etymology is thus a choice under-determined by evidence — the analyst picking which possible history to credit, as the exegete picks which analysis to run (2.38's non-fixity of means: 'a fixed rule about means does not necessarily stand'). This is not a complaint. Bhartrhari's tradition prizes nirukta (1.10's angas), and he is not dismissing it; he is placing it — as upaya, a discardable instrument whose value is what it teaches, not what it reveals about the word's real constitution (2.13's prakriya-bheda for the avidvan). The next verses exhibit the multiplicity concretely.
Verse 2.170.contested: nimittam kimcid isyate we read as the etymologist's selection among possible grounds; some construe 'some particular occasion is (what is) desired (by the deriver)' — the under-determination is the point on either reading

Verse 2.171.translation: Vaira, Vasistha, Girisa, and likewise Ekagarika and the rest, have been explained somehow, by some, through confusions of ground and limit.
Verse 2.171.commentary: The multiplicity exhibited, with a rueful precision. Four specimens whose derivations the tradition contests: vaira (enmity — from vira? from a lost base?); vasistha (the seer's name — from vasin, from vasistha as superlative?); girisa (Siva, mountain-dweller — giri-sa, but sa from which root, and why?); ekagarika (a term the tradition glosses variously — 'burglar', 'one who keeps a single house'). Kaiscit kathamcid akhyatah: explained *by some*, *somehow* — the two indefinites doing withering work. And the diagnosis: nimitta-avadhi-sankaraih — by *confusions of ground and limit*: the etymologist mixes up which element is the occasioning ground and where the analysis is to be cut, and by shuffling those two variables generates as many derivations as he pleases. Bhartrhari has isolated exactly the degrees of freedom that make etymology unfalsifiable: choose a base, choose a boundary, and any form yields any sense. The verse is his most economical demonstration that the analyst's tools generate their own results — and it lands with special force here, since the whole kanda's argument has been that the parts analysis finds are the analysis's own postings (2.10, 2.97, 2.167).

Verse 2.172.translation: As the designation of a road by trees, ant-hills and mountains is not incompatible — so with 'cow' and the rest, by different accompanying things.
Verse 2.172.commentary: The movement closes with a simile that redeems what 2.170-171 seemed to demolish. A road is designated by its landmarks: 'the way past the great tree', 'the road by the ant-hill', 'the mountain road' — three designations, three grounds, one road, and aviruddham: *not incompatible*. No one asks which landmark is the road's true name; each picks the road out, from where the speaker stands, by what lies to hand. Tatha gavadinam bhinnaih sahacaribhih: so with 'cow' and the rest, by *different accompanying things* — a word may be occasioned by any of its object's companions (its gait, its lowing, its horns), and rival etymologies are rival landmarks, all serviceable, none constitutive. The simile completes the movement's whole argument with a single image. The word does not carry its object's furniture (2.153-160); the mind supplies the rest by association (2.161-163); the analyst's derivations pick among associations for their occasioning grounds (2.170-171) — and none of this impugns the word, because designation was never a matter of *containing* the thing. It is a matter of *pointing successfully from where one stands*. That the pointing succeeds while every account of how it succeeds diverges is not a scandal; it is the ordinary condition of language, and 2.142 has already told the wise man to leave the world's namings undisturbed.

Verse 2.173.translation: And a designation is made otherwise by those who see difference of state, from the determination of a single portion — as in the case of the kimsuka and the like.
Verse 2.173.commentary: The landmark-simile (2.172) given its human dimension: designators differ because *seers* differ. Avastha-bheda-darsibhih — by those who see a difference of state: the kimsuka tree (the flame-of-the-forest, a stock example precisely because it is a different sight in each season — bare, budded, blazing red) receives its designation ekadesa-avadharanat, from the determination of a *single portion*: whoever names it names it from the aspect he has met. The traveller who saw it in flower and the one who saw it bare have named the same tree by different grounds, and neither has erred. This completes the etymological relativism (2.170-171) by locating its source not in the etymologist's carelessness but in the structure of encounter itself: nobody meets the whole (2.161), everybody names from a portion, and the portions differ. The kimsuka also carries an old resonance the audience would hear — it is the tree of the parable in which several men describe it from their several seasons and each is mocked by the others — which is exactly the moral: the mockery is misplaced.
Verse 2.173.contested: the kimsuka's traditional parable-associations are assumed rather than stated by the karika; the verse needs only that its appearance varies by state and that names are given from states

Verse 2.174.translation: By some, a different derivation has been shown here for 'gauh' — from 'girati', from 'garjati', from 'gam', from 'gavate', or indeed from 'gata'.
Verse 2.174.commentary: The relativism made concrete on the tradition's most-derived word. 'Gauh' — cow — has been referred to: girati, she swallows (the grazing beast); garjati, she bellows (the lowing beast); gam, to go (the wandering beast); gavate, a rare root the etymologists press into service; gata, gone/motion. Five derivations, five grounds, one animal — each a landmark on the road (2.172), each picking the cow out by a real companion of hers (her feeding, her voice, her going). Kaiscid, 'by some': the anonymity is now familiar and is the point — the derivations have authorities, and the authorities do not agree, and the disagreement has never once impeded anyone's use of the word. That is the datum. Etymology is a genuine discipline producing incompatible results and costing nothing, because the word's working never depended on it: derivation reconstructs a possible occasion for a name whose life is entirely in its employment (2.104-106). The verse is the kanda's cheerful demonstration that the search for a word's 'true meaning' in its origin is a search for a landmark, not a foundation.

Verse 2.175.translation: Or the word 'gauh' operates upon cows simply from its own form, as 'gauh'; either everything is derived, or not — and by some it is accepted in both ways.
Verse 2.175.commentary: The alternative to derivation, and then the tradition's characteristic settlement. First: gaur ity eva svarupad va — 'gauh' operates on cows simply *from its own form*, underived, the word standing on itself with no history to lean on (the avyutpanna, the underivable, that Panini's own system concedes for certain forms, 2.37). Then the general question posed in its starkest form: vyutpadyate na va sarvam — is *everything* derived, or nothing? The two schools are the tradition's real ones: the vyutpatti-vadins who hold every word analyzable to root and affix, and the a-vyutpatti-vadins who hold that stems are simply given. And the settlement: kaiscid ca ubhayatha isyate — by some it is accepted *both ways*. Not a compromise but a principled dual-standing: the same word is underived as *used* (its life needing no history) and derived as *taught* (its analysis serving the learner, 2.13). This is 1.74 and 2.38 arriving in etymology — the two directions of description, each valid for its purpose, neither the fact of the matter — and it is why the kanda can honour the nirukta-tradition (2.170's craft) while denying it any authority over meaning.

Verse 2.176.translation: And instruction by way of the general is resorted to in the science for the sake of brevity; as with another universal, its particulars are (still) the conveyors.
Verse 2.176.commentary: A methodological note that will govern the movement on roots and preverbs now beginning. Samanyena upadesah — instruction by way of the *general* — is what the sastra resorts to laghv-artham, *for brevity*: the grammar teaches 'the root pac', 'the affix tin', stating rules over general categories because stating them over every instance would be endless (laghava, economy, is the Paninian science's governing virtue). But the brevity is a device, and the verse says so: jaty-antaravad asyapi visesah pratipadakah — as with any other universal, its *particulars* are the conveyors: what actually conveys, in any utterance, is the particular form in its particular employment; the general is the teacher's shorthand for a family of particulars, not an entity doing semantic work. The point matters for what follows: the grammar will be seen to treat dhatu and upasarga as separate general items, and the whole question (2.180-190) will be whether that separation is more than pedagogical economy. Bhartrhari has answered before asking: instruction is general for brevity's sake; conveyance is always particular.

Verse 2.177.translation: And what has occurred in another meaning, they know as a different base; though its form is alike, it is not carried over to another in established currency.
Verse 2.177.commentary: A principle of lexical identity, drawn from the same soil. Artha-antare yad vrttam — what has occurred with a *different meaning* — tat prakrty-antaram viduh: they know it as a *different base*: sameness of meaning-behaviour, not sameness of shape, individuates a stem. And the consequence: tulya-rupam na tad rudhau anyasmin anusajyate — though the form be alike, it is not carried over into another word's established currency: the identical syllables occurring in a rudhi-word (a conventionalized term, 2.37) do not drag their other life along. This is kupa-supa-yupa (2.169) stated as a rule of the lexicon rather than exhibited as an oddity: homophony is not identity, and the etymologist who reasons from shared shape to shared base has confused a landmark with a road (2.172). The principle also anticipates the movement's central case: if 'pra-' in one verb means one thing and in another means nothing recoverable, then by 2.177 they are not the same item at all — which is precisely the reef on which preverb-semantics will run.
Verse 2.177.contested: vrttam we take as 'has occurred/operated' (the form used with another sense); the compressed syntax also permits 'what has been formed (as a compound) in another meaning', narrowing the rule to vrtti-formations — the individuation-principle survives either way

Verse 2.178.translation: The two roots 'ji' and 'yaj', distinct and fixed to different domains, have been somehow set forth by some as one; for conveying is a various thing.
Verse 2.178.commentary: A specimen of the etymologist's over-reach, chosen for its audacity. The roots ji (to conquer) and yaj (to sacrifice) are bhinnau — *distinct*, niyatau visayantare — each fixed to its own domain, sharing nothing but a formal resemblance that phonological ingenuity can manufacture. And yet kaiscid kathamcid uddistau: by *some*, *somehow*, they have been set forth as one — the derivers, having the two freedoms 2.171 identified (choose a base, choose a boundary), unified what usage keeps forever apart. Bhartrhari's comment is a single wry clause: citram hi pratipadanam — 'for conveying is a various thing', or more bluntly, exposition is a wonderful business. The dryness is the argument. He does not refute the derivation; he lets its existence testify. If the method can identify conquering with sacrificing, the method's outputs are not constraints on meaning — and 2.177 has just given the rule the derivers violated: different meaning-behaviour, different base, whatever the shapes suggest.
Verse 2.178.contested: the identification of the two roots as ji and yaj follows the transcription's aviji-yaji; the specific etymological claim being mocked is not recoverable, and the verse's force is entirely in the mockery

Verse 2.179.translation: And thus one should proceed as with 'bala', 'vaya' and 'jitvari' and the like; in accepting both difference and non-difference, there is no contradiction whatever.
Verse 2.179.commentary: The settlement of 2.175 formalized as a maxim, with a handful of examples the tradition disputes (bala, vaya, jitvari — words whose analyses divide the grammarians). Evam upacaret: proceed thus — accepting bheda-abheda-abhyupagame na virodho 'sti kascana: in accepting *both* difference and non-difference there is *no contradiction whatever*. Two words may be one base and two bases, according to the purpose of the description: one, for the economy of a rule (2.176's samanyena upadesa); two, for the fidelity of a usage-account (2.177's individuation by meaning-behaviour). The maxim is the kanda's constant (1.74, 2.38, 2.58, 2.175) at its most explicit — bhedabheda not as a metaphysical doctrine to be defended but as a *methodological permission* to describe the same material twice, incompatibly, without incoherence, because descriptions answer to purposes and the material answers to neither. It is the exact license the coming analysis of root-and-preverb will need: they are two (the grammar says so, and rules require it) and one (usage says so, and meaning requires it), and the verse has pre-authorized both.

Verse 2.180.translation: The construing of root and preverb as separate, in the science, is for the sake of settling 'at' and the rest; but the root itself is of that very kind.
Verse 2.180.commentary: The movement's thesis, stated at once. In the sastra, dhatu and upasarga — root and preverb ('pra-', 'sam-', 'ud-') — are prthaktvena prakalpanam, construed as *separate* items. Why? Ad-adinam vyavastha-artham: for the sake of settling the augment 'at' and the rest — that is, for strictly formal purposes: the augment a- of the past tense must be positioned (does one say pra-agacchat or pra-a-gacchat?), sandhi must be regulated, accent assigned, and none of that can be stated without treating the preverb as a separable unit. The separation is *rule-driven*, exactly as 2.176 said instruction is general for brevity's sake. And then the counter-claim that governs everything following: dhatur eva tu tadrsah — *the root itself is of that very kind*: what actually conveys is the root already-as-so-qualified, the preverbed root as one meaning-unit; the separation that the grammar's formal operations require does not correspond to any division in the meaning. Two items for the rule-writer; one for the speaker. It is 2.10's apoddhara-doctrine arriving at the innermost joint of the word — and the reason it must arrive here is that if analysis *anywhere* found a real semantic seam, this is where it would be.

Verse 2.181.translation: For thus, a rule is taught for 'samgramayati' as having its preverb; and such particular actions are undertaken by whole complexes.
Verse 2.181.commentary: The evidence for 2.180, from the grammar's own practice. Samgramayati — 'he fights, wages battle': the form is derived from samgrama, itself sam + grama, and the grammar teaches its rules sopasargat — *with its preverb*: the sastra cannot state the derivation by taking the root alone and adding the preverb's contribution; it must treat the preverbed complex as the base from the start. The grammarian's own procedure thus concedes what 2.180 claimed: at the point where a rule must actually apply, the unit is the complex. And the generalization: kriya-visesah sanghataih prakramyante tathavidhah — such particular actions are undertaken *by whole complexes*: the specific action a verb names (waging-battle, not going-together-with-a-village) is delivered by the sanghata, the complex as a whole, not composed from separable donations. Note that 'wage battle' is precisely *not* recoverable from sam + grama by any calculus — the meaning is the complex's own (2.169's kupa-supa-yupa at the verbal root). The grammar keeps root and preverb apart for the augment's sake (2.180) and keeps them together for the rule's sake here: the two treatments are 2.179's licensed double description, and only the second tracks meaning.

Verse 2.182.translation: Thus the operations relating to root and preverb are internal; the action, being of that very kind, comes into connection with the means.
Verse 2.182.commentary: The doctrine given its technical seat. Karyanam antarangatvam evam dhatupasargayoh — the operations concerning root and preverb are *antaranga*, 'internal': the Paninian term for rules that apply at the innermost layer, before the outer ones get a chance (antaranga rules take precedence over bahiranga, and the metarule governing this is among the system's most consequential). Bhartrhari's use is exact and shrewd: the preverb's union with its root happens *inside*, in the derivation's core, complete before the word meets anything else. Hence: sadhanaih yati sambandham tathabhuta eva sa kriya — the action, being *already of that kind* (preverbed, particular, its sense settled), then comes into connection with the means, the karakas — the agent, instrument, object, everything the sentence supplies. The order is the argument. By the time the verb reaches the sentence, there is no root-plus-preverb to be summed; there is one action-meaning, which the sentence then furnishes with participants (2.48's architecture presupposes exactly this: the verb arrives whole and the nouns orbit *it*). The grammar's own layering of rules thus certifies the semantics: what is settled antaranga is settled before semantics at sentence-scale begins.
Verse 2.182.contested: antarangatva is the technical priority-notion and we render it 'internal'; whether Bhartrhari invokes the full paribhasa-machinery or the plainer sense ('these operations belong to the inner constitution') is undecidable, and the argument works on the plainer sense alone

Verse 2.183.translation: Being accomplished among things fit for employment, the object to be split is (further) specified; and prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise at all.
Verse 2.183.commentary: A pair of claims about how verbal meaning comes to be determinate. First: prayogarhesu siddhah san bhettavyo 'rtho visisyate — the object-to-be-split (bhettavya: the standard example of a verb's patient, 'what is to be split') is *already accomplished* among things fit for employment, and is then further specified: the log exists before the splitting is spoken of; the verb's participants are drawn from a world of usable things and then narrowed by the utterance. Second, and it is the striking one: prak ca sadhana-sambandhat kriya naiva upajayate — *prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise at all*. There is no bare action waiting to be supplied with participants. 'Splitting' as such, unagented, unpatiented, uninstrumented, is not a meaning that exists and then gets furnished; the action-meaning *comes into being* with its karaka-connections. Read against 2.182, the picture is precise: the preverbed root arrives with its *kind* fixed internally (waging-battle, not going-with-village), and its *being an action at all* is constituted in the sentence, by the means-connection. Kind from within, existence from without — which is why the verb is the sentence's centre (2.48) and yet not the sentence's meaning (2.1's first view, refused).
Verse 2.183.contested: bhettavyo 'rthah we take as the illustrative patient ('the to-be-split thing'); some read it abstractly ('the meaning that is to be analysed is specified'), making the verse about semantic decomposition — the second claim (no action prior to means-connection) is textually secure and is the load-bearing one

Verse 2.184.translation: As root-hood, and the condition of being an object, belong to a root by the commencement of its coming connection with the means — so let the other case be seen as well.
Verse 2.184.commentary: The retroactive constitution made explicit, and then extended. Dhatoh sadhana-yogasya bhavinah prakramat — from the *commencement* of the root's *coming* (bhavin: future, prospective) connection with the means — dhatutvam karma-bhavas ca: root-hood itself, and the condition of being an object, accrue. This is remarkable: a root is a root *in virtue of* the means-connection it is heading toward; the item's very categorial status is conferred by a relation not yet realized. (The grammar behaves exactly so: an element is called a dhatu because of what it will govern; a stem is called a karman because of the action it will stand under.) Tatha anyad api drsyatam: *so let the other case be seen too* — the invitation is to read the root/preverb relation by the same light: as root-hood is conferred prospectively by connection, so the preverbed root's unity is conferred by the meaning it is going to deliver, not assembled from what its pieces antecedently were. Categories in this grammar are functional and forward-looking — an item is what its role will make it (2.104's capacity-assumption, now temporalized) — which is why no analysis of the pieces will ever yield the whole they were destined to constitute.

Verse 2.185-186.translation: (185) As lac-juice and the like, applied at the time of the seed, benefit the fruits by transformation of colour and the rest — (186) so, from the connection standing in the intellect, the difference of root and preverb, having been made internal, shines forth at the time of the word.
Verse 2.185-186.commentary: The movement's great simile, and one of the most delicate images in the work. The horticultural practice: lac-juice and other dyes applied *at the time of the seed* — bija-kalesu sambaddhah — work their effect not then but far downstream, varna-adi-parinamena phalanam upakurvate: benefiting the *fruits* by a transformation of colour and the rest. Nothing is visible at sowing; the whole efficacy is latent, carried invisibly through the plant's growth, and emerges in the fruit as its colour. So (186): buddhistat abhisambandhat — from the connection *standing in the intellect* (the union of root and preverb accomplished inwardly, in the speaker's mind, antaranga in the fullest sense) — abhyantarikrto bhedah — the difference *having been made internal*, taken inside, absorbed — pada-kale prakasate: shines forth *at the time of the word*. The preverb's contribution is real and is never separately present: it was mixed in at the seed, and what appears at the word is one colour. The simile answers precisely the question that would otherwise sink the doctrine — if root and preverb are semantically one, why does the preverb make a difference? — and answers it without conceding a seam: the difference is *in* the unified meaning as the dye is in the fruit's redness, constitutive and unlocatable. It also re-sounds the kanda's deepest structure: latency ripening into manifestation (1.84's seeds, 2.148's fermentation), here at the scale of a single word's inner history.
Verse 2.185-186.contested: laksarasa-adayah we take as lac-juice and similar dyes used in horticulture (the traditional gloss); the exact practice is obscure to us, but the simile's logic — applied at the seed, manifest in the fruit — is fully carried by the text

Verse 2.187.translation: Sometimes possible differences, not exhibited by the roots alone, are manifested in connection with a preverb — 'pra', 'para' and the rest.
Verse 2.187.commentary: The phenomenon the doctrine must account for, stated fairly. Sambhavino bhedah kevalair anidarsitah — *possible* differences which the bare roots do not exhibit: har- alone gives nothing determinate; pra-har- is to strike, a-har- to fetch, sam-har- to gather, vi-har- to sport. The preverbs pra, para and the rest *do* something, undeniably and systematically. And the verb chosen for what they do is the movement's whole answer in one word: vyajyante — they are *manifested*. Not produced, not added, not composed: manifested — the technical term of Kanda I's entire apparatus (the sounds manifesting the sphota, 1.44-49; the flower's scent released by crushing, 1.79). The differences were *sambhavin*, possible, latent in the root's own semantic range; the preverb does not import them from outside but occasions them — the dye at the seed (2.185-186), the fan raising the ever-present air (1.116). This is why the doctrine of 2.180 does not have to deny the obvious: the preverb's contribution is total and the seam is still fictional, because manifesting is not contributing. The next verse canvasses the alternatives with characteristic fairness.

Verse 2.188.translation: That (preverb) is the expressor of the particulars; or, since it is possible, it is the manifestor; or it is employed as a co-operating factor for the sake of infusing capacity into the root.
Verse 2.188.commentary: The three accounts of what a preverb does, catalogued without immediate adjudication — and the catalogue is the tradition's live one. (i) Vacaka: the preverb *expresses* the particular — 'pra' denotes forth-ness and the verb's sense is compounded of root-sense plus preverb-sense. This is the compositional account, and it is the one 2.180-186 have been dismantling. (ii) Dyotaka: it is the *manifestor* — sambhavat, 'since it is possible', i.e. because the particulars are already possible in the root, the preverb need only light them up (2.187's vyajyante given its technical name; the dyotaka-doctrine, first canvassed for case-endings at 2.164, here at its most consequential home — this is the account the grammatical tradition would ultimately make its own, and this verse is among its earliest clear statements). (iii) Sahakarin: a *co-operating factor*, employed sakty-adhanaya, for the *infusing of capacity* into the root — a third way between the first two: the preverb neither denotes nor merely reveals but *conditions*, adding potency to the root's own power (as heat conditions the seed without being the plant). Bhartrhari lets all three stand; but the middle one is his (2.187's verb was vyajyante), and 2.189-190 will supply the evidence that only manifestation-or-conditioning can absorb.
Verse 2.188.contested: which of the three Bhartrhari holds is a classical question; our reading takes (ii) as favoured on the strength of 2.187's vyajyante and 2.185-186's simile, with (iii) as a friendly variant — the tradition after him divides, and the dyotaka-account's eventual dominance is not itself evidence about the karika

Verse 2.189.translation: And where going and the like is understood from 'stha' and the rest taken alone, there, by inference of two kinds, the preverb 'pra' and the rest is said to have that same property.
Verse 2.189.commentary: Evidence for the manifestation-account, from the roots that need no preverb. Sthadibhih kevalaih — by roots such as stha *alone*, without any preverb — gamanadi tu gamyate: going and the like is (nonetheless) understood: bare roots already deliver directional and modal particulars that one might have credited to preverbs. So the particulars are in the root's range natively — which is (i)'s embarrassment: if 'pra' *denotes* forth-ness, why does the bare root sometimes deliver it? Then the inference: tatra anumanad dvividhat tad-dharma pradir ucyate — from a two-fold inference, the preverb pra and the rest is said to have *that same property*: reasoning from the bare root's capacity (it can deliver the particular unaided) together with the preverbed form's behaviour (it delivers it reliably), one concludes that the preverb shares the root's kind of work rather than contributing a separate content — it is a manifestor of what was there, tad-dharma, of the same character. The argument is genuinely evidential rather than doctrinal: it turns on a distributional fact (bare roots covering preverb-territory) that only the dyotaka or sahakarin accounts predict. And the next verse supplies the mirror-image evidence.
Verse 2.189.contested: anumanad dvividhat ('by a two-fold inference') is unexplained by the karika; our reconstruction (from the bare root's capacity, and from the preverbed form's behaviour) is one plausible filling among several the commentators offer

Verse 2.190.translation: And in the non-employment of 'adhi' and 'pari', so far as a different action is seen — the root that expresses that action is, apart from those two, meaningless.
Verse 2.190.commentary: The mirror-evidence, and the movement's closing stroke — the preverbs that cannot be removed. Some roots simply do not occur bare: adhi-i (to study), pari-dha (to don) and their kind. In the *non-employment* of adhi and pari — aprayoge — yavad drstam kriyantaram, so far as a different action is seen (i.e., the distinct actions these complexes name), tasya abhidhayako dhatuh saha tabhyam anarthakah — the root that expresses that action, *apart from those two, is meaningless*: strip 'adhi' from 'adhi-i' and no residue survives that means studying, or anything at all in that domain. Here the compositional account (2.188's (i)) fails completely and visibly: one cannot sum root-sense and preverb-sense when the root has no sense to contribute. And here 2.180's thesis is vindicated at its strongest: dhatur eva tu tadrsah — the root *itself is of that very kind*, i.e. the real unit is and always was the preverbed complex, the grammar's separation being for the augment's sake (2.180) and nothing more. The obligatory preverb is thus to the movement what kupa-supa-yupa (2.169) was to the earlier one: the case where the analysis's own units are exposed as postings. Root and preverb are two for the rule-writer, one for the speaker — and in adhi-i they are not even two for the rule-writer, since neither piece can be given a meaning to be summed.
Verse 2.190.contested: saha tabhyam is awkward ('together with those two... meaningless') and we construe it as 'apart from those two' following the argument's evident need; some read 'the root, together with those two, expresses that action — (alone it is) meaningless', which yields the same doctrine by a smoother route

Verse 2.191.translation: Just so, certain self-meaning suffixes, occurring within another complex, are commingled with what is meaningless and merely restate the base's meaning.
Verse 2.191.commentary: The preverb-doctrine (2.180-190) extended to the svarthika suffixes — those affixes that, by the grammar's own account, add *nothing*: the -ka of many a form, the affixes taught as sve arthe, 'in their own meaning', which is a courteous way of saying they mean what the stem already meant. Prakrty-artha-anuvadinah: they *restate* the base's meaning (anuvada, 2.115's term for idle repetition). And they are anarthakena samsrstah — *commingled with the meaningless*: the complex contains formatives that neither denote nor manifest. This is the movement's most awkward datum honestly placed: language is full of morphology doing no semantic work at all (2.167's nut and sap already showed it in the derivational core). The verse does not explain it away; it registers that the analyst's inventory contains items real as units and null as meanings, and that the word nonetheless means. Which is precisely the kanda's thesis from another side: if meaning were assembled from the pieces, meaningless pieces would be subtractions — and they are not; they are simply present, and the whole means what it means.

Verse 2.192.translation: Some particles are manifestors; some express a separate meaning; and some might be like augments — expressors of a meaning (only) in combination.
Verse 2.192.commentary: The nipatas — particles — canvassed in three accounts, the same triad the preverbs received (2.188) and for the same reason: particles are the hard case for any denotational semantics. (i) Dyotakah, manifestors: 'eva', 'ca', 'hi' do not denote anything but light up a determination already in the sentence-meaning (the account that would become the tradition's standard for particles, and the one Bhartrhari's system favours). (ii) Prthag-artha-abhidhayinah: expressors of a *separate* meaning — the particle denotes a content of its own (conjunction, emphasis, limitation) which then combines. (iii) The third and most interesting: agama iva — *like augments*: sambhuya arthasya vacakah, expressors of meaning *only in combination* — as an augment (the nut of 2.167) has no independent existence or sense and yet the form containing it means, so the particle contributes only as absorbed, never as a term. The three placements track exactly the possible relations of a formative to meaning: revealing, denoting, or constituting-only-in-combination — and it is characteristic that Bhartrhari lists them without decision. The next verses supply the evidence that only the first and third can absorb.

Verse 2.193.translation: Whether above or before, the manifestor-hood is not differentiated, when they are employed — even in the case of different meanings, in every way.
Verse 2.193.commentary: Evidence for the dyotaka-account, from positional freedom. Uparistat purastad va — whether the particle stands *after* or *before* (the preverb pra- prefixed, the particle ca postposed, the Vedic freedom of placement) — dyotakatvam na bhidyate: its manifestor-hood is *not differentiated*: the work it does is the same work regardless of where in the utterance it falls. And sarvatha, in every way, bhinnarthesv api — even where the meanings differ. The argument turns on a fact about denotation: a denoting element contributes a content that must be *composed* with the rest, and composition is order-sensitive (this is why word order and case are doing labour). A manifesting element illuminates a whole that is already there, and illumination is order-indifferent — the lamp lights the room from any corner. So the particle's positional freedom is evidence of its character: it does not enter the semantic sum (there would be a place for it in the sum), it lights up the sentence-meaning from wherever it stands. The observation is delicate and quite exact about how particles behave; it also explains why the tradition eventually settled on dyotakatva for them.

Verse 2.194.translation: 'Ca' and the rest are not employed alone, though they have word-hood; and a suffix, though expressive, is not employed alone.
Verse 2.194.commentary: Two dependencies, exhibited as a pair. Cadayah — 'ca' and its kind — na prayujyante kevalah, are never employed *alone*, padatve sati: *although* they possess word-hood (the grammar counts them as padas, full words, by its own criteria). And the mirror: pratyayah vacakatve 'pi kevalo na prayujyate — a suffix, *though expressive* (the grammar assigns it a meaning: the -ti of pacati denotes agency-and-present-time, no one doubts it), is likewise never employed alone. Two categories, each with a property that on the atomist's terms should confer independence — wordhood, expressiveness — and neither can stand by itself. The pairing is the argument: if being a word meant being a semantic atom, 'ca' would be utterable; if being expressive meant carrying a deposit, '-ti' would be. Neither is. What both cases show is that the grammar's categories (word, expressive element) are *rule-postings* (2.3, 2.176), tracking the science's operational needs, and that they underdetermine semantic standing entirely. Dependence is the norm; independence, the citation-artifact (2.17).

Verse 2.195.translation: Even where there is expression of cumulation, no exclusion is found; and the state of being, not being a thing, is an action — expressed by something else.
Verse 2.195.commentary: Two observations about what particles and abstract terms fail to deliver. First: samuccita-abhidhane 'pi vyatireko na vidyate — even where cumulation is expressed ('Devadatta *and* Yajnadatta come'), *no exclusion* is found: the conjunction cumulates without thereby excluding anything; 'and' does not contain a 'not-others'. The point registers that a particle's contribution is thin and one-directional — it lights one determination and carries no negative freight; the exclusions a hearer may draw come from elsewhere (the penumbra of 2.163). Second: asattva-bhuto bhavas ca kriya anyena abhidhiyate — bhava, the state-of-being, *not being a thing* (asattva: not a substance, the standing classification of what verbs mean as against what nouns mean), is an action, and is expressed by something *else* — by the verbal apparatus, not by the nominal. The two remarks share a moral relevant to the movement: what the categories of the grammar deliver — cumulation, being, action — are not object-like contents that could be deposited and summed; they are aspects that some element or other lights up within an already-whole meaning.

Verse 2.196.translation: Even where cumulation is expressed, the connection is of words expressing qualified meanings, by way of their subordinate elements; but 'ca' and the rest are dependent.
Verse 2.196.commentary: The syntax of conjunction analysed, and the particle put in its place. When 'ca' cumulates, what is actually connected? Visista-artha-abhidhayinam padanam sambandhah gunaih — the connection is of *words expressing qualified meanings*, by way of their gunas, their subordinate elements: the conjunction joins already-determinate contents through the structure they already have (2.47's pre-fixed roles, 2.48's karaka-architecture); it does not manufacture the joining. And the particle itself: paratantras tu cadayah — 'ca' and the rest are *dependent*, paratantra: not standing on their own, contributing only within a structure that supplies them their office (2.62's finger-and-hand; 1.62's paratantra guna). This completes 2.194's observation with its explanation: 'ca' cannot be uttered alone not because of some accident of usage but because dependence is its nature — it is an operator on a whole, and where there is no whole there is nothing for it to be. The verse also quietly closes the door on the second account of 2.192 (particles as separate-meaning-expressors): a separate meaning could be uttered; a dependent manifestor could not.

Verse 2.197.translation: Some action, having generated the connection, withdraws; sometimes the connection arises while the action-word is being heard.
Verse 2.197.commentary: A new topic — the genitive and the syntax of relation — opened with a temporal observation about how relations come to be. Two patterns. Janayitva kriya kacit sambandham vinivartate: some action, *having generated* the connection, then *withdraws* — the action that produced a relation is no longer present when the relation is spoken of: 'the king's man' (rajapurusa) records a relation that some act of appointing established and which now stands without it; the verb has done its work and gone. Srtiyamane kriya-sabde sambandho jayate kvacit: and sometimes the connection *arises while the action-word is being heard* — the relation is present in the very utterance of the verb, live in the sentence ('he cooks the rice'). The distinction between relations that outlive their generating action and relations constituted in the saying is about to do real grammatical work: it is what separates the domain of the genitive (relations standing without a present verb) from the domain of the karakas (relations constituted by a present verb). Bhartrhari's semantics of relation always runs this way — through the temporality of how the relation came to stand (Kanda III's Sambandha-samuddesa will take it up at length).

Verse 2.198.translation: There the sixth case is prescribed word by word, for the exclusion of the compound; but the karaka has been cited as a counter-example, for the sake of showing.
Verse 2.198.commentary: The technical seat of 2.197's first pattern: the genitive. Where the generating action has withdrawn, the relation is expressed by sasthi, the sixth case — and it is prescribed pratipadam, word by word, samasasya nivrttaye: *for the exclusion of the compound* — that is, the rule teaches the genitive individually so as to block automatic compounding (rajnah purusah rather than rajapurusah, where the analysis is to be kept visible). The grammar's motive here is purely operational: the case-ending is posted where the science needs the relation *displayed*. And then: darsanartham tu karakam pratyudahrtam — the karaka has been cited as a *counter-example*, for the sake of showing: where the action *is* present (2.197's second pattern), the relation is a karaka-relation and the genitive does not apply — the counter-example teaches the boundary. Two devices, two domains, one distinction — and the distinction is the temporal one 2.197 drew. The verse is a small model of the kanda's method: a semantic observation about relations, then the grammar's machinery shown to be its faithful engineering.

Verse 2.199.translation: And that arisen connection, when the action-word has withdrawn, is restricted, in this case and that, by the karmapravacaniya.
Verse 2.199.commentary: The third device in the system, and the most delicate. Consider a relation that *arose* from an action (2.197's first pattern) and now stands with the verb gone. It is vague — a relation, but which? Here the grammar deploys the karmapravacaniya: the class of words (anu, pari, adhi, prati and the rest) that Panini defines as former preverbs now serving to *specify* a relation rather than to qualify an action. Tatra tatra niyamyate: the standing connection is *restricted*, case by case, by this element — 'anu' fixing it as following-upon, 'prati' as directed-toward. The name itself is the doctrine: karma-pravacaniya, 'that which speaks *about* the action' — it does not name the action, it speaks *of* it, from outside, retroactively specifying the relation the withdrawn action left. This is a category that exists precisely because the grammar needed a place for words that are neither preverbs (no live verb to qualify) nor case-endings (they are not endings) but which nonetheless pin down relations — and Bhartrhari's interest is that its very existence testifies to relation-specification as a distinct semantic office (2.204 will state its character exactly).

Verse 2.200.translation: That by which an action-word is implied is construed with the karaka case-endings — as, for example, the non-preverb-hood of 'vi' in the case of 'likh'.
Verse 2.200.commentary: The boundary-test between the categories. Yena kriya-padaksepah — that element *by which an action-word is implied*, which brings a verb into the construal even though none is uttered — sa karaka-vibhaktibhih yujyate: *that* one is construed with the karaka case-endings, and hence belongs on the live-action side of 2.197's divide (the case-endings being the machinery of verb-governed relations, 2.198). The example is a grammarian's: vi- with likh (to write, to scratch) exhibits anupasargata, non-preverb-hood — vi- there is not qualifying a live action in the way a preverb does, and its behaviour with the case-endings shows it. The details of the specific Paninian ruling matter less than the method being displayed: the categories (preverb, karmapravacaniya, particle) are distinguished by *how they behave with the case-apparatus*, which is to say, by whether the relation they touch is verb-constituted or verb-abandoned. Bhartrhari is showing that the grammar's finest categorial cuts are made on exactly the semantic distinction he drew at 2.197 — and this is the recurring pattern of the kanda's argument from grammar: the science's postings are not arbitrary, they are the semantics engineered.
Verse 2.200.contested: the transcription is faulty at likhav anupasargata and the intended Paninian case is not securely recoverable; we translate the evident sense and note the corruption

Verse 2.201.translation: And the use of 'stha' is seen in 'apratyajayan'; and in 'sunbanti', in the sense of facing-toward, 'abhi' is employed even alone.
Verse 2.201.commentary: Two attested usages cited as data — and their state in the source is poor. The first illustrates a root's employment in a context where the expected element is absent; the second, more pointedly, exhibits abhi employed kevalah, *alone*, in the sense of abhimukhya, facing-toward: a preverb standing by itself and delivering a determinate sense. That second datum matters for the movement, because it cuts against 2.194's dependency-claim for particles and shows that the classes are not tidy: some elements the grammar files as preverbs can, in attested (probably Vedic) usage, function independently. Bhartrhari's practice throughout has been to record such untidiness rather than legislate it away (2.157's mass-terms, 2.175's both-ways settlement), and the honest moral is 2.179's: difference and non-difference both admissible, the categories being purpose-relative postings and not natural kinds. The verse is also a reminder that this grammar's categories were built to fit a corpus, and the corpus keeps producing residue.
Verse 2.201.contested: the transcription of both examples (apratyajayan, sunbanti) is corrupt and the underlying Vedic citations are not recoverable from it; our rendering translates what the text gives and we flag that the specific attestations are uncertain

Verse 2.202.translation: Karmapravacaniya-hood is prescribed in connection with an action, for the sake of excluding the retroflexion of 's' and the like, in the case of 'sva-ati' and the rest, which are of contrary property.
Verse 2.202.commentary: The karmapravacaniya category shown to be, in part, a phonological posting. Its being *prescribed* kriya-yoge, in connection with an action, has a purpose the verse names bluntly: satva-adi-vinivrtty-artham — *for the exclusion of the retroflexion of s and the like*. Sanskrit's sandhi rules retroflex an s after certain preverbs; classify an element as karmapravacaniya rather than upasarga and the retroflexion is blocked. So the category earns part of its keep by getting the sound-changes right — vidharmanam, for elements 'of contrary property', whose phonological behaviour deviates from what their apparent class would predict. This is 2.180's lesson recurring at the highest level of the movement: a categorial distinction that looks semantic is, at least partly, posted for the augment's and the sandhi's sake. Bhartrhari is not debunking the category (2.199 and 2.204 give it a real semantic office); he is showing, with his usual exactness about the science's motives, that its boundaries are drawn where the *rules* need them — which is why no inference runs from the grammar's categories to the meaning's joints.
Verse 2.202.contested: the transcription's svatyadinam we read as sva-ati-adinam (the elements ati etc.); the specific list is uncertain but the satva-blocking motive is clear and is the verse's cargo

Verse 2.203.translation: When the delimitation of the connection of cause and effect has been effected by 'anu', the third case, whose mark is causality, though obtained, is annulled from the outset.
Verse 2.203.commentary: A worked case, showing the karmapravacaniya displacing a case-ending. A causal relation would ordinarily be expressed by the third case (trtiya, the instrumental: hetu-laksana, 'whose defining mark is causality' — 'by reason of X'). But when 'anu' has already effected the yoga-pariccheda — the *delimitation of the connection* between hetu and hetumat, cause and effect — then arambhat badhyate prapta trtiya: the instrumental, though obtained by rule, is annulled *from the outset*. The relation having been pinned by the particle, the case-ending that would pin it is pre-empted; two devices for one office, and the science lets the first-comer hold it. The verse is a small but exact illustration of the badha-machinery (2.66's annulment, 2.73's hierarchy) operating between *devices* rather than between meanings — and of the economy that governs the whole system: no relation is specified twice. It also shows the karmapravacaniya doing genuine semantic work (delimiting a relation), which 2.204 is about to state in its most precise form.

Verse 2.204.translation: This one is not a manifestor of the action, nor an expressor of the connection; nor does it imply an action-word — but it is a differentiator of the connection.
Verse 2.204.commentary: The karmapravacaniya defined by four exclusions and one positive — and the definition is a small masterpiece of categorial precision. It is *not* kriyaya dyotakah, a manifestor of the action (so it is not a preverb, whose office is precisely that: 2.187-188). It is *not* sambandhasya vacakah, an expressor of the connection (so it is not a case-ending, which denotes the relation: 2.198). It does *not* kriya-padaksepin, imply an action-word (so it fails the test of 2.200 and does not belong on the live-verb side). What it *is*: sambandhasya bhedakah — a *differentiator of the connection*: it takes a relation already standing (2.197's withdrawn-action relation, 2.199) and specifies *which* relation it is, without naming it, without lighting up any verb, without bringing a verb into play. The office is real, distinct from every neighbouring one, and — this is the point for the kanda — it is an office of *operating on an already-present whole*: differentiating, not contributing. That is the same office 2.187 gave the preverb (manifesting a latent particular) and 2.192-193 gave the particle (illuminating from any position). Three categories, three ways of touching a meaning that is already there in one piece. Not one of them adds a brick.

Verse 2.205.translation: A complex of meaningless things is meaningful; and likewise meaningless. The word of phonemes is joined with meaning; the parts in the word are not.
Verse 2.205.commentary: A summarizing quartet — the kanda's positions on complexes, stated with almost algebraic compression. Anarthakanam sanghatah sarthakah: a complex of *meaningless* items is meaningful — the phonemes, severally null (2.12, 2.53), constitute a meaning-bearing word: emergence, and the standing datum every semantics must face. Tatha anarthakah: and *likewise meaningless* — some complexes of meaningless items remain meaningless (the augment plus the marker, the syllable-strings that are not words). So complexity alone does not confer meaning; something else must. And then the settlement, in the plainest words the kanda ever uses: varnanam padam arthena yuktam — the word of phonemes *is joined with meaning*; na avayavah pade — the *parts in the word are not*. Meaning attaches at the word and does not distribute inward: everything the movement since 2.180 has argued (roots and preverbs, particles, suffixes, augments — none of them a semantic atom) here reduced to a single line. The next verses take the same question one level up, to complexes of *meaningful* things.
Verse 2.205.contested: the second clause (tatha anarthakah) is elliptical and readers divide over whether it means 'likewise (a complex of meaningless things may be) meaningless' (our reading) or 'and (a complex) of meaningful things (may be) meaningless'; 2.206-207 canvass both, which is why the compression here is tolerable

Verse 2.206.translation: But a complex of words joined with meaning is differentiated again, by the exclusion of a further meaning and by the departure of the connection.
Verse 2.206.commentary: One level up: complexes of *meaningful* items. The words, each artha-yukta (2.205's line, granted at their own grain), form a sanghata — and that complex bhidyate punah, is *differentiated again*: it acquires a character of its own, and the verse names the two ways. Arthantara-avarodhena: by the *exclusion of a further meaning* — the complex bars a sense its members would have admitted (compounds notoriously narrow: 'rajapurusa' does not cover every man related to a king). And sambandha-vigamena: by the *departure of the connection* — the relation that generated the compound withdraws and leaves the whole standing on its own (2.197's first pattern, now at the level of word-formation: the compound outlives the syntax that made it). Both mechanisms are subtractive — the complex is *not* the sum plus something; it is the sum minus the freedom of its members. This is a genuinely acute observation about lexicalization, and it prepares the two disputes the next verses report: whether meaningful-plus-meaningless can combine, and whether a complex has a meaning beyond its parts.

Verse 2.207.translation: A meaningful and a meaningless, being different, do not attain connection; they do attain it, say some, on the evidence of 'kutira' and the like.
Verse 2.207.commentary: The first dispute, and it is exactly the question 2.191's svarthika suffixes forced. Can a meaningful element and a meaningless one *connect*? The negative case is principled: sarthaka-anarthakau bhede sambandham na adhigacchatah — being of different kinds, they do not attain connection: a semantic relation needs two terms with semantic standing, and a null formative offers none; you cannot relate something to nothing. The affirmative case is empirical: adhigacchata iti eke, some say they do — kutira-adi-nidarsanat, on the evidence of 'kutira' and its kind: words whose analysis yields a meaningful stem plus a formative that means nothing, and which nonetheless behave as unified derivatives, the null element visibly doing formal work. The dispute has no resolution offered, and its irresolution is the point: the analyst's inventory contains meaningful and meaningless items whose 'combination' is either impossible or ubiquitous depending on which grain one credits — which is precisely what one expects if the items are postings and the combination a description (2.10, 2.88). The whole is a word; its pieces are the grammar's bookkeeping; asking how the pieces connect is asking how entries in a ledger touch.

Verse 2.208.translation: From meaningful elements a complex arises with a distinct meaning; it does not so arise, say some, in the case of compounds, self-meaning suffixes and the like.
Verse 2.208.commentary: The second dispute, and it is the kanda's own thesis contested from within. Arthavadbhyo visistarthah sanghata upajayate — from meaningful elements, a complex arises *with a distinct meaning*: the whole means something the parts did not (2.143's anyaiva at the word's scale; 2.169's kupa-supa-yupa; 2.181's samgramayati). Nopajayate iti eke: it does *not* so arise, say others — samasa-svarthikadisu, in the case of compounds and self-meaning suffixes: precisely where the grammar teaches that the formation adds nothing (the svarthika means 'in its own meaning', i.e. the base's; many a compound is held to convey just its members' relation). The two parties are reading the same data in opposite directions — one sees emergence everywhere and calls the grammar's 'adds nothing' a bookkeeping convenience; the other sees the grammar's own testimony that some complexes are semantically inert. Bhartrhari reports without deciding, and by now the reader knows why: both are correct at their grain (2.179's licensed double description), and what settles the question in any instance is not the pieces but the comprehension the whole occasions (2.34's pramana). The catalogue of complexes will continue; its moral has been fixed since 2.143.

Verse 2.209.translation: But some elements have their meaning inseparably established, their capacities known in isolation; others have their capacities merely constructed, by agreement and difference.
Verse 2.209.commentary: A distinction among the analyst's own units, and it grades them by evidential warrant. Some are yuta-siddha-arthah — their meaning established as *conjoined*, inseparable from the whole they occur in — and yet bhede nirjnata-saktayah, their capacities *known in isolation*: independently attested, met elsewhere on their own, so that crediting them in the complex rests on real evidence (2.166's conditions for trusting distributional method). Others are kalpita-saktayah — their capacities *merely constructed*, and constructed anvaya-vyatirekabhyam, by agreement and difference alone: the method finds a recurrence and posits a power to explain it, with no independent attestation whatever. The two grades are not equal, and the verse's quiet work is to say so: some of grammar's units are discovered, others are fabricated by the very procedure that reports them (2.167's sap, meaningless and undeniable). Since the whole preceding movement has shown that meaning attaches at wholes, this grading tells the analyst which of his posits he may lean on and which are his own shadow.

Verse 2.210.translation: It is only the scientific meaning of the phonemes that has been shown, in the matter of their meaningfulness; of roots and the rest, in their pure form, no worldly meaning exists.
Verse 2.210.commentary: The distinction between two kinds of meaning, and it governs everything the movement has been reporting. When the grammar treats an element as arthavat — meaningful — the meaning in question is sastrartha eva, *only the scientific meaning*: the value assigned within the system, for the system's operations. Dhatvadinam visuddhanam laukiko 'rtho na vidyate: of roots and the rest *in their pure form* — the root as the grammar isolates it, stripped of every affix and preverb — no *worldly* meaning exists at all. Nobody ever means 'pac' at anyone; the pure root is not a thing one can say. Its 'meaning' is an entry in a derivational ledger, real as an entry and null as an utterance. This is the sharpest formulation the kanda gives of its central methodological claim (2.10, 2.88): the units of analysis have meanings *in the analysis*, and to slide from that to meanings in speech is to mistake the ledger for the world. The verses following extend the diagnosis to the affixes.
Verse 2.210.contested: sastrartha we render 'scientific meaning' (the value within the science); the compound also permits 'the meaning taught in the sastra' — the deflationary point, that it is not laukika, is explicit either way

Verse 2.211.translation: And the meaning of primary and secondary suffixes, taken alone, is non-worldly; likewise, prior to the case-ending, the meaning of what ends in them does not exist.
Verse 2.211.commentary: The diagnosis of 2.210 extended in two steps. First: krt and taddhita suffixes — the primary and secondary affixes, the grammar's great derivational engines — kevalanam alaukikah: taken *alone*, their meaning is non-worldly. The -ana of 'karana', the -tva of 'gotva' mean nothing anybody ever meant; they mean within the derivation. Second, and this goes further: prag vibhaktes tad-antasya tatha eva artho na vidyate — *prior to the case-ending*, the meaning of what ends in those suffixes likewise does not exist: even the derived stem, suffix and all, has no meaning until inflected. 'Karana-' is not yet a thing said; 'karanena' is. This is the strictest possible statement of where meaning enters: not at the root, not at the derived stem, but at the *padа* — the inflected word, the smallest thing utterable (2.205's line: 'the word of phonemes is joined with meaning; the parts in the word are not'). Everything the derivation traverses on its way there is scaffolding, meaningful only in the ledger. Panini's own system says as much in its way, teaching that a form is not a pada until it bears its ending.

Verse 2.212.translation: The meaning that is more manifest, which is observed in forms ending in a suffix — that, being of such a kind, has been resorted to on account of the section on meaningfulness.
Verse 2.212.commentary: Why, then, does the grammar talk as though suffixed forms had meanings? Because they *more manifestly* do: abhivyaktataro yo 'rthah pratyayantesu laksyate — the meaning observed in forms ending in a suffix is more manifest, closer to the surface, nearer to something one could say. And the science asritah — has *resorted* to that appearance — arthavatta-prakaranat, on account of its own section on meaningfulness: because a rule-system needs to classify its elements as meaningful or not, it takes the most manifest available meaning and posts it. Tathavidhah: 'being of such a kind' — that is, being a matter of degree of manifestness rather than of presence or absence. The verse completes 2.210-211 with an account of the grammar's motive that is neither debunking nor credulous: the science's assignments of meaning to sub-word elements track a real gradient (some pieces are nearer to sayability than others) and posit, at a convenient point on that gradient, a boundary that speech does not draw. Postings again — but *motivated* postings, which is why the science works.
Verse 2.212.contested: arthavatta-prakarana refers to the grammar's treatment of meaningfulness (the arthavat-section); precisely which discussion is meant is not recoverable from the karika, and our rendering keeps it general

Verse 2.213.translation: If there is no difference of self of word and sentence from the phonemes, then the phonemes, by a capacity dependent on one another, would be the expressor.
Verse 2.213.commentary: A conditional put to the phoneme-realist, and it is a trap he cannot escape by either exit. Suppose atma-bhedo na kascit — there is no *difference of self* between the phonemes on one side and word-and-sentence on the other: the word just *is* its phonemes, nothing over and above (2.52's nominalism, 2.72's atomism). Then what expresses? Not any phoneme severally, since none means (2.53). So: anyonya-apeksaya saktya varnah syad abhidhayakah — the phonemes, by a capacity *dependent on one another*, would be the expressor. But notice what has just been conceded: a capacity that exists only in mutual dependence is not a property any phoneme has; it is a property of their standing-together — which is to say, of the whole, under another name. The realist who refuses a distinct word-self must relocate expressive power into a relational capacity, and the relational capacity is the word-self he refused. The argument is a fine specimen of the kanda's habit: not refuting the atomist but letting him restate holism in his own vocabulary (2.213's apeksika sakti is exactly 2.168's relationally-fixed power).

Verse 2.214.translation: The complex that is the expressor is not deficient by any phoneme; if it is not a different word, it is understood by that as not deficient.
Verse 2.214.commentary: The consequence of 2.213's concession, and it is stringent. The sanghata yo 'bhidhayakah — the complex that does the expressing — na kenacid varnena nyunah: is *not deficient by any phoneme*: every one is required; drop one and this complex is not that complex. Then the dilemma's second horn: na ced sabdantaram asau — if the phoneme-diminished string is *not a different word* (if the realist wants to say it is the same word, imperfectly uttered), anyunas tena gamyate: it is understood by that very (claim) as non-deficient — i.e. he must hold that the shortened string still expresses, which contradicts the requirement he just granted. The realist is caught between saying that every phoneme is constitutive (in which case the word is a whole with no tolerance, and its unity is doing the work) and saying that some are dispensable (in which case he cannot say which, or why). Non-deficiency is a property of wholes: this is 2.34's non-monotonicity (add or subtract, and you get not more or less meaning but meaninglessness) driven down to the phonemic grain.

Verse 2.215.translation: That one, depositing a memory from its occasioning ground in the expressive word, brings the mediated meaning near, as it were directly, through the word.
Verse 2.215.commentary: How the mediation actually feels — and the verse describes the phenomenon that makes all the atomist's mistakes tempting. Something (the phoneme, the part, the occasioning element) nimittat smrtim adadhat — *depositing a memory* from its occasioning ground in the expressive word (the seed-and-trace apparatus of 1.84, the association-channel of 2.163) — saksad iva vyavahitam sabdena artham upohate: brings the *mediated* meaning near *as though directly*, through the word. Two words carry it. Vyavahita: the meaning is mediated, reached through a relay (2.152's doctrine: the corrupt form expressing with the correct one interposed; here generalized). Saksad iva: *as if* directly — the mediation is invisible from inside; the hearer experiences immediacy. That is precisely why the parts seem to carry meaning: their contribution is real, it is mediated, and the mediation does not show.
