So word-based practice is a fiction with the two virtues that matter in an instrument: generality and economy — laghava, the very virtue Panini's system is built to maximize (2.176's brevity). The verse is Bhartrhari's answer to the obvious objection against Audumbarayana: if the parts of speech are unreal, why does everyone use them? Because they work, and working is what fictions are for (2.238's untrue path).

Verse 2.344.translation: In the world, comprehenders' establishments do not arise from connection with the object; therefore there is nothing non-worldly, other than the sentence.
Verse 2.344.commentary: The conclusion of the movement, and it is a strange and powerful one. Na loke pratipattrnam artha-yogat prasiddhayah: in the world, comprehenders' *establishments* — their settled takings, their prasiddhis — do not arise *from connection with the object*: what a community settles into meaning is not fixed by how things are (2.296 gave prasiddhi the verdict on the real; here prasiddhi is shown to owe nothing to the real). Tasmat alaukiko vakyat anyah kascin na vidyate: *therefore* there is nothing non-worldly other than the sentence. The inference is compressed and its force is this: if the world's semantic settlements float free of the objects, then everything below the sentence — the words, their meanings, the categories — is thoroughly *laukika*, a creature of settlement and currency; and the only item that is *not* a settlement, the only thing that stands as it is regardless of what anyone has agreed, is the sentence itself, standing whole in cognition (2.342's nityatvam buddhau). The sentence is the sole non-conventional item in language. Everything else is what we have agreed to see in it.
Verse 2.344.contested: alaukikah is doing surprising work here — elsewhere in the kanda it names what falls outside practice (2.297's seer's cognition, excluded from vyavahara); here the sentence is called the only alaukika, which we read as 'the only item not constituted by worldly settlement'. The tension with 2.297 is real and may be deliberate

Verse 2.345.translation: And the own-meanings, indicated in sentences by marks heard elsewhere, are themselves understood — unnoticed, because of the non-difference of form.
Verse 2.345.commentary: A mechanism-note on how the sentence supplies what it does not say. Svarthah eva pratiyante — the *own-meanings* are themselves understood — sucitah lingaih anyatra sruyamanaih: *indicated* by marks *heard elsewhere*, in other sentences, other contexts (the intertextual linga of 2.86). And the crucial clause: rupa-abhedad alaksitah — *unnoticed*, because of the *non-difference of form*: the indication does its work invisibly, since nothing in the shape marks the indicated content as having come from outside. So a sentence delivers meanings whose warrant lies in utterances the hearer met long ago, and delivers them seamlessly, as though they were in the words. This is 2.92's fallacy (projecting other sentences' meanings into this one) seen from the other side — as a *competence* rather than an error: the same transfer-by-resemblance that misleads the provincial is what lets any of us understand anything, since every word we hear is met with the residue of every prior hearing. The kanda has consistently refused to treat this as either simply veridical or simply erroneous. It is the untrue path (2.238), and it is the only road.

Verse 2.346.translation: What is abandoned in a general rule's sentence — as if wordless, though word-like — that, heard in the annulling sentences, is understood elsewhere.
Verse 2.346.commentary: The doctrine applied to the structure of rule-systems. In an utsarga-vakya, a general rule, something is tyaktam — *abandoned*, left out: asabdam iva sabdavat, as if wordless though word-like — present in the rule's sense without any word for it (the exception the general rule silently reserves). And: tad badhakesu vakyesu srutam anyatra gamyate — *that*, heard in the *annulling* sentences (the exceptions, the apavadas that override the general), is understood *elsewhere* — i.e. the general rule's unstated reservation is what the exception-sentences make explicit, and reading them, one understands retroactively what the general rule was always meaning. Rule-systems are thus *distributed* sentences: no single formulation carries its own content, and each is completed by others it does not mention. This is 2.76's maha-vakya doctrine (the discourse as the real unit) applied to the sastra itself — Panini's grammar is one sentence, and every sutra is a word in it.

Verse 2.347.translation: 'Curds for the brahmins' — the text is commenced, excluding Mathara; Mathara, from his connection with buttermilk, there declares its actual meaning.
Verse 2.347.commentary: The mechanism of 2.346 in a worked scene, and it is a small comedy. Brahmananam srutir dadhi prakranta — the text says 'curds for the brahmins'; and mathara vina, *excluding Mathara* — one brahmin gets buttermilk instead. Now: matharas takra-sambandhat tatra acaste yatharthataam — *Mathara*, from his connection with the buttermilk, *declares the actual meaning* there. The exception reveals the rule. Until Mathara's buttermilk, 'curds for the brahmins' looked universal; his exclusion shows that the general provision always meant 'curds for the brahmins other than Mathara' — the reservation was in it from the start, unworded (2.346's asabdam iva). And the pleasant irony the verse leans on: it is the *excluded* member who tells you what the rule says. The one left out is the interpreter. This is the apavada-doctrine (exception overrides general) recast as semantics: exceptions do not defeat general sentences, they *disclose* them, and a rule's meaning is not available until you know what it excludes — which is 2.344's point about words in another key.
Verse 2.347.contested: the Mathara-example is from the exegetical tradition's stock and its details vary; nothing turns on the specific figure

Verse 2.348.translation: Even where there is connection of several finite verbs, the sentence of the general rule and its exception is held by some to be one alone, though standing as if of divided form.
Verse 2.348.commentary: The unity of rule-and-exception, stated as doctrine. Aneka-akhyata-yoge 'pi — even where *several* finite verbs are connected (the general rule has its verb, the exception has its own; by every formal criterion, two sentences) — nyayya-apavadayoh vakyam ekam eva isyate kaiscid: the sentence of the general and the exception is held by some to be *one alone* — bhinna-rupam iva sthitam, *standing as if* of divided form. The iva is doing the work it always does in this kanda: the division is apparent, the unity real. So a general rule and its exception, however far apart in the text, however separately verbed, constitute a single sentence — because a single expectancy closes across them (2.4: the sentence is where requirement is satisfied; the general rule alone is sakanksa, still requiring, until the exception limits it). This is the most extreme extension of the vakya-concept in the work: sentencehood spanning discontinuous text, indifferent to verb-count, defined solely by where the meaning completes.

Verse 2.349.translation: Restriction, prohibition, and likewise the remainder of the injunction — that being so, what has elision of the finite verb in the second is known as its remainder, without elision.
Verse 2.349.commentary: The technical consequences of 2.348's unity. If rule and exception are one sentence, then the exception's various functions — niyama, *restriction* (narrowing the general); pratisedha, *prohibition* (barring a case); vidhi-sesa, the *remainder of the injunction* (completing what the general left open) — are all articulations of one meaning rather than independent assertions. And a grammatical marker of the unity is offered: dvitiye yo lug akhyatah tac-chesam alukam viduh — what has *elision of the finite verb* in the second member is known as *its remainder without elision*: the exception's verb may be dropped precisely because the general's verb is doing duty for both — the ellipsis is possible because the two are one sentence (2.327's unuttered word supplied by the cognition, here supplied by the other half of the same sentence). Elision is thus evidence of unity: you can only omit what the whole already carries.

Verse 2.350.translation: They are requirement-free in their accomplishment, and principal with respect to one another; since they are of no assistance to each other, how could there be single-sentence-hood?
Verse 2.350.commentary: The objection to 2.348, put with full force, and the movement ends on it. Consider the general rule and the exception as sentences: nirakanksani nirvrttau — each is *requirement-free* in its own accomplishment: 'curds for the brahmins' is a complete provision; 'buttermilk for Mathara' is another. Pradhanani parasparam: and they are *principal* with respect to one another — neither is subordinate to the other; each stands as its own injunction, not as a member of the other. Hence: tesam anupakaritvat katham syad eka-vakyata — since they are *of no assistance* to each other, how could there be single-sentence-hood? The objection is exact and it uses the kanda's own criteria: unity requires expectancy, expectancy requires incompleteness, and two complete injunctions have none to offer each other (2.325: dependence is what demotes a sentence to a word). Bhartrhari has stated the difficulty at its strongest and left it standing here — the movement's next verses take it up. It is characteristic: the hardest objection to his own position gets the best statement in the text, and it gets it from him.

Verse 2.351.translation: Because of the need created by the particular rule, the sentence-remainder is inferred; since the meaning is to be excluded as qualified, the mutual requirement is therefore equal.
Verse 2.351.commentary: The answer to 2.350's objection, and it turns on where the need arises. The objection was that a general rule and its exception are each complete and so cannot require each other. The reply: visesa-vidhina arthitvat — *because of the need created by the particular rule* — vakya-sesah anumiyate: the sentence-remainder is *inferred*. The exception generates the requirement retroactively: once 'buttermilk for Mathara' is heard, 'curds for the brahmins' is no longer complete as it stood — it now needs its reservation, and the reservation was always its own (2.346's asabdam iva, the unworded exclusion). And the ground: visesavat nivartye 'rthe — since the meaning to be *excluded* is itself qualified, particular — tasmat tulyam vyapeksanam: the mutual requirement is therefore *equal*. Each half needs the other, symmetrically: the general needs the exception to be determinate, the exception needs the general to have something to except from. So nirakanksata (2.350's premise) was an illusion of reading the general rule alone — completeness that only looked complete because the reader had not yet met what limits it.

Verse 2.352.section: Names, Their Parts, and the Sciences
Verse 2.352.translation: That which is one part of a name-word — of it there is no elision; that name, having a distinct form, does not withdraw from anywhere.
Verse 2.352.commentary: A new movement opens, on proper names and their internal parts, and its first rule is severe. Samjna-sabda-ekadesah — a *part* of a name-word ('datta' inside 'Devadatta', 'agni' inside 'Agnidatta') — tasya lopo na vidyate: of it there is *no elision*: you cannot drop it, because it is not a semantically detachable element that the whole could survive losing. And: visista-rupa sa samjna kutas ca na nivartate — that name, having a *distinct form*, does not *withdraw* from anywhere: the name is one form, self-identical, and no part of it can be treated as an ingredient that might be subtracted or replaced. This is 2.220's conclusion (the complex alone is the ground of the distinct meaning) applied to names, and names are the ideal case: nobody thinks 'Devadatta' means god-given, everybody knows whom it means, and the members are visibly idle. The movement will use names as a controlled experiment in what parts can and cannot do.

Verse 2.353.translation: And from another name — 'Datta' and the like — no other name is understood; how should the word 'Datta' express the name-bearer called Devadatta?
Verse 2.353.commentary: The rule of 2.352 argued from the obvious. Samjnantarat dattadeh na anya samjna pratiyate: from 'Datta' — which is itself a name, a perfectly good one, borne by other men — no *other* name is understood. And the question that makes it vivid: samjninam devadattakhyam datta-sabdah katham vadet — how should the word 'Datta' express the name-bearer *called Devadatta*? Call for Datta and Devadatta does not turn. The two names share a form and share nothing else; the presence of 'datta' in 'Devadatta' gives you no purchase on Devadatta whatever. This is kupa-supa-yupa (2.169) in the domain where the intuition is strongest: phonetic containment is semantically inert, and names make it undeniable because names have no penumbra of associated sense to blur the test. If parts contributed, 'Datta' would half-summon Devadatta. It does not summon him at all.

Verse 2.354.translation: Some consider the connection of the own-forms of words with all their name-bearers to be equal, by all their parts, as with the aggregate.
Verse 2.354.commentary: The rival account, stated fairly. Kecid manyante: some hold that the connection of sabda-svarupani — the own-forms of words — with sarva-samjnibhih, *all* their name-bearers, is tulyam sarvaih avayavaih: *equal, by all their parts* — samudayavat, as with the aggregate. That is: the whole name and each of its parts are equally connected to the named; the connection distributes. On this view 'datta' *is* connected to Devadatta, just as 'Devadatta' is — the parts being co-owners of the reference. The position is not silly: it explains why we feel that 'Agnidatta' has something to do with fire, why patronymics work, why name-elements recur meaningfully across a family. And it is exactly what 2.353 has just denied. Bhartrhari's practice throughout has been to let the rival state its best case immediately after the datum that embarrasses it — the reader is meant to hold both and feel the pressure, which the next verses release.

Verse 2.355.translation: But the meaningfulness of phonemes would be as that of names with their name-bearers; a connected part is not construed in the analysis of a name.
Verse 2.355.commentary: The reductio of 2.354. If the parts of a name are connected to the named as the whole is, then by parity varnanam arthavattvam — the *phonemes'* meaningfulness — would be samjnanam samjnibhih bhavet: on the same footing as names' connection with their bearers: the 'd' of 'Devadatta' would name him too, and every phoneme would be a name. The regress is the one 2.28-29 ran against linguistic atomism, and it lands here with the same force. Hence the rule: sambaddho 'vayavah samjna-pravive ke na kalpyate — a *connected part* is not construed in the *analysis* of a name: when the grammar analyses a name into elements, it does not thereby credit those elements with the connection the name has. The analysis divides; the connection does not. This is the movement's version of 2.88's settlement (abstraction according to capacities, the practice preserved, the constitution untouched) applied where it is cleanest.

Verse 2.356.translation: When there is connection of the name-bearer with all the own-forms simultaneously, there is no possibility of conveying it from things that are alike to one part.
Verse 2.356.commentary: The rival's position pressed to its collapse. Suppose the samjnin is connected sarva-svarupaih yugapat — with *all* the own-forms *simultaneously*: every part of the name bonded to the bearer at once. Then: na ekadesa-sarupebhyah tat-pratyayana-sambhavah — there is *no possibility* of conveying him from things alike to *one part*. The reasoning: if the bond requires all parts at once, then anything sharing only a part shares none of the bond — so 'Datta' cannot even partially convey Devadatta (which 2.353 observed), but neither can any partial resemblance ever convey anything, and the phenomenon the rival was invoking (the felt pull of name-elements) is unexplained by the very doctrine invoked to explain it. The rival wanted parts to carry reference and has ended with parts carrying nothing. The next verse gives the correct account of what the parts actually do.

Verse 2.357.translation: But from one part, in the case of some, a memory arises with respect to the aggregate; and from the word that is the object of the memory, the aggregate's meaning is understood.
Verse 2.357.commentary: The right account, and it is the kanda's standing mechanism. Ekadesat tu sanghate kesamcit jayate smrtih: from *one part*, in the case of some (hearers, cases), a *memory* arises with respect to the *aggregate* — hearing 'datta', one is put in mind of 'Devadatta'. And then: smrteh tu visayat sabdat sanghatartah pratiyate — from the *word that is the object of that memory* — i.e. from the whole name now recalled — the aggregate's meaning is understood. The relay is exact: part → memory of whole → meaning of whole. The part never touches the meaning; it touches the *whole word*, which touches the meaning. So the felt pull of name-elements (2.354's datum) is real and is a fact about recollection, not about reference — 2.315-316's visesa-smrti-hetavah, the causes of the recollection of the particular, operating inside a name. The parts are mnemonic triggers, and the trigger is not the gun.

Verse 2.358.translation: How does a memory arise from one part with respect to a distinct aggregate? And how could a word that is being cognized be the expressor of a meaning?
Verse 2.358.commentary: Two objections to 2.357, and they are good ones. First: ekadesat smrtih bhinne sanghate jayate katham — how does a memory arise from one part with respect to a *distinct* aggregate? 'Datta' is a part of many names; how does it recall *this* one rather than another? (The answer is implicit in 2.357's kesamcit — 'in the case of some': the recall is occasion-relative, context supplying what the part cannot, per 2.315-316's fourteen factors.) Second, and deeper: katham pratiyamanah syat sabdo 'rthasya abhidhayakah — how could a word that is *being cognized* be the expressor of a meaning? If the recalled whole is itself an object of cognition — something the hearer is aware *of* — then it stands as content, not as vehicle; and a content does not express. This is 2.57's problem (the word must be grasped to convey, yet what is grasped is object not instrument) returning at the movement's heart, and it has no easy answer. Bhartrhari raises it against his own account, which is his habit.

Verse 2.359.translation: But words that are alike to one part, accompanied by these and those marks, arising-in-consequence, stand settled in names.
Verse 2.359.commentary: The reply to 2.358's first objection. Ekadesa-sarupah tu sabdah — words that are *alike to one part* ('datta' as it stands in 'Devadatta') — taih taih lingaih samanvitah: accompanied by *these and those marks* — the indicators that context supplies (2.315's linga) — anunispadinah: *arising-in-consequence* (2.121's term: the entailed, not the expressed) — samjnasu samavasthitah: stand settled *in* the names. So the part-like word inside a name is not a word doing word-work; it is an anunispadin, something that comes along, marked by whatever the occasion marks it with, lodged in the name without office. And the resolution of 2.358's first question falls out: the memory reaches *this* aggregate rather than another because the marks accompanying the part are these and not those — the linga discriminates where the part cannot. The vocabulary is precise: what lives inside a name is not a constituent but a resident.

Verse 2.360.translation: Being common, they are doubtful; by capacity, they have a fixed substratum; among them, those that are correct — for those, elision and the rest is taught in the science.
Verse 2.360.commentary: The status of these residents, in one verse. Sadharanatvat sandigdhah — being *common* (the same syllables occurring in many names), they are *doubtful*: taken alone, no part tells you which name it belongs to. Samarthyat niyatasrayah — by *capacity*, they have a *fixed substratum*: what they can do, in a given case, fixes what they belong to (2.339's samarthyat vikalpyate: capacity resolving ambiguity). And then the science's interest: tesam ye sadhavah tesu sastre lopadi sisyate — among them, *those that are correct*: for *those*, elision and the rest is taught in the science. Here is the sharp point. The grammar's operations (elision, augment, sandhi) apply to some of these residents and not others — and the criterion is sadhutva, *correctness* (1.27's dharma-bearing property). So the parts inside names are sorted by the science into those it will operate on and those it will not, and the sorting is a posting of the sastra, not a discovery about the name. Which parts of your name the grammar recognizes is a fact about the grammar.

Verse 2.361.translation: The consequence being equal, 'jye', 'dra', 'gha' are incorrect; for there is no analytic teaching in the science with respect to them, as there is a memory in the case of 'datta' and the like.
Verse 2.361.commentary: The sorting of 2.360 exhibited. Tulyayam anunispattau — the *consequence being equal*: 'jye', 'dra', 'gha' arise inside names exactly as 'datta' does, by the same mechanism, with the same standing as residents — and yet asadhavah, they are *incorrect*: the grammar will not operate on them. Why? Na hi anvakhyayakam sastre tesu — there is no *analytic teaching* in the science with respect to them — dattadivat smrtih: as there *is* a memory (a recollection-relation, 2.357) in the case of 'datta' and the like. The difference is not in the syllables and not in their mode of occurrence; it is that 'datta' is independently a word (so a memory can reach a whole through it, 2.357) and 'jye' is not (so no memory, no analytic treatment, no correctness). The verse thus completes the movement's demonstration: what makes a part of a name analysable is not its being a part but its being, elsewhere, a word — which is to say, the analysis of names borrows entirely from the lexicon and finds nothing in the name itself.
Verse 2.361.contested: the three specimens are our reading of the transcription; the class intended — syllable-sequences occurring in names that the grammar declines to treat as elements — is clear from the contrast with dattadi

Verse 2.362.translation: And those words which have retroflexion effected, permanently — 'kharanasa' and the like — because they teach a single substance, they call correct.
Verse 2.362.commentary: A further class, and its criterion is startling. Some words have krta-natva nityam — retroflexion *permanently* effected: the sandhi-change is baked into the word, no longer derivable, simply part of its shape (kharanasa, 'ass-nosed', where the n has become n and stays so). And the ground on which the science calls them sadhu: eka-dravya-upadesitvat — *because they teach a single substance*: the word names one thing, individually, as a name does. So correctness here is conferred by *referential singularity* — the word is proper, it picks out one, and therefore its frozen phonology is licensed rather than corrupt. The criterion is worth pausing on. Sadhutva was defined in Kanda I as a dharma-bearing property established by the sistas' usage (1.27, 1.141), and here it turns out to track whether a form names an individual. Names get grammatical amnesty. What would be a corruption in a common noun is correct in a proper one, because the proper noun's business is done.

Verse 2.363.translation: But they call those very things gotra-names, from their connection with the capacity of a name; among them, dependence on an occasioning ground is not necessarily accepted in respect of their own meaning.
Verse 2.363.commentary: The doctrine of 2.362 given its category. Those forms are called gotranti — gotra-names, lineage-names (the patronymics and clan-designations, which the grammar treats as a class) — samjna-sakti-samanvayat: from their connection with the *capacity of a name*. And the consequence that matters: nimitta-apeksanam tesu svarthe na avasyam isyate — among them, dependence on an *occasioning ground* is *not necessarily* accepted in respect of their own meaning. A gotra-name does not need a reason. 'Garga' does not have to mean anything about the Gargas; it names them, and the naming needs no ground beyond itself. This is the limit-case of the whole kanda's semantics: everywhere else meaning is carved by nimittas (2.250-251, 2.314-316), and here is a class of words that mean without any nimitta at all — pure reference, ungrounded, working perfectly. Names are where language touches things without saying anything about them.

Verse 2.364.translation: The restriction of names upon a name-bearer is sometimes for the sake of practice; but the connection is eternal indeed, in 'Dittha' and the like, as in 'cow' and the like.
Verse 2.364.commentary: The two-level account of naming, and it resolves an apparent tension. Vyavaharaya niyamah samjnanam samjnini kvacit: the *restriction* of names upon their bearers is *sometimes for the sake of practice* — this child is called Dittha because we needed to call him something; the assignment is conventional, dated, ours. But: nitya eva tu sambandhah ditthadisu gavadivat — the *connection* is *eternal indeed* in 'Dittha' and the like, as in 'cow' and the like. Both are true and they are about different things. The *assignment* (which bearer gets which name) is practice; the *connection* (that this word means this) is eternal, exactly as with 'gauh' — the nitya sambandha of 1.23 holding for proper names no less than common ones. Naming a child does not create a word-meaning bond; it deploys one. Which is why the reference works the instant it is conferred, and why 'Dittha' does not have to be learned twice.

Verse 2.365.translation: And in this science, the connection of 'vrddhi' and the rest, whose mark is the delimitation of capacity, is non-artificial — like that of qualifier and qualified.
Verse 2.365.commentary: The doctrine of 2.364 extended to the grammar's own technical terms, and this is a substantial claim. Vrddhy-adinam ca sastre 'smin — of 'vrddhi' and the rest in *this science* (Panini's technical vocabulary: vrddhi, guna, ti, ghu, the artificial names the Astadhyayi coins) — sakty-avaccheda-laksanah abhisambandhah akrtrimah: the connection, whose mark is the *delimitation of capacity*, is *non-artificial* — visesana-visesyavat, like that of qualifier and qualified. The claim is that even coined technical terms are not arbitrary labels: the connection between 'vrddhi' and the vowels it names is akrtrima, not made — it is a delimitation of the word's own capacity (2.250's sarvarthya carved by nimitta, here by the science's definitional act). This is 1.59's point (grammar's technical terms rest on svarupa) given its semantics: the sastra does not invent bonds, it *selects* from the plenitude. Even the most artificial-looking naming in the language is a carving, not a fabrication.

Verse 2.366.translation: Resting on the own-form of the name, some worldly (name) operates when the occasioning ground is present; some operates even in the absence of the ground.
Verse 2.366.commentary: The taxonomy of names by their dependence on grounds. Samjna-svarupam asritya — resting on the name's own form — laukiki kacit pravartate nimitte sati: some *worldly* name operates when the occasioning *ground is present*: 'the cook' names him while he cooks; remove the cooking and the name lapses (2.158's criterion: the word's employment ceases when its meaning departs). Kacid nimitta-asannidhav api: some operates *even in the absence* of the ground — 'the cook' as a man's name, sticking to him at rest, in old age, in his grave. The same form, two regimes: ground-dependent and ground-free, and only usage tells you which is running. This is the distinction the tradition would formalize as yaugika (etymologically grounded) versus rudha (conventionalized), and its interest here is that both are *laukiki*, worldly: the difference is not between a real meaning and a lapsed one but between two ways the plenitude is carved — by a live ground, or by settled currency (2.253's prasiddhi doing the work once the ground has gone).

Verse 2.367.translation: But in the science, a great name is grounded in the own-form; the inference of the occasioning ground is understood in the case of proximity.
Verse 2.367.commentary: The scientific names contrasted with the worldly ones of 2.366. Sastre tu mahati samjna svarupa-upanibandhana — in the science, a *great* name (the major technical terms) is grounded in the *own-form*: it means by exhibiting its shape (1.59-68's svam rupam, the grammar's whole metalinguistic apparatus). And: anumanam nimittasya sannidhane pratiyate — the *inference* of the occasioning ground is understood in the case of *proximity*: where the term is used near its occasion, one can infer why it was coined so. But only infer, and only there. The technical term does not carry its ground; the ground is reconstructible, sometimes, from context — which is 2.170's etymological under-determination in the sastra's own vocabulary: the grammarians dispute why Panini chose 'ghu' or 'ti' precisely because the terms do not say. Their meaning is their delimitation (2.365); their motivation is guesswork.
Verse 2.367.contested: mahati samjna ('a great name') is obscure — we take it as the science's major technical terms; some read it as 'a name of wide scope' (covering many items), which fits svarupa-nibandhana less well

Verse 2.368.translation: Or an inference from repetition is understood there, from likeness of form; or an inference of a difference of words, or the ascertaining of a difference of capacity.
Verse 2.368.commentary: The alternatives for what the 'inference' of 2.367 actually delivers, and the disarray is familiar. Avrtteh anumanam va — an inference from *repetition*: seeing the term recur, one infers its ground from the pattern — sarupyat tatra gamyate, understood there from likeness of form. Or sabda-bheda-anumanam va — an inference of a *difference of words*: the recurrence is not one term but two homonyms, and one infers the split. Or sakti-bhedasya va gatih — or the *ascertaining of a difference of capacity*: one term, two capacities, carved by the contexts (2.250's doctrine). Three accounts of what the grammarian is doing when he reasons from a technical term's occurrences, and they disagree about the most basic question — how many terms are there? This is 2.259's disarray (how many verses in the repetition?) in the metalanguage, and the diagnosis is the same: identity and number are conferred by office (2.104, 2.258), and where the office is disputed, the count is.

Verse 2.369.translation: Sometimes the artificial one stands settled by difference of domain; in the case of number, the settling has a single domain, even for both.
Verse 2.369.commentary: How artificial and worldly names divide their territory. Kvacid visaya-bhedena krtrima vyavatisthate: sometimes the *artificial* (technical) name stands settled by *difference of domain* — the technical sense and the ordinary sense coexist by occupying different territories: 'vrddhi' in grammar names three vowels, 'vrddhi' in the world names growth, and no one collides. But: samkhyayam eka-visayam vyavasthanam dvayor api — in the case of *number*, the settling has a *single* domain, even for both: the technical term for number and the ordinary word for number are about the same thing; the domains coincide, and the two names of the same territory must be disambiguated some other way. The verse marks a real complication in 2.366-368's tidy picture: the standard method (domain-separation) fails wherever the science's subject matter overlaps the world's. And number is exactly such a place — everyone counts, and the grammar counts too.

Verse 2.370.translation: Uttering, in some case, even the artificial one's domain, the worldly one pervades; from a distance, in the vocative, the grasping of the two is thus.
Verse 2.370.commentary: The movement's closing observation, and it is a scene. Visayam krtrimasya api laukikah kvacid uccaran vyapnoti: the *worldly* name, uttered, sometimes *pervades* even the *artificial* one's domain — ordinary usage floods into the technical territory and covers it. And the illustration: durat sambuddhau tatha hi grahanam tayoh — *from a distance*, in the *vocative*, the grasping of the two is thus. Someone shouts a name across a field. At that distance, with the wind, the technical and the ordinary are not distinguished — the hearer grasps whichever his situation supplies, and the vocative, which is pure summons, carries no marks to sort them. The image closes the movement on the note the whole kanda has sounded: at the edge of audibility, where form gives out, nothing remains but occasion and the trained mind — and that is not a degenerate case but the ordinary one seen clearly. Every hearing is a hearing from a distance.
Verse 2.370.contested: the verse is elliptical and our reading of the vocative-at-a-distance as the illustration follows tatha hi; the point (worldly usage overrunning technical domains where marks are unavailable) is secure

Verse 2.371.translation: In groups, single-remainders and dvandvas, some assert a standing of the actions substratum by substratum, whose mark is capacity.
Verse 2.371.commentary: A movement returns to the distribution-question of 2.221-225, now with the machinery of the intervening argument available. In sangha, ekasesa and dvandva — the three ways language gathers many into one form (2.108's single-remainder, 2.221's dvandva) — kecid: some assert pratyasrayam avasthanam kriyanam, a *standing of the actions substratum by substratum*: the action lodges separately in each member. And the mark by which they identify such cases: samarthya-laksanam — *capacity* is the criterion: what could the action do here? An action that can only be done to one at a time distributes; one that can take a group whole does not. The criterion is functional rather than formal, and it is the right one: nothing in the dvandva's shape says whether the eating reaches each or the group. Only what eating *is* decides. The verses following work this out with a precision that is among the most careful in the work.

Verse 2.372.translation: Eating is completed in each single one, by way of its fruit and its form; for on any other settlement, its purpose is not construed.
Verse 2.372.commentary: The test applied to the clearest case. Bhojanam phala-rupabhyam ekaikasmin samapyate: eating is *completed in each single one*, by way of its *fruit* and its *form* — its form (the taking of food) and its fruit (satisfaction) are both realized per eater, not per group. And the argument: anyatha hi vyavasthane na tadarthah prakalpyate — on any other settlement, its *purpose* is not construed: an eating that fed the group without feeding the members would feed nobody; the purpose fails unless it distributes. This is the samarthya-criterion (2.371) doing exactly what it should — reasoning from what the action is *for* to how it must distribute (2.113's prayojana, now determining syntax). Note that nothing linguistic settles this: 'Rama and Krsna eat' and 'Rama and Krsna surround the house' have the same form, and only the nature of eating and surrounding tells you that the first distributes and the second cannot.

Verse 2.373.translation: And all of them attain, each severally, the eating whose form is the taking of food and whose fruit is satisfaction — not, however, as with a dramatic performance.
Verse 2.373.commentary: The contrast that makes 2.372 precise. Sarve pratyekam pratipadyante bhujim — all attain the eating *each severally*: anna-adana-adi-rupam, its form being the taking of food; trpti-phalam, its fruit being satisfaction. Both form and fruit are per-person. Na tu natya-kriyam iva: *not, however, as with a dramatic performance* — and the counter-example is beautifully chosen. A troupe performs a play: the performing is genuinely the group's, no member performs the play, each does a part and the play happens between them; and its fruit (the drama, the rasa) is not divisible among the actors at all. So natya is an action that *cannot* distribute — the group is its only possible substratum — where bhoji cannot help distributing. Two actions, two behaviours, and the difference is in the actions, not in the grammar. The verse gives the tradition a working typology of collective versus distributive predicates, arrived at by asking what each action's fruit is and to whom it accrues.

Verse 2.374-375.translation: (374) Like water-for-the-feet, it stands by division, from capacity; the eating effects the eating-purpose — not by single-performance, as a lamp does. (375) But an action such as seeing, though single, with respect to objects of that sort, may rest on the aggregate even without repetition.
Verse 2.374-375.commentary: The typology given its two poles and its explanatory principle. (374): padyavat — like *water for the feet*, which must be given to each guest severally (one basin does not wash all) — bhujih vibhagena samarthyat avatisthate: the eating stands by *division*, from capacity. And the crucial denial: na tantrena pradipavat — *not by single-performance, as a lamp does*: one lamp lights a whole assembly at once, discharging its office for all by a single operation (tantra, 2.108's ritual device, here as a natural category). So actions sort into the padya-type (one per recipient) and the pradipa-type (one for all). (375) gives the other pole: drsyadis tu kriya eka api — an action such as *seeing*, though *single* — tathabhutesu karmasu, with respect to objects of that sort — samudaya-asraya bhavet avrttim antarena api: may rest on the *aggregate* even *without repetition*: one seeing takes in the whole group; no re-seeing per member. The principle throughout is the same: how an action distributes over a plurality is fixed by the action's own nature, and language merely reports it. The lamp, which has served the work as the model of manifestation (1.94, 2.298), here serves as the model of collective efficacy — the same property under another aspect.

Verse 2.376.translation: In the seeing of practice and the like, of those whose forms of activity are distinct — the agents' seeing is distinct, though jointly accomplishing the purpose.
Verse 2.376.commentary: A third pattern, and it complicates the binary. Consider a joint undertaking — vyavahara-adi-darsane, the seeing of a transaction and the like (a lawsuit, a trade, a rite performed by several officiants): the participants have bhinna-vyapara-rupanam, *distinct forms of activity* — each does something different. Their kartrnam darsanam bhinnam: the agents' seeing is *distinct* — nobody's view of the transaction is anybody else's; each sees his own part. And yet: sambhuya arthasya sadhakam — *jointly accomplishing* the purpose: the transaction happens, and it happens by their several distinct activities converging. So here the action neither distributes wholly (each does not do the whole thing) nor collects wholly (there is no single group-act over their heads): the members do different things and the purpose is one. This is the structure of cooperation, and it is a third category the padya/pradipa binary misses — which is exactly why 2.371's criterion was samarthya, capacity, and not a formal rule: capacities are as various as actions, and no dichotomy exhausts them.

Verse 2.377-378.translation: (377) Because what is to be characterized is established in the world, and because the mark is seen in the science, and from need — the name 'vrddhi' is completed by way of the sugarcane-difference. (378) In the taking of a hundred, from principality; in a punishing whose object is a hundred — though the needers differ in their subordinate elements, the countable object is not differentiated.
Verse 2.377-378.commentary: Two technical cases testing the distribution-doctrine on quantities and names. (377) is a grammarian's puzzle about how the technical name vrddhi reaches its instances — laksyasya loka-siddhatvat, what is to be characterized being established in the world; sastre lingasya darsanat, the mark being seen in the science; arthitvat, from need — and the resolution comes aiksu-bhedena, 'by way of the sugarcane-difference' (the traditional example of a taddhita-formation whose derivation is disputed). (378) is sharper: sata-adane pradhanatvat — in the *taking of a hundred*, from principality; danda ne sata-karmake — in a punishing whose object is a hundred: arthinam guna-bhede 'pi samkhyeyo 'rtho na bhidyate — though the *needers* differ in their subordinate elements, the *countable object is not differentiated*. Fine a hundred men, and the hundred is one hundred however variously they are fined. The number is a property of the group and does not distribute, even where the punishing does. So distribution can split within a single predication: the action reaching each, the count reaching only the whole.
Verse 2.377-378.contested: 377 is among the most compressed and technically opaque verses in the kanda; aiksu-bhedena and the vrddhi-samjna's completion refer to grammatical disputes not recoverable from the karika, and our rendering translates the evident syntax while leaving the case unreconstructed

Verse 2.379.translation: Since it is the group alone that is to be enjoined, as in the conveying of an operation — there the connection is by single-performance, for the compound-name and the reduplicated-name.
Verse 2.379.commentary: The tantra-model (2.374's lamp) applied to grammar's own naming. Sanghasya eva vidheyatvat — since it is the *group alone* that is to be enjoined (the rule addresses the collection, not its members) — karyavat pratipadane, as in the conveying of an operation: tatra tantrena sambandhah — there the connection is by *single-performance*: one act of naming reaches all at once. And the instances: samasa-abhyasta-samjnayoh — for the *compound*-name and the *reduplicated*-name: Panini's terms samasa and abhyasta name classes, and the naming does not repeat per member; one posting covers the class. This is the pradipa-pattern in the metalanguage: a definition is a lamp, not a basin. The verse quietly makes a point about rule-systems generally — the economy that makes grammar possible (2.343's laghava) is precisely the availability of tantra: one statement discharging indefinitely many applications. A science in which every naming had to distribute would be as long as its subject matter.

Verse 2.380-382.translation: (380) Those whose praise has characterization for its purpose, with respect to some action — by them, taken severally and taken together, that property is indicated. (381) As in 'sudras must not enter this house', the entry of them severally and of them combined is prohibited. (382) But in the establishing of a prohibition on the seeking of gain and the like jointly — since it is not prohibited severally, engagement is not contradicted.
Verse 2.380-382.commentary: The distribution-question in prohibitions, where the stakes are practical. (380) states the principle: where praise or blame serves *characterization* — laksanartha stutih, marking out a property — the property is indicated by the terms vyastaih ca samastaih ca: *severally and together* both. (381) illustrates: vrsalair na pravestavyam — 'sudras must not enter this house' — pratyekam samhatanam ca pravesah pratisidhyate: the entry of them *severally* and *combined* is prohibited: the prohibition distributes and collects at once; no sudra may enter, and no group of them may. (382) gives the contrasting case: where a prohibition is established on artha-lipsa-adi *jointly* — on seeking gain *in concert* — prthag apratisiddhatvat pravrttir na virudhyate: since it is *not prohibited severally*, individual engagement is not contradicted: one may seek gain alone if only conspiracy was barred. The pair is a piece of practical jurisprudence, and its lesson is the movement's: whether a provision distributes is not readable from its form. It is readable from what it is *for* — and getting it wrong is, in the second case, the difference between a conspiracy and a livelihood.

Verse 2.383.translation: Because the purpose is the characterizing of intervention, 'at', 'ku', 'pu', 'an' and the like — retroflexion is not prohibited either severally or by all of them taken together.
Verse 2.383.commentary: The principle of 2.380-382 applied to the grammar's most notorious rule. Panini's at-ku-pv-an-numvyavaye 'pi (8.4.2) governs natva, the retroflexion of n: the change occurs even when certain sounds *intervene* — the vowels, the gutturals, the labials, and so forth, listed as at, ku, pu, an. The question the verse settles: does the list block retroflexion when its members intervene severally, or only when they intervene all together? Answer: vyavaya-laksanarthatvat — because the purpose is the *characterizing of intervention* — anatvam na pratisidhyate pratyekam va samastaih va: retroflexion is not prohibited either *severally* or *by all of them taken together*. The rule is permissive in both directions: any of them may intervene, in any combination, and the change still runs. The reasoning is 2.380's exactly — the list's purpose is to characterize a class of interveners, not to specify a configuration — and the verse's placement shows why the movement has been so patient: the distribution-question is not scholastic decoration. It decides whether a fundamental sandhi rule fires.

Verse 2.384.translation: When the eating is undertaken for the sake of favouring the eaters — it does not favour them, without a difference of place, time and the rest.
Verse 2.384.commentary: A refinement on 2.372's distribution, and it turns on individuation. Anugrahartham bhoktrnam bhujir arabhyate yada — when the eating is undertaken for the sake of *favouring* the eaters (the host's purpose: to benefit them) — desa-kala-adi-abhedena na anugrhnati tan asau: it does *not favour them* without a difference of *place, time and the rest*. To benefit each eater, the eating must be individuated per eater — this eater, here, now, with his own portion. An undifferentiated eating benefits nobody, because benefit accrues to individuals in circumstances. So the distribution 2.372 established by the action's fruit is here shown to require the individuating apparatus (desa, kala — the same factors that carve meanings at 2.315-316, here carving events). The verse is a small but exact point: collective predicates that carry benefit must distribute, because benefit is not a thing a group can receive on its members' behalf.

Verse 2.385.translation: That single thing whose manifoldness is taught by difference of vessels and the like; or, in the reverse case, the oneness of what is differentiated is constructed.
Verse 2.385.commentary: Individuation shown to run both ways, and the verse is deliberately symmetrical. Patradi-bhedat nanatvam yasya ekasya upadisyate: that *single* thing whose *manifoldness* is taught by difference of *vessels* and the like — one substance in several pots, and the pots make it many (the ghee is 'the ghees' if it is in three jars, for ritual purposes of counting). Viparyaye va bhinnasya tasya ekatvam prakalpyate: or *in the reverse case*, the *oneness* of what is *differentiated* is constructed — many things gathered as one by a single vessel, a single occasion, a single office. Number is conferred by the individuating conditions, in either direction, and neither the one nor the many is the substance's own. This is 2.104's capacity-doctrine applied to countability itself: as wordhood is an office worn by stretches of the stream, so unity and plurality are offices worn by stuff, conferred by vessels, and the grammar's number-apparatus reports the conferral rather than discovering a count.

Verse 2.386-387.translation: (386) And those who, though acting in combination, are conveyed by way of difference, eat jointly what has been obtained severally, each his own food by division. (387) Since there is no domain for distributive repetition, and because of contradiction with another number, and because a two-fold completion is not possible — the hundred stands upon the group.
Verse 2.386-387.commentary: Two verses of exact analysis on the mixed case. (386): people samhatya kurvanah, acting *in combination* — a shared meal — are nonetheless bhedena pratipaditah, conveyed by way of *difference*; and the description is precise: svam svam bhojyam vibhagena praptam sambhuya bhunjate — each *his own* food, obtained *by division*, they eat *jointly*. Joint in occasion, several in substance and fruit: the structure of every shared meal, and language reports both aspects at once without strain. (387) then handles the count: vipsayah visaya-abhavat — there being no domain for *distributive repetition* (no 'a hundred each'); virodhat anya-samkhyaya — from *contradiction* with another number (a hundred distributed would yield some other total); dvidha samapty-ayogat ca — and because a *two-fold completion* is not possible (the count cannot both distribute and collect): satam sanghe 'vatisthate — the *hundred stands upon the group*. Three independent arguments converging on one conclusion, and the conclusion is 2.378's: actions may distribute where their counts cannot.

Verse 2.388.translation: Where, by the eating-dvandva and the single-remainder, it is taught along with others — there too, because the purpose is characterization, the sentence is completed two-fold.
Verse 2.388.commentary: The movement's last technical case, and it admits a complication the earlier analysis suppressed. Bhuji-dvandva-ekasesabhyam yatra anyaih saha sisyate — where, by the eating-dvandva and the ekasesa, something is taught *along with others*: the two gathering-devices operating together (2.108's single-remainder inside a dvandva) — tatrapi laksanarthatvat dvidha vakyam samapyate: *there too*, because the purpose is *characterization* (2.380's principle), the sentence is completed *two-fold*. The sentence closes twice — once distributively, once collectively — and both closures are real. This is 2.387's dvidha samapty-ayoga (a two-fold completion is not possible) apparently contradicted, and the contradiction is only apparent: there, of *number*, which cannot complete twice; here, of *characterization*, which can. The verse is a fine demonstration of how carefully the movement has been discriminating: what completes twice and what cannot depends on what kind of thing is completing, and no general rule about sentences settles it.

Verse 2.389.translation: By some, a completion of the several sentences is accepted, each severally; by that, sentences joined with another form are comprehended.
Verse 2.389.commentary: The doctrine of 2.388 generalized into a position. Kaiscid isyate: some accept vakyantaranam pratyekam samaptih — a *completion of the several sentences, each severally*: where a compressed utterance carries several predications (the dvandva with its distributed action, the ekasesa with its several referents), it is not one sentence with a complex meaning but *several sentences*, each complete. And the consequence: tena rupantarena yuktanam vakyanam sangrahah — by that, sentences *joined with another form* are comprehended: the several sentences are grasped through the single form that gathers them. So the ekasesa's one uttered word (2.108-109) is, on this account, several sentences wearing one body — 2.110's doctrine (one form, many sentences) stated as a semantics rather than a ritual convenience. The position is available to Bhartrhari and he does not quite claim it; but it fits everything he has argued about offices and bodies (2.104), and it explains the two-fold completion of 2.388 without paradox: two completions because two sentences, sharing a form.

Verse 2.390.translation: The difference-sentences are not things expressible by the sentence, any of them; but when that has been uttered, one comprehends those differences and others so.
Verse 2.390.commentary: The movement's closing correction, and it pulls the doctrine of 2.389 back into the kanda's frame. Na vakyasya abhidheyani bheda-vakyani kanicit: the *difference-sentences* — the several predications the analysis distributes out of the compressed form — are *not things expressible by the sentence*, not any of them: the utterance does not *say* them; they are not its content. But: tasmin tu uccarite bhedan tatha anyan pratipadyate — when *that* has been uttered, one comprehends those differences, *and others*, so. The distributed predications are what the hearer *gets*, not what the sentence *says* — and 'and others' (anyan) concedes that he gets more besides, unpredictably, per hearer (2.299's overspill; 2.135's division per own-cognition). This is the kanda's settled position applied one last time: the utterance occasions a flash whose articulation the analyst can spread across several sentences, and none of those sentences is what was said. What was said was one thing. What arrived was one thing. The several sentences are the ledger.

Verse 2.391.translation: For those for whom the entire sentence-meaning is completed at each division — for them, at that moment, of what use is the existence of a separate word-meaning?
Verse 2.391.commentary: The objection turned back on the holist, and it is a sharp one. If, as 2.18 held, the krtsnah vakyarthah samapyate pratibhedam — the *entire* sentence-meaning completes at *each* division, each word — then tesam tadanim bhinnasya kim padarthasya sattaya: at that moment, what use is the *existence* of a separate word-meaning? The whole being present at every word, the word-meanings are not merely non-constitutive (which the kanda has argued at length) but *idle* — they do nothing, explain nothing, and might as well not be. The objection is a reductio of the holist's own most radical thesis, and it deserves its force: a doctrine on which the parts contribute nothing owes an account of why speech has parts at all. The verses following do not dodge it; 2.394-397 will answer with the sadharana-doctrine, and the answer will be that the word-meanings are real as *shares* in a common thing rather than as contributions to a sum.

Verse 2.392-393.translation: (392) Now if that meaning, generated by those very ones, operates upon the differentiated — then, of the prior meaning, there would be either contradiction with it, or co-standing. (393) On co-standing, there would be contradictoriness of the qualified and the unqualified; but a deviating connection follows, on the abandonment of the meaning.
Verse 2.392-393.commentary: The objection of 2.391 pressed into a dilemma. Suppose the sentence-meaning, generated by the word-meanings, then *operates upon the differentiated* — comes back to bear on the very items that produced it. Then the prior meaning (the word-meaning as it stood before) either contradicts the new one or co-stands with it. (393) takes both horns. On co-standing: viruddhata syad visista-avisistayoh — there is *contradictoriness of the qualified and the unqualified*: the word-meaning as bare (before the sentence) and as qualified (after) cannot both stand, being incompatible determinations of one item. On abandonment: vyabhicari tu sambandhah tyage 'rthasya prasajyate — a *deviating connection* follows: if the bare meaning is abandoned, then the word's connection with its meaning is not invariable, and the eternal bond of 1.23 and 2.332 fails. Contradiction or deviation: the dilemma is real, and it is the same one 2.15 posed against the deposit-theorist (the general obscured in the particular: where does it go?), now aimed at the holist. Bhartrhari has arranged for his own position to face the argument he used against everyone else.

Verse 2.394.translation: A single common expressible, standing in each word, is the meaning-self in the group and in the group's members — indicating the proximity.
Verse 2.394.commentary: The answer, and it introduces the model that will carry the movement: sadharana, the *common*. Ekah sadharano vacyah — a *single common* expressible — pratisabdam avasthitah: standing *in each word*: not divided among them, not summed from them, but present in each as a common thing is present to each of its sharers. Sanghe sanghisu ca arthatma: it is the meaning-self *in the group and in the group's members* — both, without division. And its office: sannidhana-nidarsakah — *indicating the proximity*: it is what makes the words' co-presence into a togetherness rather than a heap. The model dissolves 2.392-393's dilemma at a stroke. There is no prior bare meaning that must be either contradicted or abandoned, because the word never had a meaning of its own to lose — it had a *share* in a common one, and shares are not diminished by being shared. What the word-meaning *is*, on this account, is the sentence-meaning as standing in this word: 2.391's 'idle' padartha turns out to be the vakyartha itself, seen from where one word stands.

Verse 2.395-396.translation: (395) As, in common property, ownership, and the fruit of relinquishing, and the satisfaction, are undiminished in the wealth — so the connection is with the meaning, not with its possessor. (396) In the meaningfulness of phonemes, by that very meaning in the possessor, oneness with the aggregate stands by way of difference.
Verse 2.395-396.commentary: The sadharana-model given its illustration, and it is drawn from law. Consider sadharana dhana — *common property*, held jointly by brothers. Each has svatva, *ownership* — full ownership, not a fraction of it; each has tyagasya phalam, the *fruit of relinquishing* (the merit of giving it away — and each gets the whole merit, not a share); each has pritih, *satisfaction* — avikala, *undiminished*. The wealth is one, the ownings are several, and no owning is a portion. This is the exact structure the holist needs: the sentence-meaning is one, the words' meanings are several, and each is the whole thing owned from a position, not a slice. And the doctrinal formulation: sambandho 'rthe na tadvata — the connection is *with the meaning*, not with its *possessor*: what a word is bonded to is the meaning itself, not to the word-as-meaning-possessor. (396) applies it downward: phonemes' meaningfulness, likewise, is oneness with the aggregate standing bhedena, by way of difference — each phoneme owning the word's meaning from where it stands. The joint-property analogy is one of the most illuminating in the work: it explains how many can each have all of one thing, which is precisely what the flash-doctrine requires and what every deposit-semantics finds impossible.

Verse 2.397.translation: By a single lamp alone, all see the common wealth; likewise, by a single case-ending, number is expressed.
Verse 2.397.commentary: The lamp returns to seal the sadharana-model, and the pairing of images is exact. Ekena eva pradipena sarve sadharanam dhanam pasyanti: by *one* lamp, *all* see the *common* wealth — one illumination, many seers, one object, and nobody's seeing is a fraction of anybody's. Tadvat ekena supa samkhya abhidhiyate: *likewise*, by a *single case-ending*, number is expressed. The application is precise: the ending does not distribute a number-contribution among the words it governs; it lights the whole and each sees it entire (2.375's collective seeing, 2.374's lamp as the tantra-model, now doing semantic work). Between the joint-property (2.395) and the lamp, the movement has its two figures for how one thing can be wholly many's: ownership without division, and illumination without division. Both were needed. Property answers 'how can each have all?'; the lamp answers 'how does one act reach all at once?' — and together they explain how a single utterance delivers one undivided meaning to every word in it and every hearer of it.

Verse 2.398.translation: Meaningfulness is not thus distinguished in the word, in the phoneme, and in the sentence; but from practice, a different commencement is seen, as though contradictory.
Verse 2.398.commentary: The consequence of the sadharana-model for the whole architecture of grains. Na arthavatta pade varne vakye ca evam visisyate: meaningfulness is *not distinguished* in the word, in the phoneme, and in the sentence — not, that is, in the way the analysis supposes, with each grain owning its own quantum. On the common-property model there is one meaning and three positions from which it is held, and 'meaningfulness' names the same thing at every grain. Then the concession: abhyasat prakramo 'nyah tu viruddha iva drsyate — but *from practice*, a *different commencement* is seen, *as though* contradictory: usage proceeds as if the grains had distinct meanings, and the appearance of conflict between the grains (the word's meaning versus the sentence's) is an artifact of the practice, not of the semantics. Iva, as always, holds the line. The kanda has now answered 2.391's challenge fully: word-meanings exist, are not idle, and are not contributions — they are the one meaning, owned from a place.

Verse 2.399.translation: Without application, a word is not the illuminator of its own meaning; they call the connection with the expressing of meaning a door.
Verse 2.399.commentary: A new movement, on what activates a word, and its thesis is stated at once. Viniyogad rte sabdo na svarthasya prakasakah: *without application* — viniyoga, the ritual and grammatical term for deploying an element to an office (2.258's vinyoga individuating mantras) — a word is *not the illuminator of its own meaning*. An unapplied word illuminates nothing; it sits inert, a form without office. And the figure: artha-abhidhana-sambandham uktidvaram pracaksate — the connection with the expressing of meaning they call a *door*: the bond between word and meaning is a doorway, standing open, through which nothing passes until someone walks. The image is exactly calibrated to the kanda's position. The eternal connection (1.23, 2.332) is real and is not sufficient: it is a standing possibility, a door, and utterance with intent is the walking through. This is why the plenitude (2.250) needs nimittas, why the lamp needs bringing (2.397), why the word means everything and says one thing.

Verse 2.400.translation: As the eye, when directed, becomes fit for seeing — so a word, when intended, becomes the expressor of a meaning.
Verse 2.400.commentary: The doctrine of 2.399 given its simile, and the simile is chosen with great care. Yatha pranihitam caksuh darsanaya upakalpate: as the eye, when *directed* — pranihita, aimed, laid upon its object — becomes *fit* for seeing. The eye is always an eye; open, it is capable of every visible thing; and it sees *this* because it was turned this way. Tatha abhisamhitah sabdo bhavaty arthasya vacakah: so a word, when *intended* — abhisamhita, aimed by the speaker's intent — becomes the expressor of *a* meaning. Every element of the kanda's semantics is in the analogy. The word's omni-significance is the eye's openness to all colours (2.250's sarvarthya); the intention is the aiming; the meaning that results is what was in the line of sight — carved from the plenitude by a directing, not selected from a list. And the simile explains, incidentally, why the word's capacity is not diminished by use: an eye that has looked at a pot is no less able to look at a cloth. The vivaksa of 1.155 has become, here, the whole engine of signification.

Verse 2.401.translation: The connection of instrument and object is seen as mediated by an action; therefore the fixing of expression is of the expression and the expressible.
Verse 2.401.commentary: The structural point behind 2.400's simile. Kriya-vyavetah sambandho drstah karana-karmanoh — the connection of *instrument* and *object* is seen as *mediated by an action*: an axe and a tree are not related until there is a felling; the karaka-relations exist only within an action (2.183: prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise). By parity: abhidha niyamah tasmat abhidhana-abhidheyayoh — the *fixing* of expression is therefore of the *expression and the expressible* — i.e. the word-meaning relation, like the instrument-object relation, is constituted within an act: the act of abhidha, expressing. There is no standing relation between word and meaning waiting to be used; there is a door (2.399) that becomes a relation when someone walks through it. This is the metaphysics behind the eye-simile: relations of this kind are not found but *made*, in and by the act that has them as its terms — which is the Sambandha-samuddesa's thesis (Kanda III) in embryo, and which is why the third kanda must exist.

Verse 2.402.translation: Among many single expressions, all effecting one meaning — the word stands upon that which the speaker intends.
Verse 2.402.commentary: The intention-doctrine's decisive case. Bahusu eka-abhidhanesu sarvesu ekartha-karisu: among *many* single expressions, *all* effecting *one* meaning — the synonyms, several words any of which would do (2.330's paryayah): yat prayokta abhisandhatte sabdah tatra avatisthate — the word stands upon *that which the speaker intends*. Where several words could serve and one is chosen, nothing but the intention distinguishes them — the meaning being identical, the choice is the speaker's and no fact about the language settles it. This is the cleanest possible demonstration that abhisandhi, intention, is doing real work: it is the only variable left when the semantic contribution is held constant. And it explains 2.399's door-figure: the eternal connections stand open for all the synonyms at once, and what determines which door is walked is not the doors.

Verse 2.403-404.translation: (403) In the practice of the Vedic words, some say they are meaningless and operate on their own form alone, in the conveying to others. (404) Because of the difference in the act of expression, which conveys the meaning, those who see them as one consider them (different) by difference of application.
Verse 2.403-404.commentary: The doctrine applied to the recitation of scripture. (403): amnaya-sabdanam abhyase — in the *practice*, the drill, of the Vedic words (the rote recitation by which the Veda is transmitted) — kecid ahuh anarthakan: some say they are *meaningless* — svarupa-matra-vrttin, operating on their *own form alone*: the reciter is not saying anything, he is producing forms; meaning does not enter until the words are deployed in a rite. And (404): the ekatva-darsinah — those who see them as *one* (one word, whether recited or applied) — account for the apparent difference abhidhana-kriya-bhedat and niyoga-bhedat: by the difference of the *expressing act* and of *application*. The two accounts of what happens when a boy chants a hymn he does not understand: either the words are meaningless there (and become meaningful when applied), or they are one and the same words differing only in their office. Both are versions of 2.399: unapplied, a word illuminates nothing — whether one says it is then a different word or a meaningless one is bookkeeping (2.179's licensed double description).

Verse 2.405.translation: Those who deal in manifoldness declare their utter manifoldness — as of dice and the like, from their connection with a single class.
Verse 2.405.commentary: The contrary school, and its analogy is worth examining. Nanatva-vyavaharinah — those who *deal in manifoldness*, whose method is division (2.57's pada-darsins) — tesam atyanta-nanatvam prahuh: declare their *utter manifoldness*: the recited word and the applied word are simply two words, not one word twice. And the simile: aksadinam iva eka-jati-samanvayat — as of *dice* and the like, from their connection with a *single class*: dice are many, and their oneness is only their sharing a kind — there is no one die of which the several are appearances. So words: many tokens, unified by nothing but a common type, no single word standing behind them. The analogy is well chosen for the position and it is exactly what Bhartrhari denies (2.250's one word carved by nimittas). Note that the two schools have now been running in parallel since 2.57, and neither can refute the other by the phenomena — which was 1.74's finding and has never been retracted. What separates them is which primitive they will pay for.

Verse 2.406.translation: Intention arising from employment does not exist in the mantra-words; because their capacity is fixed with respect to their domain, that stands settled there.
Verse 2.406.commentary: A limit-case for the intention-doctrine, and Bhartrhari concedes it. Prayogad abhisandhanam mat-padesu na vidyate: intention arising from employment does *not exist* in the mantra-words — the reciter does not aim them; he utters what is given, and often does not know what it means (2.403's meaningless drill). So 2.400's directed eye is absent. What then makes them mean? Visaye yata-saktitvat sa tu tatra vyavasthitah: because their *capacity is fixed* with respect to their *domain*, it stands settled there — the mantra's power is lodged in it by its office in the rite, independently of any reciter's intention (1.138's mantra-efficacy: form-bound, working whether or not understood). The concession is significant: intention is the engine of ordinary signification (2.400-402) and is *not* the whole story — scripture works without it, its capacities fixed by domain rather than aimed by minds. Which is exactly what a tradition holding an authorless Veda must say (1.144), and Bhartrhari says it rather than forcing the doctrine to cover everything.

Verse 2.407.translation: The knowledge of manifoldness alone comes by the object, the context and the rest; there is never, in any way, an operation of words meant for other meanings upon a different meaning.
Verse 2.407.commentary: The movement's closing thesis, and it is a strict one. Nanatvasya eva samjnanam artha-prakaranadibhih: the *knowledge of manifoldness alone* comes by the object, the context, and the rest — the fourteen factors (2.315-316) tell you *which* meaning is in play, and that is all they do. And the denial that follows: na jatv arthantare vrttih anyarthanam kathamcana — there is *never*, in any way, an operation of words *meant for other meanings* upon a *different* meaning. A word aimed at one meaning does not stray into another; the context does not bend a word's sense, it *identifies* which of the word's senses is being used. The distinction is fine and it is the movement's whole point: context is not a force acting on meanings but a light showing which door was walked through (2.399). This closes the door on any account of figurative language as sense-stretching (2.266's forcing) and it protects the eternal bond (2.332): the word never means what it was not, because everything it could mean, it always could.

Verse 2.408-410.section: The Word as Sentence; Existence and Predication
Verse 2.408-410.translation: (408) But that sentence which has the form of a word, grounded in existence — let this reflection be there, granted, and not in the word that is a member of a sentence. (409) Just as, by meaningless phonemes, a qualified meaning is expressed — so, by meaningless words, a qualified meaning is expressed. (410) But the knowledge that arises in the interval, with respect to the word-meanings, is a means to the comprehension, on account of the non-ascertainment of the commencement.
Verse 2.408-410.commentary: Three verses that state the kanda's architecture one last time, now in terms of existence. (408): a pada-rupam vakyam — a sentence *having the form of a word* (the one-word sentence, 2.270, 2.326) — is astitva-upanibandhanam, *grounded in existence*: its whole predication is the assertion that something is. Grant the reflection *there*, the verse says — kamam vimarsah tatra — and *not* in the word that is a *member* of a sentence: the isolated word that constitutes a sentence has a predication; the word inside a sentence has none. (409) then draws the parity that has been the kanda's constant: as anarthakaih varnaih — by *meaningless phonemes* — a qualified meaning is expressed, so padaih anarthakaih — by *meaningless words*: the words within a sentence are as semantically null as the phonemes within a word, and the sentence means all the same. (410) rescues the intermediate cognitions from that nullity: the knowledge that arises antarale, *in the interval*, concerning the word-meanings, is pratipatteh upayah — a *means* to the comprehension: not a contribution but a ladder (2.38's discardable means; 1.85's productive error), and it is needed prakrama-anavadharanat, because the commencement is not ascertained — because one does not know, mid-utterance, where the whole will land.

Verse 2.411.translation: As each successive meaning-self is accompanied by the prior meanings, so, in other meaning-things, the commingling alone is what is commenced.
Verse 2.411.commentary: The phenomenology of the interval (2.410) described. Purvaih arthaih anugatah — accompanied by the *prior* meanings — yatha arthatma parah parah: each successive meaning-self, one after another, comes attended by what preceded it: no word arrives bare; each is met by a mind already shaped by the words before it. And the conclusion: samsarga eva prakrantah tatha anyesu artha-vastusu — the *commingling alone* is what is *commenced*, in the other meaning-things as well. What is under way through the interval is not a series of meanings but a *commingling* — a single process of fusion whose stages are not stages of a sum. This is why 2.410 could call the interval-cognitions a means without calling them constituents: what they are doing is not accumulating but converging, and 2.143's flash is the convergence, not its total.

Verse 2.412.translation: But when, by some, the means is admitted along with the to-be-accomplished meaning — the re-hearing of the means is only for the sake of fixing the substratum.
Verse 2.412.commentary: A technical case: why sentences state what their verbs already imply. When a verb is uttered, its means are implied (2.183: the action does not arise apart from means-connection; 2.47: the roles come pre-fixed). So why does the sentence bother to name them? Sadhananam punah srutih — the *re-hearing* of the means: adhara-niyama-artha eva — *only for the sake of fixing the substratum*: the verb implies *some* agent, and the noun tells you *which*. So the nouns are not adding the roles; the roles were there in the verb (2.48's architecture). They are specifying the fillers. This is 2.66's structure exactly — vrihi restricting the substance-in-general that yajeta obtained — and it shows the same layering: what the sentence *says* and what it *fixes* are different offices, and most of a sentence's words are doing the second.

Verse 2.413.translation: In the absence of the fixing of the substratum, its implication does not exist; its possibility is from capacity, but the explicit hearing is for the exclusion of others.
Verse 2.413.commentary: The complement to 2.412, and it is a precision about implication. Adhara-niyama-abhavat tad-aksepah na vidyate: in the absence of a *fixing* of the substratum, the *implication* of it does not exist — an unfixed role is not implied at all; there is no floating 'some agent' hovering until named. Sambhavah tasya samarthyat: its *possibility* is from *capacity* — the verb's nature makes an agent possible (an action of that sort could have one). Srutih tu anya-nivrttaye — but the *explicit hearing* is for the *exclusion of others*: naming the agent does not create him, it excludes the alternatives. So the sentence's words are, one and all, exclusion-devices: they narrow a plenitude of possibility that the verb's capacity opened. This is 2.250-251's semantics at the level of syntax — the omni-significant carved by nimittas — and it explains why a sentence's meaning is not the sum of its words: subtraction does not sum.

Verse 2.414.translation: An action, distinct from other actions, having a fixed substratum and means, once commenced — the differences are causes of awakening for the comprehenders.
Verse 2.414.commentary: The result of 2.412-413's exclusion-work, and it recasts the whole sentence as a waking-device. Kriya kriyantarad bhinna — an action *distinct from other actions*, individuated by the exclusions the words performed — niyata-adhara-sadhana, having its substratum and means now *fixed* — prakranta: commenced. And the office of everything that did the fixing: bhedah sambodha-hetavah pratipattrnam — the *differences* are *causes of awakening* for the comprehenders. Sambodha: awakening (2.331's pratibodha-abhyupayah, words as means of awakening). The words' distinctions do not build the meaning; they wake it — narrowing until the hearer's ripened capacity (2.152's pratibha) catches, and the flash occurs. The verse gathers the movement: exclusion, fixing, and waking are one process, and the meaning was never among the words that performed it.

Verse 2.415.translation: But to others, the sentence and the sentence-meaning both shine forth as undivided, without sequence of words, from words that possess sequence.
Verse 2.415.commentary: The doctrine stated in its final form, and the symmetry is the point. Sabdebhyah kramavadbhyah — from words *possessing sequence*: the utterance is serial, its words come in order, nothing about the vehicle is undivided — prakasate avibhagam apada-kramam: there shines forth what is *undivided*, *without word-sequence*. And what shines: tad anyesam vakyam vakyartha eva ca — to others, *both* the sentence *and* the sentence-meaning. Both terms of the relation are undivided; only the vehicle has parts. This is 1.48's structure (the sequenceless born as if in sequence) at the sentence's scale, and it completes the parallel the kanda has been drawing since 2.30: as the word-sphota is manifested by sequential sounds, the sentence-sphota is manifested by sequential words, and in neither case does the manifestor's seriality touch the manifested. The one word 'prakasate' carries the whole doctrine: not built, not summed, not connected — *shone forth*.

Verse 2.416.translation: That which has an own-form — of it, the self is not described; but of that which has no own-form, of that alone is the self described.
Verse 2.416.commentary: A paradox stated with deliberate sharpness, and it concerns the limits of description. Svarupam vidyate yasya tasya atma na nirupyate: that which *has* an own-form — a determinate nature of its own — of it the *self* is *not described*: what is fully itself cannot be caught in a description, since description would have to stand outside it and there is no outside (2.144's pratibha, not describable even by its possessor; 1.35's untellable expertise). Nasti yasya svarupam tu tasya eva atma nirupyate: but of what *has no* own-form — of *that alone* is the self described.
