What can be described is exactly what has no nature of its own — the constructed, the relational, the item that is nothing but its place in a scheme (the sambandha of 2.46, asattva-bhuta, beyond thinghood, and knowable only by inference from its effect). So description tracks unreality. The verse is the epistemological ground of 2.233's claim that the sciences describe only avidya: they describe because their objects are constructions, and what is not a construction is not available for describing. It is also the deepest reason the vakyartha must be a flash: it has an own-form, and so it can only be waked, never told.
Verse 2.416.contested: the verse is aphoristic and admits an idealist reading (only the unreal is describable, hence description as such is of the unreal) and a narrower semantic one (self-standing items resist definition; relational ones are definable); we take the first as the kanda's own, with 2.233 and 2.421-422 as warrant

Verse 2.417.translation: Others know the determination of the meaning's form to be wordless; for the memory that arises from words has the form of an appearance of the meaning.
Verse 2.417.commentary: The consequence of 2.416 for semantics. Asabdam apare arthasya rupa-nirdharanam viduh: others know the *determination of the meaning's form* to be *wordless* — what fixes a meaning is not itself verbal (2.16's objection, once feared, now embraced: the sentence-meaning is asabda). And the reason: artha-avabhasa-rupa hi sabdebhyo jayate smrtih — the *memory* that arises from words has the form of an *appearance of the meaning*: what words produce in a hearer is not a word but a showing — an avabhasa, a shining-forth of the thing (2.415's prakasate). So the terminus of a verbal process is non-verbal: words wake an appearing, and the appearing is what meaning is. This does not sever the word-meaning bond (2.332 secured it) — it locates the bond's *effect*: the eternal connection delivers, at its far end, something that is not language. Which is why 1.123's thesis (all cognition is word-interwoven) and this verse are compatible: the appearing is word-woven in its structure and wordless in its presentation.

Verse 2.418.translation: It is in one way that the burnt man, from connection with fire, experiences the burning; in quite another way that the meaning of burning and the rest is understood by the word 'burning'.
Verse 2.418.commentary: The gap between the meant and the had, put with unusual directness. Anyatha eva agni-sambandhad daham dagdho 'bhimanyate: it is *in one way entirely* that the *burnt man*, from connection with fire, experiences the burning — the searing itself, undergone. Anyatha daha-sabdena dahady-arthah pratiyate: in *quite another* way is the meaning of burning understood by the *word* 'burning'. The word does not burn. What it wakes is an appearing (2.417), and the appearing of burning is not a burning — no hearer of the word is scorched. The verse marks the limit of the whole semantic enterprise with a directness the kanda has earned: language reaches everything and touches nothing; it can wake an appearance of any experience and cannot supply one. This is also why 2.416 said what it said: the burning has an own-form and is therefore indescribable in the strict sense — what the word conveys is not it, but an appearance answering to it.

Verse 2.419-420.translation: (419) As the effect of the sense-faculties — whose realities are separately lodged, and which severally follow their own objects — is not construed apart from the body; (420) so, of all the words, which severally settle on their own meanings, when they are divided out from sentences, there is no meaningfulness.
Verse 2.419-420.commentary: One of the finest similes in the work, and it answers 2.391's challenge definitively. (419): the indriyas — the senses — have prthag-nivista-tattvanam, their *realities separately lodged* (eye in the socket, ear in the head: really distinct organs); and they are prthag-artha-anupatinah, *severally following their own objects* (eye to colour, ear to sound, no overlap). So they are as separate as things can be. And yet: yatha karyam rte dehan na kalpate — their *effect* is not construed *apart from the body*: an eye out of a head sees nothing; the organ's separateness is real and its functioning is not its own. (420): tatha padanam sarvesam prthag-artha-nivesinam — so of *all* words, which *severally settle* on their own meanings — vakyebhyah pravibhaktanam arthavatta na vidyate: *divided out* from sentences, there is *no meaningfulness*. The simile grants the atomist everything he asked for — words really are distinct, really do have their own meanings, as eyes really are distinct organs with their own objects — and takes back the only thing that mattered: none of it functions in isolation. The word in the sentence is the eye in the head.

Verse 2.421-422.translation: (421) The form of commingling is grasped among meaning-things that are commingled; here the reality is not told, because of the seeing of what is not a word-meaning. (422) Even of the seeing, what is true is not established as the seeing is; that formless thing is described by way of the form of the commingling of things.
Verse 2.421-422.commentary: Two verses at the edge of what the kanda can say. (421): samsarga-rupam samsrstesu artha-vastusu grhyate — the *form of commingling* is grasped among commingled meaning-things: what one gets is the fusion, not the items. And: na atra upakhyayate tattvam apadarthasya darsanat — *here the reality is not told*, because of the seeing of *what is not a word-meaning*: what is seen is no padartha at all, so no word-meaning-talk reaches it (2.416: what has own-form is not described). (422) goes further and turns on the seeing itself: darsanasya api yat satyam na tatha darsanam sthitam — even of the *seeing*, what is *true* is not established as the seeing *is*: the seeing's own truth is not the seeing as it presents. Vastu-samsarga-rupena tad arupam nirupyate: that *formless* thing is described by way of the *form of the commingling of things*. So the formless is described in the only available idiom — the idiom of commingled things — and the description is therefore not of it. This is the kanda's most rigorous moment of self-limitation: it says that its own central object cannot be said, and says why, and continues.
Verse 2.421-422.contested: 422 is among the most difficult verses in the work and our construal is one of several; the transcription's sthitm for sthitam is a clear fault. What is not in doubt is the verse's direction: the formless described through the form of fusion, and the description acknowledged as inadequate

Verse 2.423.translation: Since a meaning is expressed either as accompanied by existence, or as standing in a withdrawn self — therefore a sentence is employed.
Verse 2.423.commentary: The definition of the sentence, reached from the side of existence. A meaning is expressed in one of two ways: astitvena anusaktah — *accompanied by existence*: 'the pot is', the predication affirming; or nivrttya atmani sthitah — *standing in a withdrawn self*: 'the pot is not', the predication denying, the meaning present but with existence withheld (2.240-245's negation-dossier, now given its positive statement). Atah vakyam prayujyate: *therefore a sentence is employed*. The inference is the definition: a sentence is what does *this* — what places a meaning in relation to existence, either way. This is why a word cannot be a sentence unless its existence-verb is discharged (2.270's carita-asti-kriya, 2.408's astitva-upanibandhanam), and it is the deepest answer the kanda gives to its title-question. A vakya is not a string of a certain length or structure. It is an assertion of being or non-being.

Verse 2.424.translation: Without the accompaniment of an action, a word-meaning is not understood — whether real or contrary; therefore it does not exist in practice.
Verse 2.424.commentary: The corollary, and it is severe. Kriya-anusangena vina na padarthah pratiyate: without the *accompaniment of an action*, a word-meaning is *not understood* — satyo va viparito va, whether *real or contrary*: neither a true one nor a false one; not at all. A meaning unattached to any action, any predication, any being-or-not-being, does not present itself to anyone. Atah na so 'sti vyavahare: *therefore it does not exist in practice*. Note the qualification: vyavahare — *in practice*. The bare word-meaning is not declared unreal simpliciter; it is declared absent from practice, which by 2.297 (sabdah loka-nibandhanah: words are grounded in the world) is where words live. So the padartha is an item with no place in the only domain language occupies — the exact status the kanda has assigned it since 2.10: real in the ledger, absent from the transaction.

Verse 2.425.translation: But that sentence which is just 'sat' — 'it was', 'it is', or 'not' — is not conceived apart from the connection with the expressing of an action.
Verse 2.425.commentary: The limit-case tested: the barest possible sentence. Sad iti eva yad vakyam — a sentence that is just *'sat'*, 'being' — tad abhut asti na iti va: 'it was', 'it is', or 'not'. Even here, at the minimum, kriya-abhidhana-sambandham antarena na manyate — it is not conceived *apart from* the connection with the *expressing of an action*: the existence-verb is an action-word, and 'sat' predicates only because it carries one. There is no assertion so thin that it escapes the verb. The verse forecloses an obvious escape from 2.423-424 — the thought that pure existence-claims might be predication without action — and in doing so it makes the action-centred account of the sentence complete: every sentence has a verb, even the ones that are only a verb, and the verb is what makes the sentence a sentence rather than a naming.

Verse 2.426-427.translation: (426) In the meaning expressible by a finite verb, grounded in the means, expectancy does not withdraw without the expressing of existence. (427) But from principality, the action is first divided out of the meaning; the auxiliaries are employed for the sake of the to-be-accomplished; its fruit is the occasioner.
Verse 2.426-427.commentary: The architecture of the sentence, stated completely. (426): in what a finite verb expresses — sadhana-upanibandhane, *grounded in the means* — na akanksa vinivartate vina sattva-abhidhanena: *expectancy does not withdraw* without the *expressing of existence*: the sentence does not close (2.4's nirakanksata, the mark of the vakya) until being has been predicated. So existence-assertion is the closure-condition. (427): pradhanyat tu kriya purvam arthasya pravibhajyate — from *principality*, the *action* is *first* divided out of the meaning: when the analyst carves, the verb comes off first, being the principal (2.4's karma-pradhanam). Sadhya-prayuktani angani: the *auxiliaries* are employed for the sake of the *to-be-accomplished*. Phalam tasyah prayojakam: its *fruit* is the *occasioner*. The chain is complete and it runs backwards from the end: the fruit occasions the action, the action requires the means, the means are named by the nouns — and the sentence closes when existence is predicated of the whole. Purpose at the top (2.113), existence at the bottom, and everything between is auxiliary.

Verse 2.428.translation: It is the speaker alone who intends the character of to-be-accomplished and means; or he desires the constructing of the meaning's connection.
Verse 2.428.commentary: The architecture of 2.427 handed to the speaker. Prayokta eva abhisandhatte sadhya-sadhana-rupatam: it is the *speaker alone* who *intends* the character of to-be-accomplished and means — which element shall be the action and which the instrument is not read off the world; it is conferred by the intending (2.131's theorem: the karaka-grid is constructed by words alone). Artthasya va abhisambandha-kalpanam prasamihate: or he *desires the constructing* of the meaning's connection. The same event can be carved as a cooking-with-fire or a fire's-burning-under-a-pot; the world does not choose, the speaker does. This is 2.400's directed eye applied to syntax rather than to reference: intention aims not only *at* a meaning but *into* an articulation — deciding what shall count as the action. And the next verse gives the demonstration.

Verse 2.429.translation: In 'I do the act of cooking', the cooking-action is expressed as the object; but the cooking, in the form of the instrument, is understood as the to-be-accomplished.
Verse 2.429.commentary: The demonstration, and it is elegant. Take paci-kriyam karomi — 'I do the cooking'. Here the *cooking* — which is an action — is karmatvena abhidhiyate, expressed *as the object*: it has become the patient of 'do'. And yet paktih karana-rupam tu sadhyatvena pratiyate — the cooking, in the *form of the instrument*, is understood as the *to-be-accomplished*: it is still what is being brought about. One and the same cooking is simultaneously object (of 'do'), instrument (of the doing), and the sadhya (what is accomplished) — three karaka-roles at once, assigned by how the sentence was built. Nothing in the cooking determines which; the speaker's construction does (2.428). This single example refutes any view on which the karakas are read off events: an event's roles are as many as the sentences one can make about it, and the sentence-maker assigns them.

Verse 2.430.translation: That which is intended by speakers, with whatever assistance — since the meaning is all-capable, that stands settled just so.
Verse 2.430.commentary: The movement closes by folding syntax into the omni-significance doctrine. Yo 'sau yena upakarena prayoktrnam vivaksitah — whatever is *intended* by speakers, with whatever *assistance* (in whatever role, contributing however) — arthasya sarva-saktitvat sa tatha eva vyavasthitah: *since the meaning is all-capable*, that stands settled *just so*. Sarva-saktitva — the same all-capacity 2.253 predicated of the word (sarva-sakti) and 2.250 of its meaning (sarvarthya) — is here predicated of the *meaning* to explain the karaka-freedom of 2.429: the cooking can be object, instrument, or accomplished because the meaning can be anything, and the speaker's vivaksa selects. The doctrine has now been stated at every level of the language: the word means everything and says one thing; the meaning plays every role and takes one; and in both cases the carving is intention's, working on a plenitude. Kanda III will spend twelve hundred verses examining the categories this carving produces — and 2.430 is its charter.

Verse 2.431-433.section: Connection, Assistance, and the Word's Sovereignty
Verse 2.431-433.translation: (431) In derivative formations, a connection is sometimes expressed from afar; what is unattached is sometimes understood as attached. (432) Of the commingled there is discreteness, and commingling of the discrete; of the many-natured, oneness — and manifoldness in the reverse case. (433) Whether from the meaning's being all-natured, or from its being self-less, it stands settled; because capacity is utterly fixed, the word alone is the ground.
Verse 2.431-433.commentary: Three verses that state the movement's thesis by first cataloguing the inversions language performs. (431): in the vrttis, connection is expressed arat, *from afar* — relations asserted between things not adjacent; and aslisto yo 'nupaslistah — what is *unattached* is understood as *attached*. (432) makes the inversions systematic and total: samsrstanam vivekitvam — of the *commingled*, discreteness (language separates what is fused); samsargas ca vivekinam — and commingling of the *discrete* (it fuses what is separate); nanatmakanam ekatvam — of the *many-natured*, oneness; nanatvam ca viparyaye — and manifoldness in the reverse. Every possible inversion, and language performs them all. Then (433) the conclusion, with its startling either/or: sarvatmakatvad arthasya nairatmyad va vyavasthitam — whether because the meaning is *all-natured* (able to be anything, 2.430's sarva-saktitva) *or* because it is *self-less* (having no nature of its own to resist) — it stands settled thus. Bhartrhari does not choose between plenitude and emptiness; either grounds the same result. And the result: atyanta-yata-saktitvat sabda eva nibandhanam — because capacity is *utterly fixed*, the *word alone* is the ground. The word is sovereign because the meaning offers no resistance, whether from having every nature or none.
Verse 2.431-433.contested: 433's sarvatmakatvad / nairatmyad va is the crux: the first is the kanda's own sarvarthya-doctrine (2.250), the second a nairatmya-formulation with unmistakable Buddhist resonance. That Bhartrhari treats them as interchangeable grounds for one conclusion is remarkable and, we think, deliberate

Verse 2.434.translation: The word is an indicator of the thing; it is not the expressor of the assistance. The own-capacity of word-meanings cannot be touched by it.
Verse 2.434.commentary: A precise limitation on what the word does. Vastu-upalaksanam sabdah: the word is an *indicator of the thing* — upalaksana, a mark that points out without describing (2.309's saptaparna: naming without characterizing). Na upakarasya vacakah: it is *not the expressor of the assistance* — of the contribution one meaning makes to another, the relational work that goes on among the items. And: na sva-saktih padarthanam samsprastum tena sakyate — the *own-capacity* of word-meanings cannot be *touched* by it. The word points at things; it does not reach into their powers, does not manipulate what they can do, does not express the assistance they render one another in a construction. This is why the karaka-relations must be conferred by the speaker (2.428) rather than expressed by the words: the words are indicators, and indicators indicate — they do not perform the joinery. The verse limits the word's sovereignty of 2.433 in exactly the right place: sovereign over what is meant, powerless over what the meant things are.

Verse 2.435.translation: Conjunction, a property of the relata, is expressed by its own word; connection and inherence are understood by way of being relata.
Verse 2.435.commentary: The taxonomy of relation, and it discriminates carefully. Samyoga — *conjunction*, the Vaisesika category (two substances in contact) — is sambandhi-dharma, a *property of the relata*, and svasabdena abhidhiyate: expressed by *its own word*: 'conjunction' names it, as any word names any thing, because it is a thing — a property residing in its terms. But sambandhah samavayas ca — *connection* as such, and *inherence* (the Vaisesikas' other relation, binding qualities to substances) — sambandhitvena gamyate: are understood *by way of being relata* — i.e. not named directly but grasped only through the terms they relate. The distinction is exact: conjunction is a property and so nameable; relation-as-such is not a property of anything and so is reachable only obliquely (2.46: the relation is inferable from its effect, its form is never seen, it is utterly beyond thinghood). The verse is the third kanda's Sambandha-samuddesa in miniature — and it shows Bhartrhari treating the rival school's categories with technical care before dissolving them.

Verse 2.436.translation: Word-meanings stand settled by way of characterization, not in reality; from the assistance, that very meaning is somehow followed along.
Verse 2.436.commentary: The status of the padartha, stated flatly. Laksanad vyavatisthante padarthah na tu vastutah: word-meanings stand settled *by way of characterization*, and *not in reality* — laksana, the marking-out by which analysis fixes an item, is what gives a word-meaning its determinacy, and the determinacy is the marking's, not the thing's. And the concession: upakarat sa eva arthah kathamcid anugamyate — from the *assistance*, that very meaning is *somehow* followed along. Kathamcid — 'somehow': the shrug is the verse's honesty. The word-meanings do have a life, traceable through the assistance they render in constructions; but the tracing is of something the characterization posited, and how it manages to be followed is not further explained. This is 2.10's apoddhara-doctrine in its final formulation: the padartha is a creature of laksana, real in the practice, and its reality *in* the practice is not nothing — it is just not vastu.

Verse 2.437.translation: The connection which is in the sentence-meaning — its self does not stand anywhere; in the practice of word-meanings, they call that its self.
Verse 2.437.commentary: The connection dissolved, and its ghost given an address. Vakyarthe yo 'bhisambandhah — the connection that is *in* the sentence-meaning (the relatedness by which the items cohere, 2.145's upaslesa) — na tasya atma kvacit sthitah: its *self does not stand anywhere*. It is nowhere: not in the words (which only indicate, 2.434), not among the meanings (which are characterizations, 2.436), not as a thing (2.46's asattva-bhuta). And then: vyavahare padarthanam tam atmanam pracaksate — *in the practice* of word-meanings, they call *that* its self: the practice has a name for this nothing, treats it as an item, and transacts with it. So the connection is a posting with no referent, indispensable to the practice and absent from the world — which is the sharpest thing the kanda has said about the central concept of every rival semantics.

Verse 2.438.translation: Whether completed in the word-meaning, or in the aggregate, or nowhere at all — its self is divided out by way of the difference of word-meaning-forms.
Verse 2.438.commentary: The three options for the connection's location, and their equivalence. Padarthe — completed in the *word-meaning* (each item carrying its own relatedness); samudaye va — or in the *aggregate* (the relation belonging to the collection); samapto na eva va kvacit — or *nowhere at all* (2.437's verdict). Whichever: tasya atma pravibhajyate padartha-rupa-bhedena — its self is *divided out* by way of the *difference of word-meaning-forms*. The connection, wherever one places it, gets its apparent articulation from the items it supposedly relates — which is circular in exactly the way 2.46 diagnosed: the relation is known only from its terms, and the terms are individuated by the relation. Bhartrhari lists the three placements without choosing because the circularity infects all three. What escapes it is his own account: no relation, one meaning, and the 'items' its capacities (2.394's sadharana-model).

Verse 2.439-440.translation: (439) The difference which, for the sake of analysis, is the ground of comprehension — by that, in the differentiation, something else, having mutually-requiring parts, is described. (440) The division of a single thing possessing many capacities is followed; for a sentence's single-meaningedness is understood even by a fraction.
Verse 2.439-440.commentary: The analysis's status, and then a striking empirical claim. (439): the *difference* posited anvakhyanaya, *for the sake of analysis*, and serving as pratipatti-nibandhanam, the ground of comprehension (the ladder, 2.410) — by it, tena anyad upavarnyate: *something else* is described: sakanksa-avayavam bhede — 'having mutually-requiring parts', which is 2.3-2.4's definition of the sentence. So the analytic definition of the vakya describes not the sentence but *something else* — the analysis's own construct. (440) then names what is really there: anekasakteh ekasya pravibhagah anugamyate — the *division* of a *single* thing possessing *many capacities* is what is being followed (the sadharana-model, 2.394-396: one meaning, many capacity-positions). And the evidence: ekarthatvam hi vakyasya matraya api pratiyate — the sentence's *single-meaningedness* is understood *even by a fraction*: hear part of a sentence and you already grasp that it has one meaning — the unity is given before the completion, which no summative account can explain (2.40's single phoneme conveying the whole).

Verse 2.441.translation: The external meaning, whether existent or non-existent, is divided out from the meaning of the comprehension; but the division, having made it external, has the mark of the abstraction of capacities.
Verse 2.441.commentary: The relation of comprehension to the world, and it is the movement's most careful formulation. Sampratyayarthad bahyo 'rthah san asan va vibhajyate: the *external* meaning — *whether existent or non-existent* — is *divided out from* the meaning of the comprehension. The order is the point: what is primary is the sampratyaya-artha, the meaning-in-comprehension (2.328's criterion: what is understood *is* the meaning); the 'external' object is then carved off from it, by us, and its existence or non-existence is a further question that the carving does not settle. And: bahyikrtya vibhagas tu sakty-apoddhara-laksanah — the division, *having made it external*, has the mark of the *abstraction of capacities*: externality itself is an apoddhara, an abstraction, and what it abstracts are capacities (2.88's settlement: abstraction according to capacity-difference). This is as close as the kanda comes to an explicit idealism, and it stops precisely where 2.132-133 stopped: the external is a division within the comprehended, and whether anything answers to it is not a question the semantics can reach.

Verse 2.442.translation: Even among many finite verbs that are mutually requiring, there is single-sentence-hood; hence the exclusion of accent-suppression from the finite verbs is also meaningful.
Verse 2.442.commentary: The doctrine of 2.348 (rule and exception as one sentence) given its grammatical proof. Bahusv api tinantesu sakanksesu eka-vakyata: even among *many finite verbs*, if they are *mutually requiring*, there is *single-sentence-hood* — verb-count does not individuate sentences; expectancy does (2.6's tinanta qualifying tinanta). And the evidence from the sastra: tinantebhyo nighatasya paryudasah tatha arthavan — the *exclusion* of accent-suppression *from* the finite verbs is *thus meaningful*: Panini's nighata-rule (8.1.28: a verb loses its accent after a preceding word in the same sentence) has an exception for verbs following verbs — and that exception only makes sense if multiple verbs *can* stand in one sentence. The grammar's exception presupposes the doctrine. This is the kanda's favourite move (2.5's vocative, 2.59's pada-patha): a semantic thesis confirmed by a rule that would be unstatable without it.

Verse 2.443.translation: But that sentence which has one finite verb, whose definition is fixed in the science — for it, there is no purpose in the mention of 'not-tin', because of the difference of sentences.
Verse 2.443.commentary: The alternative position and its cost. Suppose one holds eka-tin yasya vakyam — that a sentence has *one* finite verb, by a definition niyata-laksanam sastre, fixed in the science (the operational definition of 2.3, adopted as metaphysics). Then: tasya atin-grahanena artho na vidyate — for such a theorist, there is *no purpose* in the mention of 'not-tin' (the atin of the nighata-rule's exception) — vakya-bhedat, *because of the difference of sentences*: on his view, two verbs simply *are* two sentences, so the rule's exception is idle — no verb ever follows a verb within a sentence, and the exception excludes nothing. The argument is a clean reductio from grammatical economy: Panini does not legislate vacuously; his exception has work to do; therefore the one-verb definition is false. The verse thus turns the science's own laghava-principle (2.343) against a definition the science itself uses (2.3) — which is exactly what 2.3 warned: rule-definitions are for the rules' purposes and settle nothing beyond them.

Verse 2.444.translation: In cases joined with another finite verb, or again in the joined-and-joined — 'the deer! look! it goes' — difference and non-difference do not stand.
Verse 2.444.commentary: The hard case, honestly displayed. Mrgah pasyata yati — 'The deer! Look! It goes' (or 'Look at the deer going'): three predications, or one utterance? Tinanta-antara-yuktesu — in cases joined with *another* finite verb — yukta-yuktesu va punah, or in the *joined-and-joined* (chains of them): bheda-abhedau na tisthatah — *difference and non-difference do not stand*: neither the claim that these are separate sentences nor the claim that they are one can be maintained. The utterance is genuinely indeterminate between one sentence and three, and no criterion settles it — not verb-count (2.442-443 disposed of that), not expectancy (each closes and none does), not the speaker's intent (he intended the whole scene). Bhartrhari lets the indeterminacy stand. It is the same undecidability he confessed at 2.26 (whether the sequencing cognition is one or many: niruktir na avatisthate) and at 1.74, and its recurrence at the kanda's climax is not a failure: it is what a doctrine of undivided meaning carved by analysis *predicts*. Where the carving is optional, the count is.

Verse 2.445.translation: That whose what-is-to-be-done is desired, from capacity, for the meaning — having an expectancy not characterized by words, that is called 'of completed meaning'.
Verse 2.445.commentary: A definition of completion that severs it from words entirely. Itikartavyata arthasya samarthyad yasya kanksyate — that whose *what-is-to-be-done* (the practical remainder: what one must now do) is *desired*, from *capacity*, for the meaning — asabda-laksana-akanksam: having an expectancy *not characterized by words* — samapta-artham tad ucyate: *that* is called 'of completed meaning'. So a sentence is complete when its verbal expectancy has closed even though a *practical* expectancy remains open: 'the deer is going' is a complete sentence and leaves you wanting to know what to do about it. Asabda-laksana-akanksa — wordless expectancy — is the crucial coinage: the sentence's completion is measured against words, and the residue it leaves is measured against action. This distinguishes semantic closure from practical closure, and it explains why sentences can be complete and yet demand more: the more they demand is not linguistic (2.146's itikartavyata, sovereign and untransgressed).

Verse 2.446.translation: But in the mere analytic statement of the reality, however much meaning is carried along — even without the employment of that, the sentence is completed from the hearing.
Verse 2.446.commentary: The complement of 2.445, for the case of pure statement. Tattva-anvakhyana-matre tu — in the *mere analytic statement of the reality*: description for its own sake, no injunction, no practical residue — yavan arthah anusajyate, however much meaning is carried along: vina api tat-prayogena srutervakyam samapyate — *even without the employment of that*, the sentence is completed *from the hearing*. A descriptive sentence closes with its utterance; nothing further is wanted; there is no itikartavyata to leave open. Between 2.445 and 2.446 the two kinds of sentence are distinguished by what they leave: the practical sentence closes verbally and opens actionally; the descriptive closes altogether. And note which is treated as the special case: it is the *descriptive* one that needs the qualification 'even without employment'. For this school the default sentence is the one that tells you what to do — which is what a tradition whose paradigm text is a body of injunctions would naturally think, and what 2.113's prayojana-doctrine requires.

Verse 2.447.translation: 'While walking about, study here'; 'muttering, do the walking-about' — though the purposiveness is not different, a difference is understood from the word.
Verse 2.447.commentary: A beautiful demonstration that syntax means something even when semantics does not change. Two instructions: cankramyamano 'dhisva atra — 'while walking about, *study* here'; and japan cankramanam kuru — 'while muttering, *do the walking-about*'. The same two activities, the same pairing, the same practical upshot: the man walks and recites. Tadarthyasya avisese 'pi — though the *purposiveness is not different* — sabdad bhedah pratiyate: a *difference is understood from the word*. What differs is which activity is principal and which subordinate — and that difference is real, is understood, and is contributed by nothing but the grammatical construction (2.428: the speaker intends the sadhya-sadhana structure). The example is decisive against any view on which sentence-meaning is a function of the states of affairs described: here the states of affairs are identical and the meanings differ. What differs is the *articulation*, and articulation is the speaker's.

Verse 2.448.translation: The differences of action, having fruits, grounded in other actions, are uncounted; by the designations of sequence, they have a single finite verb for their exhibition.
Verse 2.448.commentary: The verb's compression, stated as a fact about the world it reports. Kriya-bhedah phalavantah kriyantara-nibandhanah asamkhyatah: the differences of action — each *having its fruit*, each *grounded in other actions* — are *uncounted*: any action is indefinitely divisible into sub-actions, each with its own result, each depending on others (the cooking is a fetching, a lighting, a stirring, a waiting). And: kramoddesaih eka-akhyata-nidarsanah — by the *designations of sequence*, they have a *single finite verb* for their exhibition. One verb, 'he cooks', displays an unbounded manifold. This is the sadharana-model (2.394) at the verb: the single verb does not sum its sub-actions, it *is* them, wholly, from where it stands. And it explains 2.415's undivided shining: the vakyartha can be undivided while its analysis is inexhaustible, because analysis of a whole is not decomposition into parts.

Verse 2.449-450.translation: (449) The whole action, its differences withdrawn, is expressed in the finite verb; the constructing of a division of the differences is not possible from the hearing. (450) 'The kings will sacrifice with the horse-sacrifice'; 'the brahmins sit the session' — a difference is not understood from the finite verb's form.
Verse 2.449-450.commentary: The consequence of 2.448, with evidence. (449): nivrtta-bheda sarva eva kriya akhyate abhidhiyate — the *whole* action, its differences *withdrawn*, is expressed in the finite verb; sruteh asakya bhedanam pravibhaga-prakalpana: the constructing of a division of the differences is *not possible from the hearing* — listen as hard as you like to 'he cooks' and no sub-action is audible in it. (450) supplies two cases where the distribution question is live and the verb is silent: asvamedhena yaksyante rajanah — 'the *kings* will sacrifice with the horse-sacrifice' (each king his own? or one joint rite?); satram asate brahmanah — 'the brahmins *sit* the session' (the sattra is by definition collective, but the verb does not say so). Na akhyata-rupad bhedah pratiyate: a difference is *not understood from the finite verb's form*. The verb form is identical in the distributive and collective cases; only samarthya (2.371) tells them apart. Language does not encode the distinction that ritual practice most needs, and the exegetes have been supplying it from capacity ever since.

Verse 2.451.translation: The action which, heard once, is not repeated among the seventeen — in the Prajapatya rites it attains difference, from capacity.
Verse 2.451.commentary: The doctrine at work in a real ritual crux. In the Prajapatya rites, seventeen items are involved and an action is sakrt sruta — *heard once*, uttered a single time, anavrtta, not repeated. Does it apply once, or seventeen times? Sa bhedam pratipadyate samarthyat: it *attains difference*, from *capacity* — the action distributes across the seventeen, though nothing in the utterance says so, because what the action *is* requires it (2.371's criterion; 2.372's eating completed in each). The case is chosen because the stakes are exact: get this wrong and the rite is malperformed. And it demonstrates the movement's thesis in the domain where the tradition is most careful: the verb, uttered once, undivided (2.449), distributes by capacity and not by any linguistic mark — which is why the exegetical science exists and why it reasons from samarthya rather than from grammar.

Verse 2.452.translation: The eating stands severally with respect to Devadatta and the rest; or the sentence is independent for each — the difference is not divided out.
Verse 2.452.commentary: Two descriptions of the distributed case, offered as equivalent. Devadattadisu bhujih pratyekam avatisthate: the *eating* stands *severally* with respect to Devadatta and the others — one sentence, its action distributing (2.372). Prati-svatantram vakyam va — *or* the sentence is *independent for each*: several sentences, one per eater, sharing a form (2.389's position). Bhedo na pravibhajyate: the *difference is not divided out* — nothing distinguishes the two descriptions; no fact settles whether this is one sentence distributing or several sentences coinciding. This is 2.444's undecidability again, and its recurrence is now clearly systematic: wherever the kanda's own doctrine (undivided meaning, analytic carving) applies, the count of sentences is a function of the carving. Bhartrhari does not treat this as a problem to be solved. It is the doctrine's signature, and its consistency across every application is the best evidence for it.

Verse 2.453.translation: But in the uttering of sentences, one form is grasped; while in the comprehension of the differentiated, another form is understood.
Verse 2.453.commentary: The two-sidedness that generates 2.452's undecidability, named. Uccarane tu vakyanam anyad rupam nigrhyate: in the *uttering* of sentences, *one* form is grasped — the utterance is one, a single stretch of sound, however many predications it carries (2.110's tantra: one form, many sentences). Pratipattau tu bhinnanam anyad rupam pratiyate: but in the *comprehension* of the differentiated, *another* form is understood — the hearer's grasp articulates into as many as the case requires. Utterance-side unity, comprehension-side plurality: and since sentencehood is defined by neither the sound (2.104) nor the count of words (2.326) but by the closing of expectancy in a mind, the two sides can disagree without contradiction. The verse explains, at last, why the kanda's central question — what is a sentence? — has been answered with a phenomenon rather than a definition. Sentences are individuated where they happen, and they happen in comprehenders.

Verse 2.454.translation: One comprehensive sentence is expressed by way of the general — as in 'in the agent' — and that is divided out among the animal and the rest.
Verse 2.454.commentary: The mechanism of generality in rule-sentences. Ekam grahanakam vakyam samanyena abhidhiyate: *one comprehensive sentence* is expressed *by way of the general* — the example is Panini's kartari, 'in (the sense of) the agent' (the rule-fragment governing a whole class of affixations) — and tad ca pasvadisu vibhajyate: *that* is *divided out* among the animal and the rest: the single general provision distributes across all its instances. This is 2.176's economy (instruction by the general, for brevity) shown to be a case of 2.448's compression: one utterance, indefinitely many applications, no division audible. And it closes the circle the movement opened at 2.371: how a single utterance distributes over a plurality — whether of eaters, of kings, of sacrificial items, or of grammatical instances — is settled by capacity and purpose, never by form, and the grammar's own most general rules are the clearest case.

Verse 2.455.translation: If the expectancy of that were to withdraw upon a single hearing — then it could not in any way attain connection with another.
Verse 2.455.commentary: A closing argument, and it is a conditional whose antecedent the kanda denies. Yadi akanksa nivarteta tad-bhutasya sakrc-chrutau: *if* the expectancy of that were to *withdraw* upon a *single hearing* — if one utterance discharged the expectancy completely and finally — na eva anyena abhisambandham tad upeyat kathamcana: then it could *not in any way* attain connection with *another*. A fully discharged sentence is inert: nothing can be added to it, no further clause can join it, no exception can limit it (2.348's rule-and-exception unity would be impossible; 2.442's multi-verb sentences would not exist). But such joinings manifestly occur. Therefore expectancy is not discharged by a single hearing — it remains live, reaching across utterances, closing only where the whole discourse closes (2.76's maha-vakya). The verse defends, by reductio, the most expansive consequence of the kanda's doctrine: sentencehood has no fixed upper bound, and what looked like a completed sentence is a member of a larger one whenever a larger one is being said.

Verse 2.456.section: One Form, Many Meanings: The Womb of Sentences
Verse 2.456.translation: Therefore the ground is single in form and manifold in meaning — the womb of the sentences of division, standing as though not other than them.
Verse 2.456.commentary: The conclusion of 2.455's argument, and it names the sentence's source with the kanda's oldest metaphor. The ground — upanibandhanam, the fastening (the term for what meaning rests on since 1.13) — is eka-rupam aneka-artham: *single in form*, *manifold in meaning* (2.250's one word with many meanings, now at the sentence's scale). And its office: yonih vibhaga-vakyanam — the *womb* of the sentences of *division*: the analytic sentences, the several predications the analysis distributes (2.389-390), are born from it. Yoni is 2.58's word (samhita as the womb of the words) and 1.3's (the six modifications as wombs of differentiation) — the vocabulary of birth rather than of composition. And the closing qualification: tebhyah ananyad iva sthitam — standing as though *not other* than them: the source is not a further item behind its progeny; the division-sentences are it, differently seen. Iva, as always, holds the ontology honest.

Verse 2.457.translation: Sometimes an action operates in its assistance by way of the individual-portions; sometimes the general-portion alone of it is what accomplishes the meaning.
Verse 2.457.commentary: The distribution-doctrine (2.371-455) restated in terms of portions. Kvacit kriya vyakti-bhagaih upakare pravartate: sometimes an action operates, in its *assistance*, by way of the *individual-portions* — reaching its object through the particulars (the eating, per eater, 2.372). Samanya-bhaga eva asyah kvacid arthasya sadhakah: sometimes the *general-portion alone* of it is what accomplishes the meaning — the action engaging only the type, indifferent to instances (the lamp's lighting, 2.374). The vocabulary of bhaga, portion, is doing careful work: these are not two actions nor two parts of one action, but two *aspects* under which one action engages a plurality. The sadharana-model again (2.394-397): one thing, wholly present at several positions, and which position bears the weight is settled by capacity.

Verse 2.458.translation: The differences that are separated by time, and those in 'ustrasika' and the like — in the commencement of the class-portion, the word's self is not differentiated by them.
Verse 2.458.commentary: The invariance of the word under two kinds of variation. Kala-bhinnah ca bhedah — differences separated *by time*: the same word uttered on different occasions, its instances scattered across the years. And ye ca api ustrasikadisu — those in 'ustrasika' and the like: the traditional class of words whose formation or reference is irregular. Prakrame jati-bhagasya sabdatma taih na bhidyate: in the *commencement of the class-portion*, the word's *self is not differentiated* by them. Whether the word is met now or a century hence, in a regular formation or an odd one, its atman is one — 2.256's svarupa-invariance (what is fixed about a word is its form) given its temporal and morphological range. The verse guards the sadharana-model from an obvious objection: if the word's meaning is carved fresh at every occasion, is it the same word? Yes — the carving varies, the word does not.

Verse 2.459.translation: Among differences of a single number, actions differentiated by class and the rest are applied by way of difference, on a single hearing of that word.
Verse 2.459.commentary: A refinement: distribution can be selective. Eka-samkhyesu bhedesu — among differences that are *of a single number* (items counted as one group) — bhinnah jaty-adibhih kriyah: actions *differentiated* by class and the rest — bhedena viniyujyante: are applied *by way of difference* — tac-chabdasya sakrc-chrutau: on a *single hearing* of that word. One utterance, one number, and yet the several actions distribute selectively across the group by class — some members getting one treatment, some another, all from a single hearing. Nothing in the utterance encodes the sorting; capacity and class do it (2.371's samarthya-laksanam). The verse is a fine specimen of how much the exegetical tradition asks of a single sentence, and of why the kanda has insisted that the words are not carrying the load: no distribution of deposits could produce a selective sorting that the utterance never mentions.

Verse 2.460-463.translation: (460) As, with dice and the like, the actions of breaking, eating and playing are distinct — though there is no difference in the time of employment, they stand separately for each. (461) The single-performance of dice and of lute-players is a means; likeness of form is the way; their sequence, being divided, is the once-hearing bound up with that. (462) Two means stand settled in the employment of words: sequence, or simultaneity — which the world does not transgress. (463) In sequence the form is differentiated; in simultaneity it is not; but the action, even in simultaneity, follows the form of sequence.
Verse 2.460-463.commentary: Four verses on the two modes of linguistic employment. (460): aksa — 'dice', and the pun is deliberate, since aksa also names other things — take distinct actions: bhanji (breaking), bhaksi (eating), divi (playing); prayoga-kala-abhede 'pi, though the time of employment does not differ, prati-bhedam prthak sthitah, they stand separately for each. (461) gives the machinery: tantra, single-performance, and tulya-rupata, likeness of form, as the means; the sakrc-chruti, the once-hearing, bound to the divided sequence. Then (462) the doctrine: dvau abhyupayau sabdanam prayoge samavasthitau — *two* means stand settled in the employment of words: kramo va yaugapadyam va — *sequence* or *simultaneity* — yau loko na ativartate: which the world does not transgress. Every utterance is either serial or simultaneous; there is no third. And (463) the asymmetry: krame vibhidyate rupam — in sequence the *form* is differentiated; yaugapadye na bhidyate — in simultaneity it is not. Kriya tu yaugapadye 'pi krama-rupa-anupatini: but the *action*, even in simultaneity, *follows the form of sequence*. Even where the words come at once, what they mean unrolls in order — sequence expelled from the vehicle reappears in the meaning, which is 2.24-27's doctrine (krama as the mind's contribution) confirmed from an unexpected side.

Verse 2.464.translation: The two capacities, of difference and of commingling, stand as though distinct from the word; even in simultaneity, in employment with several, the hearing is differentiated.
Verse 2.464.commentary: The two capacities named. Bheda-samsarga-sakti dve — the capacity of *difference* (to be carved into distinct meanings) and of *commingling* (to fuse into one) — sabdad bhinne iva sthite: stand *as though distinct* from the word. The iva is essential: they are not two properties the word has but two aspects of its one omni-capacity (2.433's atyanta-yata-saktitva). And the consequence: yaugapadye 'pi anekena prayoge bhidyate srutih — even in *simultaneity*, in employment with several, the *hearing* is differentiated: the word uttered once, at once, is nonetheless heard as several where several are meant. This is 2.463's asymmetry given its mechanism: simultaneity in the vehicle does not prevent differentiation in the uptake, because the differentiating is not the vehicle's work.

Verse 2.465.translation: Where one meaning, undivided, is intended in the form of difference — there the aggregate is favoured by the property of the parts.
Verse 2.465.commentary: A case where the whole borrows from its parts. Abhinnah bheda-rupena yah eko 'rtho vivaksitah — where *one* meaning, *undivided*, is intended *in the form of difference*: the speaker means one thing and means it as many (a group as a group-of-these). Tatra avayava-dharmena samudayah anugrhyate: there the *aggregate* is *favoured* — assisted, benefited — by the *property of the parts*: the whole takes on characteristics that belong to its members, and the taking-on is what makes the group-as-many available to speech. This is the reverse of the kanda's usual direction (wholes lending to parts, 2.61's samnivisate); here the parts lend upward. And it is licensed by the same model: on the sadharana-account (2.394), whole and parts are one thing at different positions, so lending can run either way. What is not licensed is composition — the aggregate is *favoured* by the parts' property, not *made of* it.

Verse 2.466.translation: But in the explicit stating of its difference, either it is completed severally, or the hearing, differentiated by the expression, stands in a difference of sentences.
Verse 2.466.commentary: The two descriptions of 2.465's case, and they are 2.452's pair again. Bheda-nirvacane tu asya — in the *explicit stating* of its difference (where the speaker spells out the members) — pratyekam va samapyate: either it is *completed severally* — one sentence, distributing; or srutih vacana-bhinna va vakya-bhede 'vatisthate: the *hearing*, differentiated by the expression, stands in a *difference of sentences* — several sentences, one per member. Undecidable, as always where the carving is optional (2.444, 2.452). The recurrence at this point is worth marking: the kanda is closing, and it has not resolved this question anywhere. It has instead shown, case after case, that the question has no answer *and that nothing depends on it* — which is a philosophical result rather than an evasion, and the strongest possible confirmation that the unit was never in the utterance.

Verse 2.467.translation: There, either that word 'aksa' is employed as ending in the singular, or severally, by way of plurality; the division is according to the hearing.
Verse 2.467.commentary: The two options exhibited on the aksa-example (2.460). Either eka-vacana-antah — the word ends in the *singular*, one form for the group; or pratyekam bahutvena — *severally*, by way of *plurality*, the plural form spelling out the members. And the settlement: pravibhagah yatha-sruti — the division is *according to the hearing*: whichever was uttered, the division follows. Yatha-sruti is a modest and exact criterion: the grammar does not decide whether this group is one or many; the speaker's chosen number does, and the hearer follows. Which is 2.385's doctrine (number conferred by vessels) at the level of morphology — the singular and the plural are two vessels, and the same stuff is one or many according to which one it is poured into.

Verse 2.468.translation: Even in those sentences which are two-placed, for those who see oneness, the own-capacity of a single thing possessing many capacities is divided out.
Verse 2.468.commentary: The oneness-doctrine applied to sentences with two loci. Dvisthani yani vakyani — sentences that are *two-placed*: predications reaching two subjects, two sites, two applications. Tesu api ekatva-darsinam — even in those, for *those who see oneness* (Bhartrhari's own party, 2.57's abheda-purvaka) — anekasakteh ekasya sva-saktih pravibhajyate: the *own-capacity* of a *single* thing possessing *many capacities* is *divided out*. Not two sentences, not one sentence with two halves: one capacity, carved. The formulation gathers the movement's whole vocabulary — anekasakti (2.440), sva-sakti, pravibhajyate (2.314's carving) — and applies it where the temptation to count is strongest. Two places, one meaning, and the twoness is the division of a capacity rather than the presence of a pair.

Verse 2.469-471.translation: (469) Or there might be an employment, characterized by single-performance, of two utterly distinct things; the means there is the commingling, which is differentiated among the comprehenders. (470) Two words, previously apprehended by way of difference, alike in hearing, are again conveyed by single-performance to the comprehender by the speaker. (471) Even when one alone is intended, the other arises-in-consequence; without any intention, the word shines forth in the form of capacity.
Verse 2.469-471.commentary: Three verses on homonymy under tantra, and the last is the movement's most interesting concession. (469): two *utterly distinct* things may be employed tantra-laksanah, by single-performance — one utterance serving both — and the samsarga, the commingling, is the means, pratipattrsu bhidyate, *differentiated among the comprehenders*: different hearers get different fusions. (470): two words bhedena avagatau purvam — previously apprehended *by way of difference* (known separately, as distinct words) — tulya-sruti punah, *alike in hearing*, are conveyed tantrena by the speaker: one utterance, both words, deliberately. That is punning, and the verse describes it exactly. Then (471) the concession: ekasya api vivaksayam anunispadyate parah — even when *one alone* is intended, the *other* arises-in-consequence (2.121's anunispadin: the entailed, unbidden); vina abhisandhina sabdah sakti-rupah prakasate: *without any intention*, the word shines forth *in the form of capacity*. The second meaning comes whether the speaker wants it or not — because the word's capacity is not under his control (2.400's eye can be aimed; what falls in the periphery cannot be excluded). Intention governs what is said and not what arrives (2.301's smoke), and here that doctrine reaches its sharpest formulation: the pun is not always the punster's.

Verse 2.472-473.translation: (472) The many capacities of a single thing are sometimes heard simultaneously; fire is applied, even in one place, by way of both illuminating and burning. (473) In a sentence whose meaning is differentiated by the capacity of repetition, even when heard once, it stands by way of division — either from a mark or from the property of single-performance.
Verse 2.472-473.commentary: The doctrine's culminating illustration, and it is the work's oldest image put to a new use. (472): aneka-saktih ekasya yugapat sruyate kvacit — the *many capacities* of a single thing are sometimes heard *simultaneously*; and the example: agnih prakasa-dahabhyam ekatra api niyujyate — *fire* is applied, *even in one place*, by way of *both* illuminating *and* burning. One fire, two capacities, exercised at once, in the same act, on the same occasion — and no one thinks the fire is two, or that the burning is a secondary use of the lighting (2.253's gauna/mukhya dissolved by the plainest fact about fire). So with the word: many capacities, exercised at once, one word. (473) then applies it to the sentence heard once and meaning severally — standing vibhagena, by way of *division*, either lingat, from a *mark*, or tantra-dharmat, from the *property of single-performance*. The fire-image is the perfect close to the movement, and it is chosen for a reason: fire has been the work's figure for the word since 1.46 (fire latent in the fire-sticks), and here it is the figure for omni-capacity exercised without division.

Verse 2.474.translation: In the samprasarana-designation, by the two marks of phoneme and sentence, the division is construed there in one and the same sutra.
Verse 2.474.commentary: The doctrine confirmed from the grammar's own metalanguage. Samprasarana is Panini's term for the vowel-substitution of semivowels (y→i, v→u, r→r). The verse observes that in the samprasarana-samjna, the division is effected lingabhyam varna-vakyayoh — by the *two marks* of *phoneme* and *sentence*: two different kinds of indicator, operating at different grains — and tatra sutre ekasmin eva kalpate: the division is construed *in one and the same sutra*. One rule, two grains of marking, both required and both present. This is 2.472's fire in the sastra: a single item exercising several capacities at once, and the grammar's own most technical machinery presupposing exactly the multi-capacity account the kanda has argued. The verse is characteristic of the work's method at its most confident: after four hundred verses of philosophy, the confirmation is a footnote to a sutra.

Verse 2.475.translation: So too in 'reduplication before a vowel', the designation by way of single-remainder, characterized by the means of single-performance and the rest, has been described in the Bhasya itself.
Verse 2.475.commentary: A second confirmation, and this one names its authority. Dvirvacane aci — 'reduplication before a vowel' (Panini 6.1.1-2 territory): the rule's formulation employs ekasesa, the *single-remainder* device (2.108-110), and is tantropaya-adi-laksanah, characterized by the means of single-performance and the rest. And: bhasya eva upavarnitah — it has been described *in the Bhasya itself*. Patanjali got there first, and Bhartrhari says so. The citation is doing more than crediting: it establishes that the whole apparatus the kanda has built — tantra, ekasesa, one form serving many offices — is not the author's invention but the tradition's own, present in the Mahabhasya and only made explicit here. Which is exactly the claim 1.30 requires of any teaching: it must be agama-purvaka, preceded by tradition, and even the grammarian's boldest semantics is a drawing-out of what the gurus had already done.

Verse 2.476.translation: Having, for the most part, reached grammarians who delight in brevity and have grasped little learning — when the Sangraha had gone to its setting —
Verse 2.476.commentary: The famous historical passage begins, and its first word is a lament. The Sangraha — Vyadi's vast lost work, the tradition's great compendium — has *gone to its setting*, astam upagate: the sun-verb, the same used at 2.133 for doctrines that set like stars and at 1.18 for differentiations that set like the sun. The book is gone. And the reason it went: the grammarians it reached were samksepa-rucin — *delighting in brevity*, wanting the short version — and alpa-vidya-parigrahan, having *grasped little learning*. A great work perished because its inheritors preferred summaries. Prayena — 'for the most part': the qualification is scholarly and it is bitter. This is the only extended historical narrative in the work, and it is placed at the very end of the Vakyakanda, after four hundred verses arguing that meaning lives in a flash waked by tradition in a ripened mind — which is precisely what a tradition loses when its heirs want it brief.
Verse 2.476.contested: the Sangraha's authorship (Vyadi) and its loss are known only from this passage and later report; nothing of it survives, which makes 2.476-482 the principal evidence for a major chapter of the tradition's history

Verse 2.477-478.translation: (477) — and when, thereafter, the Mahabhasya had been composed by the teacher Patanjali, the seer of the ford, as the binding-together of the seeds of all reasonings — (478) that work, unfathomed because of its depth, and as though on the surface because of its excellence — in it, for those whose understanding is unformed, no settled conclusion stood at all.
Verse 2.477-478.commentary: The second act of the history, and its praise is exact. Patanjali is guruna tirtha-darsina — the *teacher*, the *seer of the ford*: tirtha is the crossing-place, and to see the ford is to know where the river can be passed. His Mahabhasya is sarvesam nyaya-bijanam nibandhane — the *binding-together of the seeds of all reasonings*: not a commentary but a seed-bank, and bija is the work's word for what survives dissolutions (1.132's world-seed). Then (478) the diagnosis of its difficulty, in a phrase of great beauty: alabdha-gadhe gambhiryad — *unfathomed* because of its *depth*; uttana iva sausthavat — and *as though on the surface* because of its *excellence*: the very perfection of its exposition made it look shallow, so that readers waded in and drowned. Tasmin akrta-buddhinam na eva avasthita niscayah: in it, for those whose understanding was *unformed*, *no settled conclusion stood at all*. The book was too good to be read by the unready, and being unread it was misread.

Verse 2.479-481.translation: (479) When the seer's work had been swept away by Baiji, Saubhava and Haryaksa, followers of dry reasoning, with the Sangraha as their cover — (480) the grammatical tradition which had fallen away from Patanjali's pupils, at that time stood established among the Southerners in the mere book. (481) Having obtained the tradition from the mountain, by those following the seeds of the Bhasya — by Candracarya and the rest — it was again brought to many branches.
Verse 2.479-481.commentary: The near-death and the recovery, and it is the most cited historical passage in Sanskrit grammar. (479) names three men — Baiji, Saubhava, Haryaksa — as suska-tarka-anusarinah, *followers of dry reasoning*: tarka without agama, exactly what 1.30 and 1.137 declared groundless; and they operated sangraha-pratikancuke, with the Sangraha *as their cover* — invoking the authority of the great work while destroying its teaching. Arse viplavite granthe: the *seer's work was swept away*. (480): the tradition bhrastah, *fallen* from Patanjali's pupils, survived kale daksinatyesu grantha-matre — among the *Southerners*, in the *mere book*: text without living transmission, the letter surviving where the flash had gone out (2.152's transmitted pratibha, broken). And (481) the rescue: parvatad agamam labdhva — having obtained the tradition *from the mountain* — bhasya-bijanusaribhih, by those *following the seeds of the Bhasya* (477's bija) — candracarya-adibhih punah, by *Candracarya and the rest*: sa nito bahu-sakhatvam, it was brought again to *many branches*. The whole doctrine of Kanda I is enacted here as history: tradition is not a text but a living inheritance (2.151's agama ripened by bhavana), it can be broken, and its recovery requires both the book and the mountain.
Verse 2.479-481.contested: 'from the mountain' (parvatat) is the great crux: a place-name, a mystical source, a teacher's name, or a lineage-designation have all been proposed, and nothing decides. Candracarya is plausibly the Buddhist grammarian Candragomin. The three named opponents are otherwise unknown

Verse 2.482-485.translation: (482) Having studied those paths of the courses of reasoning, and his own school, this compendium of the tradition was composed by our teacher. (483) Here, of some of the ways, the bare substance has been cited; in the third kanda the examination will be thorough. (484) One obtains discrimination of understanding through differing traditional views; how much can be raised up by one who runs after his own reasoning? (485) For those who imagine this and that without the ancient traditions, and who have not waited upon their elders, knowledge does not become very gracious.
Verse 2.482-485.commentary: The kanda closes on four verses that gather the whole work's method. (482): the compendium was composed by gurunasmakam — *our teacher* — having studied both the nyaya-prasthana-margan, the paths of reasoning, and svam darsanam, *his own school*: both, and in that order. (483) is a promise and a pointer: here vastu-matram udahrtam, the *bare substance* of some views has been cited; kande trtiye nyaksena bhavisyati vicarana — in the *third kanda* the examination will be *thorough*. The Padakanda is announced. Then (484-485) the closing counsel, and it is the work's own epistemology addressed to its reader. Prajna-vivekam labhate bhinnair agama-darsanaih — one obtains *discrimination of understanding* through *differing* traditional views: the doxography that has filled two kandas was not scholarly display but the method itself; discrimination comes from holding many inherited views, not from any one. Kiyad va sakyam unnetum sva-tarkam anudhavata — *how much can be raised up* by one who *runs after his own reasoning*? And the last word: vidya na atiprasidati — knowledge does *not become very gracious* — to those who imagine without the puranair agamaih, the ancient traditions, and who are anupasita-vrddhanam, *who have not waited upon their elders*. Prasidati: knowledge is not seized but *graced*, and it graces those who have sat with the old. After four hundred verses arguing that meaning is a flash in a ripened mind, the kanda ends by naming what does the ripening: not argument, but sitting with those who know.
Verse 2.482-485.contested: 'our teacher' (guruna asmakam) in 482 is disputed — whether Bhartrhari refers to a teacher of his own or, as some hold, to himself in the third person; the Vrtti-authorship question is entangled with it and remains open

Verse 2.127cd.translation: [Second half-lines of 2.126 and 2.127 respectively — see the commentary at 2.126ab and 2.127ab, which treat each verse entire.]
Verse 2.127cd.commentary: A note on the source rather than on the text. The wisdomlib edition paginates verses 2.126 and 2.127 as half-verses, and — evidently in error — assigns the identical label '2.127cd' to two different pages: one carrying the second half-line of 2.126 (asatyo vapi samsargah sabdarthah kaiscid isyate), the other the second half-line of 2.127 (sabdo vapy abhijalpatvam agato yati vacyatam). Because this edition keys its commentary to the source's own page labels, a single entry necessarily serves both pages. The substantive commentary for each verse is given complete at 'Verse 2.126ab' and 'Verse 2.127ab', where each karika is translated and discussed in full. Readers consulting the source directly should be aware that its labelling here cannot be relied upon.
